topics.title: Measurement of Tool Angles of Single Point Cutting Tool Using Tool Makers Microscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Tool Angles of Single Point Cutting Tool Using Tool Makers Microscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Tool Makers Microscope (TMM)** is a high-precision, non-contact optical metrological instrument used to measure the **linear dimensions, angular settings, and edge geometry** of small precision tools such as taps, drills, milling cutters, and **single-point cutting tools (SPCT)**. It combines the functions of an optical microscope and a precision two-axis (XY) micrometer workstage, allowing direct visual observation of the tool profile overlaid with a **cross-hair reticle** and a **goniometric (protractor) scale** for angular evaluation.

According to **KTU 2024 Scheme / ISO 3002 / ORS (Orthogonal Rake System) nomenclature**, a single-point cutting tool is characterized by **seven primary geometric parameters** measured in three mutually perpendicular reference planes:

| Symbol | Tool Angle | Plane of Measurement |
|--------|------------|----------------------|
| $\alpha_b$ | Back Rake Angle | Tool minor (transverse) plane |
| $\alpha_s$ | Side Rake Angle | Tool major (longitudinal) plane |
| $\gamma_e$ | End Clearance Angle | Tool minor (transverse) plane |
| $\gamma_s$ | Side Clearance Angle | Tool major (longitudinal) plane |
| $C_e$ | End Cutting Edge Angle | Tool minor (transverse) plane |
| $C_s$ | Side Cutting Edge Angle | Tool major (longitudinal) plane |
| $N$ | Nose (Lip) Angle | Tool major plane (face) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The TMM is the *only* instrument in the Metrology lab capable of directly measuring all **six angular tool parameters** without contact, ensuring the cutting edge is undamaged during inspection. Standard resolution: **angular = 1 arc-minute (1′)**, **linear = 0.001 mm (1 µm)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Magnifying Ruler with a Swiveling Compass"

Imagine trying to measure the *exact* angles of a sharpened pencil tip to determine how it will slice paper. A regular ruler can only measure length. A protractor can measure angle, but only if you can see the lines clearly. Now combine the two, place them on a **sliding glass table that moves 1/1000 mm at a time**, and look through a **magnifying glass with a built-in crosshair and a rotating dial** — *that* is a Tool Makers Microscope.

- The **sliding table (workstage)** = a "micro-ruler" that shifts the tool left/right and front/back under the lens.
- The **cross-hair reticle** = a fixed cross-line that acts as the **reference ruler** inside your view.
- The **goniometer (protractor) eyepiece** = a **swiveling compass** whose crosshair can be rotated, and the rotation angle is read off a digital/vernier scale.

For a single-point cutting tool, every "surface" and "edge" of the tool face becomes a visible line on the screen. By aligning the rotating crosshair with each line and reading the angle on the protractor dial, we directly obtain all the rake, clearance, and cutting-edge angles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** Aerospace and automotive CNC shops use TMM for **first-off inspection** of freshly ground carbide inserts. A 1° error in back rake can alter chip flow, increase cutting force by up to **15–20 %**, and drastically reduce tool life.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Specifications

- **Magnification range:** 10×, 20×, 30×, 50× (typically three-objective turret).
- **Workstage travel:** X = 50 mm, Y = 50 mm (or 75 mm × 50 mm).
- **Micrometer least count:** **0.001 mm (1 µm)**.
- **Protractor (goniometer) least count:** **1′ (one arc-minute) = 0.0167°**.
- **Light source:** 12 V / 50 W halogen lamp (vertical illuminator + sub-stage lamp).
- **Standard tool shank size accommodated:** Up to **200 mm length × 25 mm shank diameter** (varies by make).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tool Reference System (TRS) – Foundation of Every Angle

The **ASA (American Standards Association) / ISO 3002 tool reference system** defines three mutually perpendicular planes that act as the *coordinate frame* in which all six angles are measured:

1. **Reference Plane ($\pi_R$)** — the horizontal plane passing through the **axis of the tool shank** and perpendicular to the cutting velocity vector. Acts as the "ground" against which rake angles are tilted.
2. **Longitudinal / Tool Major Plane ($\pi_T$)** — vertical plane parallel to the tool axis and perpendicular to $\pi_R$. All *side* angles ($\alpha_s, \gamma_s, C_s$) are measured here.
3. **Transverse / Tool Minor Plane ($\pi_T'$)** — vertical plane perpendicular to both $\pi_R$ and $\pi_T$. All *back/end* angles ($\alpha_b, \gamma_e, C_e$) are measured here.
4. **Cutting Edge Plane ($\pi_S$)** — plane *tangent* to the cutting edge and perpendicular to $\pi_R$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three planes?** A 3D cutting tool cannot have its angles described in a single plane without ambiguity. By projecting each cutting-edge feature onto one of the three orthogonal planes, every angle becomes a unique, unambiguous 2-D angular quantity — the same logic used in engineering drawings.

## 2.2 The Six Angles – Logic Step-by-Step

### A. Rake Angles (define chip flow direction)
- **Back Rake Angle $\alpha_b$**: angle between the **tool face (rake face)** and $\pi_R$, measured in the **transverse plane** $\pi_T'$. Positive $\alpha_b$ tilts the face away from the cutting edge.
- **Side Rake Angle $\alpha_s$**: angle between the tool face and $\pi_R$, measured in the **longitudinal plane** $\pi_T$.

**Why it matters:** Rake angle controls **chip thickness, cutting force, and surface finish**. A positive rake reduces cutting power but weakens edge strength.

### B. Clearance Angles (prevent rubbing of flank against workpiece)
- **End Clearance Angle $\gamma_e$**: angle between the **end flank (primary relief)** and $\pi_R$, measured in $\pi_T'$.
- **Side Clearance Angle $\gamma_s$**: angle between the **side flank (secondary relief)** and $\pi_R$, measured in $\pi_T$.

**Why it matters:** If clearance $\to 0$, the flank rubs the workpiece → friction heat, vibration, poor finish. Clearance $\to$ too large → weak edge.

### C. Cutting Edge Angles (define cutting edge orientation)
- **End Cutting Edge Angle $C_e$**: angle between the **end cutting edge** and $\pi_R$, measured in $\pi_T'$.
- **Side Cutting Edge Angle $C_s$**: angle between the **side cutting edge** and $\pi_R$, measured in $\pi_T$.

**Why it matters:** $C_s$ controls **feed marks** on the finished surface; $C_e$ controls **strength of the nose**.

### D. Nose Angle (closes the geometric loop)
- **Nose Angle $N$**: angle between the **two principal cutting edges**, measured on the tool face.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **only** equations you need for the KTU 2024 lab viva and the 14-mark descriptive question.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Value (HSS on mild steel) |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | $N + C_s + C_e = 180°$ | Geometric closure of cutting edges on tool face | $90°$ |
| 2 | $\theta = \theta_{reading} - \theta_{reference}$ | True angle = protractor reading − reference offset | — |
| 3 | $\alpha_b + \alpha_s$ form the **true rake** $\alpha_t$ | $\tan \alpha_t = \sqrt{\tan^2 \alpha_b + \tan^2 \alpha_s}$ | combined |
| 4 | $h_{chip} = t_1 \sin \phi$ | Chip thickness relation (related concept) | — |
| 5 | $f = r(1 - \cos \phi)$ | Feed component from nose radius (related) | — |
| 6 | $LC_{linear} = 0.001 \text{ mm}$ | TMM micrometer least count | — |
| 7 | $LC_{angular} = 1' = \dfrac{1°}{60} \approx 0.0167°$ | TMM protractor least count | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** The relation $N + C_s + C_e = 180°$ holds only when measured on the **tool face (rake surface)**, *not* on the projection plane. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **First-article inspection** of brazed carbide tools in CNC turning cells.
- **Verification of resharpened tools** before returning to the production line.
- **Reverse engineering** of unknown tool geometries for tool-management databases.
- **Quality audits** under ISO 9001:2015 — metrology traceability for cutting tools.
- **Research labs** studying chip-flow visualization, where precise rake angles are mandatory inputs to FEM cutting simulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Closure Relation $N + C_s + C_e = 180°$

### Step 1 — Establish the geometry

Consider the **plan view** of the tool tip (looking down the tool axis). Three lines emanate from the nose point:
- **Line 1**: the *side cutting edge*, making angle $C_s$ with the reference plane.
- **Line 2**: the *end cutting edge*, making angle $C_e$ with the reference plane.
- **Line 3**: the line connecting the two edges (closing the angle), forming the **nose angle $N$**.

These three lines form a closed triangle on the tool face. The reference plane edge of this triangle subtends the sum $(C_s + C_e)$ with the closing edge.

### Step 2 — Apply the straight-line (180°) condition

Since the two cutting edges, together with the closing nose-edge line, lie in a single plane and form a closed polygon, the interior angles must sum to **360°**. Because each cutting edge angle ($C_s$ and $C_e$) and the nose angle $N$ are interior angles of the **same triangle on the rake face**, their sum equals **180°**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_s + C_e + N &= 180° \quad &\text{(geometric closure on tool face)} \\
N &= 180° - (C_s + C_e) \quad &\text{(solving for nose angle)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** If we measure $C_s$ and $C_e$ using the TMM, we can **cross-check** our measurement by verifying that the three measured angles sum to 180°. Any deviation $> 1°$ indicates an **alignment error** on the workstage.

### Step 3 — Derive the true rake angle from $\alpha_b$ and $\alpha_s$

The two rake components act in perpendicular planes. The **true (resultant) rake angle** $\alpha_t$ is the spatial resultant vector of $\alpha_b$ (transverse) and $\alpha_s$ (longitudinal).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \alpha_b &= \dfrac{h_{\perp}}{L_{\perp}} \quad &\text{(height / length in transverse plane)} \\
\tan \alpha_s &= \dfrac{h_{\parallel}}{L_{\parallel}} \quad &\text{(height / length in longitudinal plane)} \\
\tan \alpha_t &= \sqrt{\tan^2 \alpha_b + \tan^2 \alpha_s} \quad &\text{(Pythagorean combination)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **3-D Pythagorean theorem** for orthogonal vectors.

### Step 4 — Derive the protractor angular measurement equation

Let the cross-hair of the goniometer eyepiece be aligned with the **reference plane line** of the tool, giving a reading $\theta_{ref}$. Then the cross-hair is rotated to align with the **cutting edge line**, giving a reading $\theta_{edge}$. The **true angle** between the two features is:

$$
\theta_{true} = \theta_{edge} - \theta_{ref}
$$

where each reading has a precision of $\pm 1' = \pm \dfrac{1°}{60}$.

### Step 3.5 — Numerical Example (typical KTU lab specimen)

Suppose the TMM readings obtained are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_s &= 75° \; 12' \quad &\text{(side cutting edge angle)} \\
C_e &= 14° \; 48' \quad &\text{(end cutting edge angle)} \\
\alpha_s &= 14° \; 00' \quad &\text{(side rake angle)} \\
\alpha_b &= 8° \; 00' \quad &\text{(back rake angle)} \\
\gamma_s &= 8° \; 30' \quad &\text{(side clearance)} \\
\gamma_e &= 6° \; 30' \quad &\text{(end clearance)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using closure relation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{calculated} &= 180° - (C_s + C_e) \\
&= 180° - (75°12' + 14°48') \\
&= 180° - 90°00' \\
&= 90°00' \quad &\text{(matches standard tool)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of true rake:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \alpha_t &= \sqrt{\tan^2 8° + \tan^2 14°} \\
&= \sqrt{(0.1405)^2 + (0.2493)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.01975 + 0.06215} \\
&= \sqrt{0.08190} \\
&= 0.2862 \\
\alpha_t &= \arctan(0.2862) = 15.96° \approx 16° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Reading Strategy Summary

| Step | Action | TMM Control Used | Reading Obtained |
|------|--------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Mount tool shank on workstage between centers | V-block / center holder | — |
| 2 | Illuminate tool face (vertical illuminator ON) | Lamp switch | — |
| 3 | Focus microscope on tool nose | Focus knob | Sharp image |
| 4 | Align cross-hair parallel to reference plane (shank axis) | Rotate goniometer head | $\theta_{ref}$ |
| 5 | Rotate cross-hair to align with side cutting edge | Rotate goniometer head | $\theta_{C_s}$ |
| 6 | Compute $C_s = \theta_{C_s} - \theta_{ref}$ | — | $C_s$ |
| 7 | Repeat for end cutting edge, face, flanks | — | $C_e, \alpha_s, \alpha_b, \gamma_s, \gamma_e$ |
| 8 | Verify $C_s + C_e + N = 180°$ | — | Self-check |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tool Makers Microscope

> *Note:* Since the TMM is a physical optical instrument, we use a **block-level functional flow** showing how light, mechanics, optics, and the user interact to produce a measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["LAMP 12V 50W HALOGEN"] --> A1["VERTICAL ILLUMINATOR"]
    A1 --> A2["TOOL FACE WORKPIECE"]
    A2 --> A3["OBJECTIVE LENS 10X 20X 30X 50X"]
    A3 --> A4["BEAM SPLITTER AND PRISM"]
    A4 --> A5["GONIOMETER EYEPIECE WITH PROTACTOR SCALE"]
    A4 --> A6["CROSSHAIR RETICLE EYEPIECE"]
    A5 --> A7["OPERATOR OBSERVES ANGULAR READING"]
    A6 --> A8["OPERATOR OBSERVES LINEAR POSITION"]
    A8 --> A9["XY MICROMETER WORKSTAGE WITH 1UM LC"]
    A9 --> A10["TOOL SHANK HOLDER BETWEEN CENTERS"]
    B1["HEAVY CAST IRON BASE"] --> A9
    B2["FINE FOCUS KNOB"] --> A3
    B3["COARSE FOCUS KNOB"] --> A3
    C1["ROTATION OF GONIOMETER HEAD"] --> A5
    C1 --> A7
    D1["DIGITAL LINEAR READOUT"] --> A9
    D1 --> A8
    E1["RESULT SIX TOOL ANGLES RECORDED"] --> A7
    E1 --> A8
    E1 --> F1["VERIFICATION USING CLOSURE RELATION 180 DEGREE"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of the Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["START MOUNT TOOL ON WORKSTAGE"] --> P2["SWITCH ON VERTICAL ILLUMINATOR"]
    P2 --> P3["ADJUST FOCUS TO GET SHARP TOOL NOSE IMAGE"]
    P3 --> P4["ROTATE GONIOMETER TO ALIGN CROSSHAIR WITH SHANK REFERENCE"]
    P4 --> P5["RECORD REFERENCE READING THETA REF"]
    P5 --> P6["ROTATE CROSSHAIR TO SIDE CUTTING EDGE"]
    P6 --> P7["RECORD THETA CS COMPUTE CS"]
    P7 --> P8["ROTATE CROSSHAIR TO END CUTTING EDGE"]
    P8 --> P9["RECORD THETA CE COMPUTE CE"]
    P9 --> P10["ROTATE CROSSHAIR TO RAKE FACE"]
    P10 --> P11["RECORD ALPHA S AND ALPHA B"]
    P11 --> P12["ROTATE CROSSHAIR TO SIDE FLANK"]
    P12 --> P13["RECORD GAMMA S"]
    P13 --> P14["ROTATE CROSSHAIR TO END FLANK"]
    P14 --> P15["RECORD GAMMA E"]
    P15 --> P16["VERIFY CS PLUS CE PLUS N EQUALS 180 DEGREE"]
    P16 --> P17["RECORD SIX ANGLES IN OBSERVATION TABLE"]
    P17 --> P18["END EXPERIMENT COMPLETE"]
```

## 4.3 Reference Plane Orientation Diagram (Textual Schematic)

```
            SIDE CUTTING EDGE
                   \
                    \  Cs (Side Cutting Edge Angle)
                     \____________________
                                       /|
                                      / |  Rake Face (alpha_s, alpha_b region)
                                     /  |
                                    /   |  NOSE (r)
                                   /    |
              End Cutting Edge  /     |
                       Ce      /      
                          \   /       |
                           \ /        |  END FLANK (gamma_e)
                            \         |
                             \________|
                              REFERENCE PLANE (pi_R)  →  axis of tool shank
```

- The **shank axis (horizontal)** = reference plane $\pi_R$.
- The **side cutting edge** is inclined above $\pi_R$ by angle $C_s$ in the longitudinal plane.
- The **end cutting edge** is inclined above $\pi_R$ by angle $C_e$ in the transverse plane.
- The **rake face** is the upper inclined surface from which both $\alpha_s$ and $\alpha_b$ are measured.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Tool Makers Microscope. List any four tool angles that can be measured using it.

**Model Answer:**
The **Tool Makers Microscope (TMM)** is an optical measuring instrument used for the non-contact measurement of linear and angular dimensions of small precision tools, employing an illuminated stage, a microscope with cross-hair and protractor eyepiece, and an XY micrometer workstage.

**Four tool angles measurable:**
1. Side Rake Angle ($\alpha_s$)
2. Back Rake Angle ($\alpha_b$)
3. Side Clearance Angle ($\gamma_s$)
4. End Cutting Edge Angle ($C_e$)

*(Other acceptable answers: End Clearance Angle $\gamma_e$, Side Cutting Edge Angle $C_s$, Nose Angle $N$.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] State the geometric closure relation among the cutting edge angles of a single-point cutting tool. Why is it important during TMM measurement?

**Model Answer:**
$$
C_s + C_e + N = 180°
$$

**Importance:** It serves as a **self-verification check**. After measuring $C_s$ and $C_e$ on the TMM, the computed $N$ must match the measured $N$. Any deviation indicates **workstage misalignment, faulty reading, or tool tip damage**, preventing faulty tools from entering production.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023] (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Tool Reference System (TRS)** of a single-point cutting tool as per ISO 3002. Define all six tool angles in their respective reference planes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure** for measuring the tool angles of a single-point cutting tool using a Tool Makers Microscope. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Tool Reference System and Six Angles (7 marks):**

The **ASA / ISO 3002** Tool Reference System uses **three mutually perpendicular planes** (see sketch in Section 4.3):

| Plane | Symbol | Description |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| Reference Plane | $\pi_R$ | Passes through tool axis, perpendicular to cutting velocity |
| Longitudinal / Tool Major Plane | $\pi_T$ | Parallel to tool axis, perpendicular to $\pi_R$ |
| Transverse / Tool Minor Plane | $\pi_T'$ | Perpendicular to both $\pi_R$ and $\pi_T$ |

**Six Angles:**

1. **Back Rake Angle ($\alpha_b$):** Angle between tool face and $\pi_R$ measured in $\pi_T'$ — controls chip curl on the back side.
2. **Side Rake Angle ($\alpha_s$):** Angle between tool face and $\pi_R$ measured in $\pi_T$ — controls chip flow laterally.
3. **End Clearance Angle ($\gamma_e$):** Angle between end flank and $\pi_R$ measured in $\pi_T'$ — prevents rubbing of end flank.
4. **Side Clearance Angle ($\gamma_s$):** Angle between side flank and $\pi_R$ measured in $\pi_T$ — prevents rubbing of side flank.
5. **End Cutting Edge Angle ($C_e$):** Angle between end cutting edge and $\pi_R$ measured in $\pi_T'$.
6. **Side Cutting Edge Angle ($C_s$):** Angle between side cutting edge and $\pi_R$ measured in $\pi_T$.

The **nose angle $N$** lies on the rake face and is related by $C_s + C_e + N = 180°$.

**(b) Measurement Procedure on TMM (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mounting:** Mount the single-point cutting tool on the TMM workstage between centers or in a V-block holder. Ensure the tool axis is parallel to the X-axis of the workstage micrometer.

**Step 2 — Illumination:** Switch ON the **vertical illuminator** so that light strikes the rake face of the tool. The tool face must reflect light back into the objective.

**Step 3 — Focusing:** Use the coarse and fine focus knobs to bring the **tool nose point** into sharp focus under the chosen objective (typically 20× or 30×).

**Step 4 — Reference Alignment:** Rotate the **goniometer head** until the cross-hair of the protractor eyepiece is **parallel to the tool shank** (i.e., parallel to $\pi_R$). Record this reference reading $\theta_{ref}$.

**Step 5 — Angular Measurements:** Rotate the cross-hair successively to align with:
- the **side cutting edge** → record $C_s$
- the **end cutting edge** → record $C_e$
- the **rake face line** in side view → record $\alpha_s$
- the **rake face line** in back view → record $\alpha_b$
- the **side flank line** → record $\gamma_s$
- the **end flank line** → record $\gamma_e$

Each true angle = $\theta_{edge} - \theta_{ref}$, read to a least count of **1′**.

**Step 6 — Verification:** Check $C_s + C_e + N = 180°$. If valid, tabulate results; otherwise, re-align.

**Step 7 — Recording:** Tabulate all six angles with units (degrees + minutes) and compare with standard tool geometry chart.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] (a)** Describe the **constructional features** of a Tool Makers Microscope with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of its **goniometer eyepiece**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During TMM measurement of a single-point cutting tool, the following readings were obtained:

| Angle | Measured Value |
|-------|----------------|
| $C_s$ | $75°\;12'$ |
| $C_e$ | $14°\;48'$ |
| $\alpha_s$ | $14°\;00'$ |
| $\alpha_b$ | $8°\;00'$ |
| $\gamma_s$ | $8°\;30'$ |
| $\gamma_e$ | $6°\;30'$ |

**(i)** Verify the geometric closure relation. **(ii)** Calculate the **true rake angle** $\alpha_t$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Constructional Features and Goniometer Eyepiece (7 marks):**

A **Tool Makers Microscope** consists of:

1. **Heavy Cast-Iron Base** — provides vibration-free support.
2. **XY Micrometer Workstage** — travels ±25 mm in X and Y with **0.001 mm least count**; carries the tool shank.
3. **Tool Holder (V-block / Centers)** — clamps the tool rigidly with the cutting edge facing the objective.
4. **Vertical Illuminator** — 12 V halogen lamp projects light down onto the tool face for reflection observation.
5. **Sub-stage Lamp** — for transmitted-light observation (used for thread measurements, etc.).
6. **Three-Objective Turret** — 10×, 20×, 30× (or 50×) magnification nosepiece.
7. **Coarse & Fine Focus Knobs** — rack-and-pinion + precision leadscrew focusing.
8. **Goniometer (Protractor) Eyepiece** — has a **rotating cross-hair** and a **vernier protractor scale** reading to **1 arc-minute**. By rotating the cross-hair and reading the protractor, angular measurements between two tool features are obtained directly.
9. **Cross-hair Reticle Eyepiece** — fixed cross-lines for linear measurements with the XY micrometer.

**Function of Goniometer Eyepiece:** It is a **rotating cross-hair eyepiece coupled to a circular protractor scale (vernier)**. The operator aligns the rotating cross-hair with successive tool features (cutting edges, faces, flanks) and reads the angular displacement between two features directly on the protractor dial. This eliminates the need for trigonometric calculation and enables direct angular measurement to ±1′.

**(b) Numerical Verification and True Rake (7 marks):**

**(i) Geometric Closure Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_s + C_e &= 75°12' + 14°48' \\
&= 89°60' = 90°00' \\
N_{calculated} &= 180° - 90°00' \\
&= 90°00' \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Measured $N$ (from tool face) $= 90°$. The closure relation $C_s + C_e + N = 180°$ is **satisfied → reading is valid**.

**(ii) True Rake Angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \alpha_t &= \sqrt{\tan^2 \alpha_b + \tan^2 \alpha_s} \\
&= \sqrt{\tan^2 8° + \tan^2 14°} \\
&= \sqrt{(0.1405)^2 + (0.2493)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.01975 + 0.06215} \\
&= \sqrt{0.08190} \\
&= 0.2862 \\
\alpha_t &= \arctan(0.2862) \\
&= 15.96° \approx 16° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\alpha_t \approx \mathbf{16°}$ — the effective rake angle the chip "feels" during cutting.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in the TMM Experiment:**
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha_s$ with $C_s$** — students often interchange side-rake and side-cutting-edge angles. Remember: $\alpha$ = rake (face); $C$ = cutting edge; $\gamma$ = clearance (flank).
> 2. **Forgetting units** — readings are in **degrees and minutes**, not decimal degrees. A 7-mark answer without proper units loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the closure verification** — examiners award 1–2 marks for the $C_s + C_e + N = 180°$ check. Omitting it = guaranteed deduction.
> 4. **Not drawing the reference plane** in the diagram — without the three reference planes labelled ($\pi_R, \pi_T, \pi_T'$), the 7-mark sketch question caps at 3 marks.
> 5. **Reporting $\tan^{-1}$ values without converting to degrees** — students often leave $\alpha_t = 0.286$ instead of $15.96°$, losing 1 mark.
> 6. **Mounting the tool with the face away from the objective** — a practical mistake; the rake face must face the lens for vertical-illumination mode.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Tool Makers Microscope = optical microscope + XY micrometer workstage + goniometer eyepiece.**
- ✅ **Least Count:** Linear = **0.001 mm (1 µm)**; Angular = **1 arc-minute (1′)**.
- ✅ **Three reference planes** (TRS): $\pi_R$ (reference), $\pi_T$ (longitudinal/major), $\pi_T'$ (transverse/minor).
- ✅ **Six tool angles** measured: $\alpha_b, \alpha_s, \gamma_e, \gamma_s, C_e, C_s$ + derived $N$.
- ✅ **Geometric closure:** $C_s + C_e + N = 180°$ — used as self-verification.
- ✅ **True rake formula:** $\tan \alpha_t = \sqrt{\tan^2 \alpha_b + \tan^2 \alpha_s}$.
- ✅ **Two illumination modes:** Vertical (face) and Sub-stage (transmitted).
- ✅ **Procedure order:** Mount → Illuminate → Focus → Reference align → Rotate & read → Verify → Record.
- ✅ **Standard HSS tool on mild steel:** $\alpha_b = 8°, \alpha_s = 14°, \gamma_e = 6°, \gamma_s = 8°, C_e = 15°, C_s = 75°, N = 90°$.
- ✅ **Examination marks split (typical 14-mark question):** Sketch = 3 marks, Definitions = 4 marks, Procedure = 4 marks, Numerical/Verification = 3 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Thread Parameters Using Profile Projector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Thread Parameters Using Profile Projector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Profile Projector** (also known as an optical comparator or shadowgraph) is a non-contact optical metrology instrument used to measure the magnified two-dimensional silhouette of a small mechanical component against a reference graticule or screen. In the context of **PCNSL408 – Measurements Lab**, the profile projector is employed to determine the geometric parameters of an external screw thread, namely the **major diameter (d/D)**, **minor diameter (d₁/D₁)**, **effective/pitch diameter (d₂/D₂)**, **pitch (P)**, **lead (L)**, and **thread included angle (α)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment validates **Course Outcome CO2** – *Apply appropriate measurement techniques for linear and angular dimensions using precision instruments.* Thread measurement on a profile projector falls under the **screw thread metrology** module using optical comparators.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you place a **tiny screw** under a magnifying glass, but instead of a small circular lens you look through, the screw's shadow is cast onto a **large white screen** on the wall, glowing 10, 20 or even 100 times bigger than its real size. Now imagine a **transparent ruler** (called a *graticule*) stuck onto that screen, so you can read the screw's crest-to-crest width, the gap between peaks, and the angle of its flanks just like reading marks on a measuring tape.

In one sentence: **The profile projector turns a microscopic thread into a giant, glowing silhouette on a screen, so you can measure it without ever touching the screw.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standards

- **Wavelength of light used:** typically **546.07 nm (green mercury line)** or **white light (≈ 550 nm)** for general illumination.
- **Standard metric thread included angle (α):** **60°** (ISO 261 / IS 4218).
- **Standard BSW/BSP thread included angle:** **55°** (BSW – British Standard Whitworth).
- **Standard ACME thread included angle:** **29°** (full angle; half-angle = **14.5°**).
- **Standard magnifications available:** **10×, 20×, 50×, 100×** (most common on a 300 mm screen).
- **Screen diameter:** typically **300 mm or 600 mm** (KTU laboratory models usually have a **300 mm** screen).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Term to Memorize:** The phrase *"effective diameter"* (also called *pitch diameter*) is the cylinder on which a perfect nut and bolt would mate with zero clearance — it is the single most critical thread parameter.

### 1.4 Why Use a Profile Projector for Threads?

1. **Non-contact:** No deformation of soft threads during measurement.
2. **High magnification:** Reveals minor surface defects, burrs, flank straightness.
3. **Rapid:** All thread parameters are read from one projected image in seconds.
4. **Traceable:** Calibrated against a glass graticule — directly traceable to national length standards.
5. **Versatile:** Works for external threads, internal threads, gears, press tools, press-pins, and stampings.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Optical Working Principle (Structured Logic)

The profile projector operates on the principle of **shadow projection (silhouette projection)** using the classical optics chain shown below:

1. **Light Source (Illuminator):** A high-intensity halogen or LED lamp (sometimes a mercury-vapour lamp for monochromatic green light). The light provides a uniform, collimated beam.
2. **Condenser Lens Group:** Collects diverging light from the source and converts it into a **collimated parallel beam** of uniform intensity.
3. **Profile Stage (Work Table):** The threaded specimen is mounted on a glass plate (transparent stage) between the condenser and the objective. The thread's silhouette blocks part of the parallel beam.
4. **Objective Lens:** Magnifies the silhouette and projects it onto the viewing screen. It has a fixed magnification (10×, 20×, 50× or 100×).
5. **Projection Screen with Graticule:** A frosted-glass screen with **engraved cross-lines, circular scales, and linear scales** calibrated for the chosen magnification. A **protractor graticule** has 0°–360° markings for angular measurement.
6. **Focussing Knob:** Moves the objective (or the stage) to bring the magnified image into sharp focus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Behind Projection:** Because the light source is **collimated**, the silhouette formed is geometrically similar to the thread outline and is scaled exactly by the objective magnification — there is no perspective distortion.

### 2.2 Procedure to Measure Thread Parameters (Operational Logic)

The following bulleted workflow is what students must follow on the lab floor and reproduce in the KTU record:

- **Step 1 — Mounting:** Clamp the threaded specimen vertically (axis perpendicular to the optical axis) on the **glass stage** such that the thread axis lies **parallel to the X-axis graticule line**.
- **Step 2 — Focusing:** Rotate the focusing knob until a sharp, magnified profile of the thread appears on the screen.
- **Step 3 — Centring:** Translate the X-Y table until the thread profile is centrally aligned with the cross-hair origin.
- **Step 4 — Major Diameter (d):** Move the cross-line to touch the **crest** on one side, read the graticule. Move the cross-line to touch the **crest** on the opposite side, read again. The difference, **divided by the magnification**, gives the actual major diameter.
- **Step 5 — Minor Diameter (d₁):** Repeat Step 4 with the cross-line touching the **root** on both sides.
- **Step 6 — Pitch (P):** Position the cross-line at the crest of one tooth, note the reading. Move the table by one full tooth pitch (use fine feed) to the next crest, note the reading. The difference, divided by magnification, gives the pitch.
- **Step 7 — Effective Diameter (d₂):** Use one of two KTU-accepted methods:
  - **(a) Direct overlay with a thread profile template** placed on the screen — match the projected flanks to the template lines, read off d₂ directly.
  - **(b) Two-wire method on the projector stage** — place calibrated **wires of best size (B = P / (2 cos(α/2)))** in the thread grooves; the projected profile now shows the effective-diameter cylinder as the wire crests.
- **Step 8 — Thread Angle (α):** Align the protractor graticule datum line with one flank; read the angle to the other flank. For **metric threads, α = 60°** is the acceptance check.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit | Notes / KTU Tip |
|---|----------|--------|----------------------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | Linear magnification | M | $M = \dfrac{\text{Image size on screen}}{\text{Actual size of feature}}$ | dimensionless | Selected by objective lens (10×, 20×, 50×, 100×) |
| 2 | Actual dimension | X | $X = \dfrac{\text{Screen reading (mm)}}{M}$ | mm | Always divide screen reading by magnification |
| 3 | Pitch of thread | P | $P = \dfrac{L}{n}$, where L = axial length, n = number of pitches in L | mm | For a single-start thread, L = lead |
| 4 | Lead | L | $L = n_{\text{starts}} \times P$ | mm | $n_{\text{starts}}$ = number of thread starts |
| 5 | Major diameter (metric) | d | $d = D - 0.6495\,P$ (basic reference) | mm | For *tolerance checks*; measured value must lie in tolerance band |
| 6 | Effective diameter (metric) | d₂ | $d_2 = d - 0.6495\,P = D - P$ | mm | Most critical for fit/assembly |
| 7 | Minor diameter (metric) | d₁ | $d_1 = d - 1.0825\,P$ | mm | — |
| 8 | Thread included angle | α | $\alpha = 2 \times (\text{half-angle from protractor})$ | degrees | **60°** metric, **55°** BSW, **29°** ACME |
| 9 | Best-size wire diameter | B | $B = \dfrac{P}{2\,\cos(\alpha/2)}$ | mm | Used in 2-wire/3-wire method |
| 10 | Measurement over wires (effective dia) | M_w | $M_w = d_2 + B\,\Bigl(1 + \csc(\alpha/2)\Bigr) - \dfrac{P}{2}\,\cot(\alpha/2)$ | mm | Reduced form for α = 60°: $M_w = d_2 + 3B$ |
| 11 | Effective diameter from wire method | d₂ | $d_2 = M_w - B\,\Bigl(1 + \csc(\alpha/2)\Bigr) + \dfrac{P}{2}\,\cot(\alpha/2)$ | mm | — |
| 12 | Best-wire simplified (60°) | B | $B = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774\,P$ | mm | Substitute $\cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** For a 60° metric thread, the **simplified three-wire formula** $d_2 = M_w - 3B$ (or equivalently, $d_2 = M_w - 1.73205\,P$) is the **expected textbook answer**. Always quote this unless the question specifies a different thread form.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control in Bolt Manufacturing:** Every mass-produced M8, M10, M12 bolt on a shop floor is sampled on a profile projector to verify that the thread conforms to IS 4218 (ISO 261).
- **Acceptance of Reground Lead-Screws:** CNC machine tool manufacturers re-profile the lead-screw after refurbishment; a profile projector checks flank straightness and pitch error before reassembly.
- **Gear-Hob and Cutting-Tool Inspection:** Hobs, taps, and chasers are checked on a profile projector to verify the tooth-profile geometry before being released to production.
- **Watch and Instrument Threads:** Tiny M0.5–M2 watch threads cannot be measured by mechanical means — only optical projection works.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Major, Effective, and Minor Diameters of a 60° Metric Thread

**Starting geometry:** Consider a single-V thread with flank half-angle $\beta = \alpha/2$. On a flat development of the thread profile, the radial drop from crest to pitch line equals $H_1$, and from pitch line to root equals $H$.

For a **60° metric (ISO) thread** the basic profile is defined by IS 4218 / ISO 261:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth of engagement (full thread)} &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,P \\[4pt]
\text{Truncation at crest (per side)} &= \frac{H}{8} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{16}\,P \\[4pt]
\text{Truncation at root (per side)} &= \frac{H}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{8}\,P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Drop from crest to pitch line:**

$$
h_1 = \frac{3}{8}\,H = \frac{3}{8} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,P = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{16}\,P = 0.3248\,P
$$

**Step 2 — Drop from pitch line to root:**

$$
h_2 = \frac{5}{8}\,H = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{16}\,P = 0.5413\,P
$$

**Step 3 — Convert radial drops to diametral drops (× 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d - d_2 &= 2\,h_1 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{8}\,P = 0.6495\,P \\[4pt]
d_2 - d_1 &= 2\,h_2 = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{8}\,P = 1.0825\,P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final closed-form relations (KTU cheat-sheet equations):**

$$
\boxed{\,d_2 = d - 0.6495\,P\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_1 = d - 1.0825\,P\,}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The factor $0.6495$ arises from $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.64952$ and is the **single most-quoted constant in KTU thread-metrology problems**. Memorize it.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Best-Size Wire Diameter for a 60° Thread

A wire of diameter $B$ rests in the thread groove such that it touches the two flanks at points where the flank makes half-angle $\beta$ with the thread axis. From the geometry of the inscribed circle inside an inverted-V groove:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At the tangent point, the wire centre lies at height } y &= \frac{B}{2\,\sin\beta} \text{ above the flank root.} \\[4pt]
\text{Projecting horizontally from the wire-centre to the thread axis, the radial offset is } r &= \frac{B}{2\,\cos\beta}.
\end{aligned}
$$

For the wire to be **best size** (touches the flanks exactly at the pitch line), we require $r = P/4$ (i.e., the wire centre projects horizontally onto the pitch line).

$$
\frac{B}{2\,\cos\beta} = \frac{P}{4}
$$

Solving for $B$:

$$
\boxed{\,B = \frac{P}{2\,\cos\beta}\,}
$$

For metric threads, $\beta = 30°$:

$$
B = \frac{P}{2\,\cos 30°} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774\,P
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Measurement Over Wires → Effective Diameter (Reduced Form for α = 60°)

The measurement $M_w$ over two parallel wires placed diametrically in opposite thread grooves is:

$$
M_w = d_2 + B + 2 \times \frac{B}{2}\,\csc\beta - \frac{P}{2}\,\cot\beta
$$

Simplify the middle term:

$$
B + B\,\csc\beta = B\bigl(1 + \csc\beta\bigr)
$$

Hence:

$$
M_w = d_2 + B\Bigl(1 + \csc\beta\Bigr) - \frac{P}{2}\,\cot\beta
$$

Solving for $d_2$:

$$
\boxed{\,d_2 = M_w - B\Bigl(1 + \csc\beta\Bigr) + \frac{P}{2}\,\cot\beta\,}
$$

**Reduced form for α = 60° (β = 30°):** $\csc 30° = 2$, $\cot 30° = \sqrt{3}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_2 &= M_w - B(1+2) + \frac{P\sqrt{3}}{2} \\[2pt]
    &= M_w - 3B + 0.8660\,P
\end{aligned}
$$

But because the wire is **best-size**, $B = P/\sqrt{3}$, so $3B = \sqrt{3}\,P \approx 1.7321\,P$, which exactly cancels the $0.8660\,P$ term only when the **pitch is nominal**. For an *unknown pitch* measured on the projector, the **KTU-friendly reduced formula** is:

$$
\boxed{\,d_2 = M_w - 3B\,} \quad \text{(valid only when best-size wire is used)}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Thread Forms (KTU Reference Table)

| Thread Standard | Included Angle α | Half-angle β | $d_2$ from d, P | Best-wire B | Reduced $d_2$ from $M_w$ |
|------------------|------------------|--------------|------------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **ISO Metric (M)** | 60° | 30° | $d - 0.6495\,P$ | $P / \sqrt{3}$ | $M_w - 3B$ |
| **BSW / BSF** | 55° | 27.5° | $d - 0.6403\,P$ | $0.5637\,P$ | $M_w - 3.9234\,B + 0.9605\,P$ |
| **ACME** | 29° | 14.5° | $d - P/2$ | $0.516\,P$ | $M_w - 4.99\,B + 0.96\,P$ |
| **BA (British Assn.)** | 47.5° | 23.75° | tabulated | tabulated | tabulated |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A metric thread M20 × 2.5 is measured on a profile projector at 20× magnification. The screen readings are: crest-to-crest = 412 mm, root-to-root = 358 mm, two successive crests = 50 mm apart. Find d, d₁, and P. Verify against theoretical values.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnification chosen: } M &= 20 \\[4pt]
\text{Major diameter: } d &= \frac{412}{20} = 20.60 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Minor diameter: } d_1 &= \frac{358}{20} = 17.90 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Pitch (1 tooth): } P &= \frac{50}{20} = 2.50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Theoretical check (for M20 × 2.5, P = 2.5 mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_2 &= d - 0.6495\,P = 20.60 - 0.6495 \times 2.50 = 20.60 - 1.624 = 18.976 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
d_1^{\text{theory}} &= d - 1.0825\,P = 20.60 - 1.0825 \times 2.50 = 20.60 - 2.706 = 17.894 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Measured $d_1 = 17.90$ mm vs theoretical $17.894$ mm — error ≈ 0.006 mm, well within IT6 tolerance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Optical Layout Block Diagram — Profile Projector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Light Source Halogen Lamp"] --> lensB["Condenser Lens Collimator"]
    lensB --> stageC["Glass Work Stage Thread Specimen Mounted Here"]
    stageC --> objD["Objective Lens Magnification 10x 20x 50x 100x"]
    objD --> mirrorE["Deflection Mirror Folds Optical Path"]
    mirrorE --> screenF["Frosted Screen 300 mm Engraved Graticule"]
    screenF --> obsG["Operator Eyepiece for Fine Reading"]
    classDef optic fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class srcA,lensB,objD,mirrorE,optic optic
    class stageC,screenF,obsG mech
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- Light travels **left → right** from the lamp, gets **collimated**, passes around the **thread specimen**, is **magnified by the objective**, **bent 90° by a mirror** (to fit the cabinet), and finally forms a sharp image on the **frosted screen** that the operator views.
- The graticule is bonded to the screen; therefore the **screen reading is already in real projected-millimetres**, not actual-millimetres.

### 4.2 Measurement Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Mount Thread on Glass Stage Axis Vertical Axis Parallel to X Graticule"] --> B["Step 2 Rotate Focus Knob Until Sharp Profile Appears"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Centre the Image Cross Hair on Tooth Crest"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Measure d Major Diameter Crest to Crest"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Measure d1 Minor Diameter Root to Root"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Measure P Pitch Crest to Next Crest"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Measure d2 Effective Diameter Wire Method or Template Overlay"]
    G --> H["Step 8 Measure Alpha Flank Angle Using Protractor Graticule"]
    H --> I["Step 9 Record All Values Divide Screen Readings by Magnification M"]
    I --> J["Step 10 Verify Against Theoretical Formula Tolerance Check"]
    classDef step fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J step
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Where Each Thread Parameter is Read on the Screen

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Screen Layout for Thread Measurement"] --> B["Top Horizontal Line Tangent to Left Crest"]
    A --> C["Bottom Horizontal Line Tangent to Right Crest"]
    A --> D["Middle Horizontal Line Pitch Line Effective Diameter"]
    A --> E["Two Diagonal Lines Flank Angles Measured with Protractor"]
    A --> F["Two Short Vertical Lines Crest to Root Depth h2"]
    B -. "Reading A minus Reading C divided by M gives d" .-> C
    D -. "Reading at thread flanks gives d2" .-> D
    E -. "Angle between lines gives alpha 60 degree for metric" .-> E
    F -. "Vertical separation gives d minus d1" .-> F
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**  
*Define a profile projector and list any **four** thread parameters that can be measured with it.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~80 words):**

A profile projector is an optical comparator that projects a magnified silhouette of a workpiece onto a calibrated screen for non-contact dimensional measurement. Thread parameters measurable include:  
(i) **Major diameter (d)**, (ii) **Minor diameter (d₁)**, (iii) **Effective diameter (d₂)**, (iv) **Pitch (P)**, (v) **Thread included angle (α)**. *(Any four for 1 mark each; definition 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**  
*State the **best-size wire formula** for a 60° metric thread and explain what "best size" means.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

The best-size wire diameter for a metric thread ($\alpha = 60°$) is:

$$
B = \frac{P}{2\,\cos 30°} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774\,P
$$

**"Best size"** means that the wire, when placed in the thread groove, touches the two flanks of the thread **exactly at the pitch line** (effective-diameter cylinder). Because of this geometric property, the simple reduced formula $d_2 = M_w - 3B$ is valid. *(Formula: 2 marks, explanation: 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, End-Semester]**  
**(a)** Describe, with the aid of a labelled block diagram, the **optical system of a profile projector**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An **M30 × 3.5** screw thread is checked on a profile projector fitted with a **20× objective lens**. The screen readings are:  
– Crest-to-crest (major diameter) = **624 mm**  
– Root-to-root (minor diameter) = **540 mm**  
– Distance between two successive crests = **70 mm**  

Calculate the **major diameter (d)**, **minor diameter (d₁)**, and the **pitch (P)**. Verify the measured values against the **theoretical effective diameter** using the standard metric-thread relation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution A(a) — Optical System of a Profile Projector (7 marks)**

1. **Light source** (1 mark): A high-intensity halogen/mercury lamp providing a collimated beam.
2. **Condenser lens** (1 mark): Converts the diverging lamp light into a parallel, uniformly illuminated beam.
3. **Work stage** (1 mark): A glass plate on an X-Y table that holds the threaded specimen; the thread's silhouette blocks part of the beam.
4. **Objective lens** (1 mark): Magnifies the silhouette (10×, 20×, 50×, or 100×).
5. **Deflection mirror + projection screen** (1 mark): Folds the optical path and projects the magnified image onto a 300 mm frosted screen.
6. **Graticule** (1 mark): An engraved reticle with cross-lines, linear scales and a protractor; bonded to the screen, gives readings in projected millimetres.
7. **Working principle** (1 mark): Because the beam is collimated, the projected silhouette is geometrically similar to the actual thread profile, scaled only by the objective magnification. *Actual size = Screen reading ÷ Magnification.*

---

**Solution A(b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $M = 20$; nominal $M30 \times 3.5$ ⇒ $P = 3.5$ mm.

**Step 1 — Major diameter** (2 marks):

$$
d = \frac{624 \text{ mm}}{20} = 31.20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Minor diameter** (2 marks):

$$
d_1 = \frac{540 \text{ mm}}{20} = 27.00 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Pitch** (1 mark):

$$
P = \frac{70 \text{ mm}}{20} = 3.50 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical effective diameter** (2 marks):

$$
d_2 = d - 0.6495\,P = 31.20 - 0.6495 \times 3.50 = 31.20 - 2.273 = 28.927 \text{ mm}
$$

**Verification:** Theoretical $d_1 = d - 1.0825\,P = 31.20 - 3.789 = 27.411$ mm. Measured $d_1 = 27.00$ mm — error 0.411 mm, indicating the thread is at the **lower limit of the tolerance band** (acceptable for an M30 × 3.5 6g fit).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End-Semester, Resit]**  
**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **two-wire method** of measuring the **effective diameter of a screw thread**. Derive the formula $d_2 = M_w - 3B$ for a 60° metric thread using best-size wires. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An **M16 × 2** thread is measured over **two best-size wires** on the profile-projector stage. The mean **measurement over wires is found to be 17.78 mm**. Determine the **effective diameter** of the thread and state whether the thread is within the **ISO tolerance for a 6g fit**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution B(a) — Two-Wire Method + Derivation (7 marks)**

1. **Diagram** (2 marks): Two cylindrical wires placed diametrically in opposite V-grooves of the thread, resting on the flanks. The micrometer/profiler measures the distance $M_w$ over the outermost crests of the wires.
2. **Principle** (1 mark): When the wires are of best size, their centres lie on the **pitch line (effective-diameter cylinder)** of the thread.
3. **Derivation** (4 marks): From the geometry of the inscribed circle inside an inverted V-groove of half-angle $\beta = \alpha/2$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{P}{2\,\cos\beta} \quad \text{(best-size condition)} \\[4pt]
M_w &= d_2 + B\bigl(1 + \csc\beta\bigr) - \frac{P}{2}\cot\beta
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\alpha = 60°$ ⇒ $\beta = 30°$, $\csc 30° = 2$, $\cot 30° = \sqrt{3}$. Substituting best-size $B = P/\sqrt{3}$ and simplifying the radial terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= d_2 + 3B - \sqrt{3}\,P \\[2pt]
    &= d_2 + 3B - 3B \quad \bigl(\text{since } \sqrt{3}\,P = \sqrt{3}\cdot\sqrt{3}\,B = 3B\bigr) \\[2pt]
\therefore\ d_2 &= M_w - 3B
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Solution B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $M16 \times 2$ ⇒ $P = 2.00$ mm; $\alpha = 60°$; $M_w = 17.78$ mm.

**Step 1 — Best-size wire** (2 marks):

$$
B = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2.00}{1.7321} = 1.1547 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective diameter** (2 marks):

$$
d_2 = M_w - 3B = 17.78 - 3 \times 1.1547 = 17.78 - 3.464 = 14.316 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with ISO tolerance** (3 marks):  
For **M16 × 2, 6g external thread** (ISO 965-1):  
– Fundamental deviation $e_s = -0.038$ mm  
– Tolerance $TD_2 = 0.125$ mm (pitch diameter)  

Hence pitch-diameter limits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{2,\max} &= (d_2)_{\text{basic}} + e_s + \tfrac{TD_2}{2} \\[2pt]
          &= 14.701 - 0.038 + 0.0625 = 14.7255 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
d_{2,\min} &= (d_2)_{\text{basic}} + e_s - \tfrac{TD_2}{2} \\[2pt]
          &= 14.701 - 0.038 - 0.0625 = 14.6005 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Measured $d_2 = 14.316$ mm < $14.6005$ mm ⇒ **The thread is UNDERSIZE and NOT within tolerance** for a 6g fit; it will pass a smaller nut (looser fit) and should be **rejected** or reworked. *(Students often forget to compare against the ISO limits — see warning below.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning**  
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by magnification:** Students frequently write the screen reading itself as the answer. Always quote **actual size = screen reading ÷ M**.  
> 2. **Using the wrong constant:** 0.6495 is for **60° metric** threads only. For BSW (55°), use **0.6403**; for ACME (29°), use **0.5**.  
> 3. **Two-wire vs three-wire confusion:** On a profile projector the **two-wire method** is used (the third wire is implicit by symmetry). The formula $d_2 = M_w - 3B$ is derived from a **two-wire setup** — do not use a different constant.  
> 4. **Forgetting the ISO tolerance check:** A numerical problem worth 7 marks almost always asks for a tolerance verdict — without it you lose 2–3 marks.  
> 5. **Mis-reading the graticule:** Always state the **screen diameter** (300 mm or 600 mm) and the **objective magnification** you used before quoting the answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Core instrument:** Profile projector = optical comparator using a collimated beam, an objective lens, and a calibrated graticule screen; magnifications 10×, 20×, 50×, 100×.
- **Thread parameters measurable:** major diameter **d**, minor diameter **d₁**, effective diameter **d₂**, pitch **P**, thread included angle **α**.
- **KTU constants (memorize):** $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.6495$, $\frac{5\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 1.0825$, $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$, $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.7321$.
- **Standard metric thread angle:** $\alpha = 60°$, **half-angle** $\beta = 30°$.
- **Effective-diameter relation (metric):** $d_2 = d - 0.6495\,P$.
- **Minor-diameter relation (metric):** $d_1 = d - 1.0825\,P$.
- **Best-size wire (metric):** $B = P / \sqrt{3} \approx 0.5774\,P$.
- **Two-wire reduced formula (metric):** $d_2 = M_w - 3B$.
- **Procedure order:** mount → focus → centre → measure d → measure d₁ → measure P → measure d₂ → measure α.
- **Final conversion:** **Actual size = Screen reading ÷ Magnification**.
- **Acceptance check:** Compare $d, d_2, d_1, \alpha$ against ISO 965 / IS 4218 tolerance tables; quote the tolerance class (e.g., 6g, 6H).
- **Common error to avoid:** forgetting to divide by magnification; using 0.6495 for non-metric threads; omitting the tolerance verdict in numerical answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Straightness Error Using Autocollimator, Spirit Level, and Straight Edge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Straightness Error Using Autocollimator, Spirit Level, and Straight Edge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Straightness (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Straightness** is a geometric form tolerance of a line or axis. According to ISO 1101 and ASME Y14.5 standards (which form the basis of KTU metrology curriculum), straightness is defined as:

> **Straightness** is the condition of a surface element or derived median line whereby all its points lie within a tolerance zone bounded by two parallel straight lines separated by a distance *t* (the tolerance value).

In simpler engineering metrology terms used in KTU labs:
- **Straightness Error** is the minimum distance between two parallel straight lines that just enclose (envelope) the entire actual profile/line being measured.

## 1.2 Classification of Straightness

| Type | Definition | Application |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Straightness of a Line** | All points of a line element must lie between two parallel straight lines | Shafts, edges, axes |
| **Straightness of an Axis** | The axis of a feature must lie between two parallel planes 0.04 mm apart | Holes, cylindrical features |
| **Straightness of a Surface Line** | A surface line element must lie between two parallel lines | Flat surfaces, slides |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** In the Measurements Lab (PCNSL408), we focus on **Straightness of a Line** for a horizontal surface (machine tool bed, straight edge, surface plate) evaluated across three classical methods.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you place a long iron rod on a workshop table and want to know how "straight" it is.

- **Real-life analogy:** Think of drawing a line on paper with a shaky hand. The actual drawn line is "wavy" and never perfectly straight. Now, place two perfectly straight rulers (one above and one below) parallel to each other such that the wavy line just fits between them. The gap between these two rulers is the **straightness error**.

- **Another analogy:** Think of a long road being built. Engineers keep checking with surveying instruments (autocollimator is essentially a precision surveying tool for the shop floor) to ensure the road is straight. If the road's centerline wavers by even 1 mm over 1 meter, that is a straightness deviation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why measure straightness?** In machine tools, even a small straightness error in the bed ways causes:
> - Poor alignment of moving parts
> - Vibration during cutting
> - Inaccurate workpiece geometry
> - Premature wear of slides and bearings
>
> Hence, straightness is **the most fundamental form tolerance** in precision engineering.

## 1.4 Principle Behind the Three Methods (One-Line Overview)

1. **Autocollimator Method:** Measures the **angular tilt** of small successive segments of the surface by projecting and receiving a reflected light beam. Most accurate and used for precision machine tools.
2. **Spirit Level Method:** Uses an **air-bubble level** to measure the slope between two adjacent positions of the level along the surface. Quick, portable, and field-usable.
3. **Straight Edge Method:** Uses a **physical master straight edge** as a reference datum. A dial indicator measures the gap between the straight edge and surface at regular intervals. Simplest and most economical.

## 1.5 Unit and Standards

- The straightness error is always expressed in **micrometers (µm)** or **millimeters (mm)**.
- A typical tolerance for a precision surface plate: **0.005 mm to 0.025 mm** over 1 m.
- For a machine tool bed: **0.02 mm to 0.05 mm** per meter length.
- The reference standards for straightness in KTU labs follow **IS 2063, IS 7320, and ISO 1101**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** Always mention the **envelope principle** (two parallel lines enclosing the profile) when defining straightness error. Examiners give credit for this keyword.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Straightness Error

Let us consider a surface line of length *L* divided into *n* equal stations separated by a distance *d*, such that:

$$L = n \times d$$

Let $y_1, y_2, y_3, \dots, y_n$ be the **deviations (heights)** of the actual profile from a chosen reference datum at each station.

The straightness error *E* is mathematically defined as:

$$E = y_{max} - y_{min} - correction$$

where the *correction* accounts for the tilt of the reference line (since we are free to orient the two parallel enclosing lines parallel to the best-fit straight line, not strictly horizontal).

## 2.2 Universal Formula for the Two-Envelope Method

If $h_i$ is the height at station *i* (relative to a common horizontal reference), and the profile is rotated by an angle $\theta$ to find the minimum envelope, then:

$$E = \left[ h_{max} - h_{min} \right] - L \cdot \tan\theta$$

where $\theta$ is chosen such that *E* is minimized.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Method** | **Measured Quantity** | **Key Formula** | **Units** |
|------------|------------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Autocollimator** | Angular tilt at each station | $\theta_i = \dfrac{R_i - R_{i-1}}{d}$ | radians |
| **Autocollimator** | Cumulative height deviation | $h_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \theta_j \cdot d$ | mm |
| **Spirit Level** | Reading per station (bubble displacement / sensitivity) | $\theta_i = \dfrac{(r_i - r_{i-1}) \cdot s}{d}$ | radians |
| **Spirit Level** | Height profile | $h_i = h_{i-1} + (r_i - r_{i-1}) \cdot s$ | mm |
| **Straight Edge** | Dial indicator reading | $y_i = D_i$ (direct) | mm |
| **All Methods** | Straightness Error | $E = y_{max} - y_{min}$ (after rotation correction) | mm |
| **Common** | Station spacing | $d = L / n$ | mm |
| **Common** | Sensitivity of spirit level | *s* (e.g., 0.01 mm/m) | mm/m |
| **Common** | Beam constant (autocollimator) | $k = 1 \text{ min of arc} = \dfrac{1}{60} \times \dfrac{\pi}{10800}$ rad ≈ $4.848 \times 10^{-6}$ | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use `|` in LaTeX within markdown tables.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` to keep table syntax valid.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for Any Straightness Measurement

### Step 1: Preparation
- Clean the surface and reference (straight edge / autocollimator base).
- Mount the measuring instrument firmly.
- Divide the total length *L* into *n* equal stations.

### Step 2: Data Acquisition
- Take readings at each station with the chosen instrument.
- For **autocollimator**: record the scale reading *R_i* (in minutes/seconds of arc).
- For **spirit level**: record the bubble displacement *r_i* (in divisions).
- For **straight edge**: record the dial indicator reading *D_i* (in mm or µm).

### Step 3: Compute Angular Slopes (Autocollimator/Spirit Level only)
- For each adjacent pair, compute the slope (tilt) using the difference in readings divided by the station distance.

### Step 4: Compute Height Profile
- Starting from the first station (height = 0), sum the slope contributions cumulatively.

### Step 5: Apply Rotation Correction
- Rotate the entire profile by the best-fit line angle so that the peak-to-valley height is minimized.

### Step 6: Report Straightness Error
- Report *E* = (maximum adjusted height) – (minimum adjusted height).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Tool Industry:** Verification of lathe bed, milling machine table, and grinding machine slide straightness.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spar and fuselage frame alignment.
- **Civil Engineering:** Alignment of rails, bridges, and high-rise columns.
- **Metrology Labs:** Calibration of surface plates, CMM axes, and precision linear stages.
- **Automotive:** Engine block deck surfaces, crankshaft journals.

## 2.6 Why Three Different Methods?

| Method | Accuracy | Cost | Best Use |
|--------|----------|------|----------|
| **Autocollimator** | ±0.5 arc-second (~0.0025 mm/m) | High (₹1,50,000+) | Precision labs, machine tool calibration |
| **Spirit Level** | ±0.02 mm/m (with electronic level) | Medium | Field alignment, large machine tools |
| **Straight Edge** | ±0.005 mm (limited by straight edge quality) | Low (₹5,000–₹20,000) | Workshop floor, basic inspection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual "Why":** Each successive method uses a different physical principle (optical, gravitational, mechanical contact). The choice depends on **accuracy needed vs. portability vs. cost**. This is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Method 1: Autocollimator — Complete Derivation

### 3.1.1 Principle

An **autocollimator** projects a collimated beam of light onto a reflector (mirror) placed on the surface. The reflected beam returns, and the angular displacement of the image with respect to a fixed reticle is read directly. If the mirror tilts by an angle $\alpha$ between two stations, the reflected beam deviates by $2\alpha$ (law of reflection).

### 3.1.2 Geometry

Let the mirror be placed at station *i* and station *i+1*. The angular tilt of segment between them is $\theta_i$.

The autocollimator reading is $R_i$ (in seconds or minutes of arc). The relationship between reading and actual mirror tilt is:

$$\theta_i = \frac{R_i}{2} \quad \text{(in arc units)}$$

But since $R_i$ is typically already calibrated to give $\theta_i$ directly, we use:

$$\theta_i = R_i \times k$$

where $k$ is the **beam constant** (radians per division).

### 3.1.3 Height Profile Derivation

Consider station *i* at horizontal position $x_i = i \cdot d$ from the origin. The height of the surface at station *i* relative to station 1 (taken as 0) is:

$$h_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \theta_j \cdot d$$

This is because each segment of length *d* tilts by $\theta_j$, contributing a vertical rise of $\theta_j \cdot d$.

**Expanded form:**

$$h_1 = 0$$
$$h_2 = \theta_1 \cdot d$$
$$h_3 = (\theta_1 + \theta_2) \cdot d$$
$$h_4 = (\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) \cdot d$$
$$\vdots$$
$$h_n = \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \theta_j \cdot d$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (Autocollimator)

**Given:** Length $L = 1000$ mm, divided into $n = 5$ equal stations, so $d = 250$ mm. Autocollimator readings in seconds of arc:

| Station *i* | Reading $R_i$ (arc-seconds) |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | 0 |
| 2 | +8 |
| 3 | +15 |
| 4 | +10 |
| 5 | +18 |

**Step 1: Compute slope $\theta_i$ between stations.**

The slope between station *i* and *i+1* is:

$$\theta_i = \frac{R_{i+1} - R_i}{2}$$

Reason: The mirror at each station gives tilt relative to the autocollimator's optical axis. The slope between two stations is the change in tilt divided by 2 (autocollimator measures twice the actual tilt).

Actually, the correct formula assuming the reading gives $\theta$ directly at each station:

$$\theta_i = \frac{R_{i+1} - R_i}{d}$$

To keep it simple, assume $R_i$ gives slope in rad/m directly. Then:

$$\theta_1 = \frac{8 - 0}{250} \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{\pi}{180 \times 3600}$$ — too complex. Let us use a simpler table.

**Simpler KTU-style Example:** Readings given directly in mm/m (the actual slope):

| Station *i* | $R_i$ (mm/m) |
|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | 0.00 |
| 2 | +0.02 |
| 3 | +0.04 |
| 4 | -0.01 |
| 5 | +0.03 |

**Step 2: Compute slope of each segment.**

Slope between stations = $\frac{R_{i+1} - R_i}{d}$ × $d$ = $R_{i+1} - R_i$ (change in slope).

Wait, the cleaner approach used in KTU labs:

The cumulative height at each station is computed as:

$$h_i = R_i \times d \text{ (if } R_i \text{ is the local slope from the first station)}$$

Or, if $R_i$ is the *cumulative* reading, then $h_i = R_i$ directly scaled.

For this example, let $R_i$ represent the slope *from station 1 to station i* in mm/m.

Then:
- $h_1 = 0.00 \times 0.250 = 0.000$ mm
- $h_2 = 0.02 \times 0.250 = 0.005$ mm
- $h_3 = 0.04 \times 0.250 = 0.010$ mm
- $h_4 = -0.01 \times 0.250 = -0.0025$ mm
- $h_5 = 0.03 \times 0.250 = 0.0075$ mm

**Step 3: Apply rotation correction.**

Find the slope of the best-fit line by least-squares or by the line that minimizes peak-to-valley:

Best-fit slope $m_{bf}$ ≈ (average of first and last slope values) = $(0.00 + 0.03)/2 = 0.015$ mm/m

Adjusted heights $h'_i = h_i - m_{bf} \times x_i$:

- $h'_1 = 0.000 - 0.015 \times 0 = 0.000$ mm
- $h'_2 = 0.005 - 0.015 \times 0.250 = 0.00125$ mm
- $h'_3 = 0.010 - 0.015 \times 0.500 = 0.0025$ mm
- $h'_4 = -0.0025 - 0.015 \times 0.750 = -0.01375$ mm
- $h'_5 = 0.0075 - 0.015 \times 1.000 = -0.0075$ mm

**Step 4: Compute straightness error.**

$$E = h'_{max} - h'_{min} = 0.0025 - (-0.01375) = 0.01625 \text{ mm} \approx 16.25 \text{ µm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer for KTU exam:** The straightness error of the surface is **16.25 µm** or **0.01625 mm**.

---

## 3.2 Method 2: Spirit Level — Complete Derivation

### 3.2.1 Principle

A **spirit level** uses an air bubble in a liquid-filled vial with a graduated scale. The bubble moves relative to the vial markings based on the local tilt. The **sensitivity** *s* of the level is the bubble movement (in mm) per division when the surface tilts by 1 mm/m.

### 3.2.2 Reading Interpretation

If the bubble reads $r_i$ divisions at station *i* and $r_{i+1}$ divisions at station *i+1* (each station being separated by *d* meters), the slope between them is:

$$\theta_i = \frac{(r_{i+1} - r_i) \cdot s}{d} \quad \text{(in mm/m)}$$

The sensitivity *s* is given on the level. For a typical electronic or precision level: $s = 0.01$ mm/m per division (or similar).

### 3.2.3 Height Profile

The cumulative height at station *i* is:

$$h_i = h_{i-1} + (r_i - r_{i-1}) \cdot s$$

with $h_1 = 0$.

### 3.2.4 Worked Example (Spirit Level)

**Given:** Level sensitivity $s = 0.02$ mm/m per division. Station spacing $d = 0.2$ m. Total length = 1 m. Five stations.

| Station *i* | Reading $r_i$ (divisions) |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | 0.0 |
| 2 | +2.0 |
| 3 | +3.5 |
| 4 | +2.5 |
| 5 | +4.0 |

**Step 1: Compute height profile.**

$$h_1 = 0.0$$
$$h_2 = (2.0 - 0.0) \times 0.02 = 0.040 \text{ mm/m} \times d$$

Wait, the units are in mm/m. To get absolute height in mm, multiply by distance:

$$h_i = \sum_{j=2}^{i} (r_j - r_{j-1}) \cdot s \cdot d$$

Actually the simpler and correct KTU approach:

The slope from station 1 to station *i* is $(r_i - r_1) \times s$ (in mm/m).
The height at station *i* relative to station 1 is:

$$h_i = (r_i - r_1) \times s \times d_i$$

where $d_i = (i-1) \times d$ is the distance from station 1.

Compute:
- $h_1 = 0$
- $h_2 = (2.0 - 0) \times 0.02 \times 0.2 = 0.008$ mm
- $h_3 = (3.5 - 0) \times 0.02 \times 0.4 = 0.028$ mm
- $h_4 = (2.5 - 0) \times 0.02 \times 0.6 = 0.030$ mm
- $h_5 = (4.0 - 0) \times 0.02 \times 0.8 = 0.064$ mm

**Step 2: Apply rotation correction.**

Best-fit slope: linear fit gives slope ≈ 0.064/0.8 = 0.08 mm/m. Subtract:
- $h'_1 = 0$
- $h'_2 = 0.008 - 0.08 \times 0.2 = -0.008$ mm
- $h'_3 = 0.028 - 0.08 \times 0.4 = -0.004$ mm
- $h'_4 = 0.030 - 0.08 \times 0.6 = -0.018$ mm
- $h'_5 = 0.064 - 0.08 \times 0.8 = 0.000$ mm

**Step 3: Straightness error.**

$$E = h'_{max} - h'_{min} = 0.000 - (-0.018) = 0.018 \text{ mm} = 18 \text{ µm}$$

---

## 3.3 Method 3: Straight Edge — Complete Derivation

### 3.3.1 Principle

A **straight edge** is a precision-machined master reference (typically cast iron or granite) whose straightness is certified (e.g., 0.005 mm over 1 m). The straight edge is placed on the surface, and a **dial indicator** (or feeler gauge) measures the perpendicular gap between the straight edge's lower surface and the workpiece surface at regular intervals.

### 3.3.2 Procedure

1. Place the straight edge on the surface with both ends resting on equal-height parallels (or on the surface directly).
2. Set the dial indicator to zero at the first station.
3. Move the indicator (or move the straight edge) to successive stations.
4. Record the indicator reading $D_i$ at each station (positive = above reference, negative = below).

### 3.3.3 Direct Straightness Error

If the straight edge is **rigid and certified straight**, and is **leveled** using parallels, then the dial indicator reading directly gives the deviation of the surface from the straight edge:

$$y_i = D_i$$

And the straightness error is simply:

$$E = y_{max} - y_{min}$$

No rotation correction is needed because the straight edge defines the reference line.

### 3.3.4 Worked Example (Straight Edge)

**Given:** Straight edge of length 1000 mm. Five stations at 200 mm spacing. Dial indicator readings:

| Station *i* | $D_i$ (mm) |
|-------------|------------|
| 1 | 0.000 |
| 2 | +0.010 |
| 3 | +0.022 |
| 4 | +0.005 |
| 5 | -0.008 |

**Step 1: Identify max and min.**

$$y_{max} = +0.022 \text{ mm (at station 3)}$$
$$y_{min} = -0.008 \text{ mm (at station 5)}$$

**Step 2: Compute straightness error.**

$$E = 0.022 - (-0.008) = 0.030 \text{ mm} = 30 \text{ µm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** If the straight edge is placed on **two equal blocks** at the ends and the dial indicator reads at the middle, the reading is the "sag correction" — but for level surfaces, blocks of equal height are used at all stations or the straight edge is shimmed.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Autocollimator | Spirit Level | Straight Edge |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|---------------|
| **Physical principle** | Optical reflection | Gravitational (bubble) | Mechanical contact |
| **Reference** | Light beam | Gravity | Master straight edge |
| **Resolution** | 0.5 arc-sec (≈ 2.5 µm/m) | 0.02 mm/m | 0.005 mm |
| **Setup time** | 20 min | 5 min | 5 min |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Source of error** | Mirror flatness, air turbulence | Level sensitivity, parallax | Straight edge straightness, dirt |
| **Best for** | Precision machine tools, lab | Field, large machines | Workshop, quick checks |
| **Reading type** | Angular | Angular | Linear (height) |
| **Computation needed** | Yes (integration of slopes) | Yes (scaling) | No (direct) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Straightness Evaluation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Surface Under Test] --> A1[Clean and Level the Surface]
    A1 --> A2[Define Total Length L]
    A2 --> A3[Choose Station Spacing d]
    A3 --> A4[Number of Stations n = L / d]
    A4 --> A5{Select Measurement Method}

    A5 -->|Method 1| B1[Autocollimator Setup]
    A5 -->|Method 2| B2[Spirit Level Setup]
    A5 -->|Method 3| B3[Straight Edge + Dial Indicator Setup]

    B1 --> C1[Place Mirror at Station 1]
    C1 --> C2[Record Reading R1]
    C2 --> C3[Move Mirror to Station 2]
    C3 --> C4[Record Reading R2]
    C4 --> C5[Repeat for All n Stations]
    C5 --> D1[Compute Slope theta_i = R difference / d]
    D1 --> D2[Cumulative Height h_i = sum of theta x d]
    D2 --> D3[Apply Best-Fit Rotation Correction]
    D3 --> D4[Compute E = h max - h min]

    B2 --> E1[Place Level at Station 1]
    E1 --> E2[Record Bubble Reading r1]
    E2 --> E3[Move Level to Station i]
    E3 --> E4[Record Reading r_i]
    E4 --> E5[Repeat for All n Stations]
    E5 --> F1[Compute h_i = r_i - r1 x sensitivity x d]
    F1 --> F2[Apply Rotation Correction]
    F2 --> F3[Compute E = h max - h min]

    B3 --> G1[Place Straight Edge on Surface]
    G1 --> G2[Set Dial Indicator to Zero at Station 1]
    G2 --> G3[Move Indicator to Each Station]
    G3 --> G4[Record Dial Reading D_i]
    G4 --> G5[Repeat for All n Stations]
    G5 --> H1[Direct E = D max - D min]
    H1 --> H2[No Rotation Correction Needed]

    D4 --> I1[Final Result: Straightness Error E]
    F3 --> I1
    H2 --> I1
    I1 --> I2[Report in micrometres or mm]
    I2 --> I3[Compare with Tolerance Spec]
    I3 --> I4{Surface Passes?}
    I4 -->|Yes| I5[Accept and Document]
    I4 -->|No| I6[Reject / Re-machine / Re-align]
```

## 4.2 Autocollimator Optical Path Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Light Source] --> A2[Beam Splitter]
    A2 --> A3[Collimating Lens]
    A3 --> A4[Parallel Light Beam]
    A4 --> A5[Reflector Mirror on Surface]
    A5 --> A6[Reflected Beam 2x alpha]
    A6 --> A2
    A2 --> A7[Eyepiece / Detector]
    A7 --> A8[Scale Reading in arc-seconds]
    A8 --> A9[Tilt Angle Calculation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safe-Build Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels contain only plain uppercase text without markdown bold/italic tags. The diagram renders correctly in GitHub, VS Code, and Confluence.

## 4.3 Comparative Methodology Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Optical Method"
        M1[Autocollimator]
    end
    subgraph "Gravitational Method"
        M2[Spirit Level]
    end
    subgraph "Mechanical Method"
        M3[Straight Edge + Dial Indicator]
    end

    SURF[Surface Profile Deviations] --> M1
    SURF --> M2
    SURF --> M3

    M1 --> P1[Convert to Angular Slopes]
    M2 --> P2[Convert to Angular Slopes]
    M3 --> P3[Direct Height Readings]

    P1 --> CALC[Compute Height Profile]
    P2 --> CALC
    P3 --> ERR[Direct Error]

    CALC --> ROT[Apply Rotation Correction]
    ROT --> ERR
    ERR --> RESULT[Straightness Error E in micrometres]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**Define straightness error. Mention the two reference lines used in its evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Straightness error is the minimum distance between two parallel straight lines that just enclose the actual profile of the line/surface element being measured. It quantifies how far a real surface deviates from an ideal straight line.
- **Two reference lines (1.5 marks):** The two parallel straight lines are:
  1. The **upper bounding line** (passes through the highest peak of the profile)
  2. The **lower bounding line** (passes through the lowest valley of the profile)
- Both lines are oriented **parallel to each other** and to the best-fit line through the profile.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**List any three methods for evaluating straightness error and state the principle behind the autocollimator method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Three methods (1.5 marks):**
  1. Autocollimator method
  2. Spirit level method
  3. Straight edge method (with dial indicator and feeler gauge)
  4. (Bonus) Laser interferometer method
- **Principle of autocollimator (1.5 marks):** The autocollimator projects a parallel beam of light onto a reflector placed on the surface. The reflected beam returns with an angular deviation of *2θ* (twice the surface tilt) due to the law of reflection. This angular deviation is measured against a fixed reticle, giving the local slope of the surface. Cumulative summation of slopes over station intervals yields the height profile.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1: Autocollimator + Numerical

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the working principle of an autocollimator. Explain how it is used for evaluating the straightness of a machine tool bed.

**(b) [7 marks]** The following readings were taken with an autocollimator along a length of 1200 mm divided into 6 equal stations. The readings (in mm per metre) at stations 1 to 6 are: 0.00, 0.04, 0.09, 0.06, 0.11, 0.07. Calculate the straightness error.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Description of Autocollimator [7 marks]:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** An autocollimator is a precision optical instrument that measures small angular displacements of a reflecting surface with high accuracy (typically 0.5 to 5 arc-seconds).

2. **Construction (2 marks):**
   - **Light source** (illuminated graticule/target)
   - **Beam splitter** (semi-reflecting glass)
   - **Collimating lens** (to produce parallel light rays)
   - **Eyepiece** with calibrated reticle (in arc-seconds or minutes)
   - **Reflector** (plane mirror placed on the surface to be tested)

3. **Working principle (2 marks):**
   - Light from the source is directed via the beam splitter to the collimating lens, which produces a parallel beam.
   - This parallel beam strikes the plane mirror placed on the surface and is reflected back.
   - The mirror image is observed through the eyepiece. When the mirror is perfectly perpendicular to the optical axis, the image coincides with the reference reticle (zero reading).
   - If the surface (and mirror) tilts by an angle *θ*, the reflected beam deflects by *2θ* (law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection; change in mirror angle = change in reflected beam angle).
   - The eyepiece scale measures this *2θ* deviation directly in arc-units.

4. **Application to machine tool bed straightness (2 marks):**
   - The machine tool bed is divided into *n* equal stations (typically 10 to 20 per metre).
   - A reflector (mirror) is placed at each station.
   - The autocollimator is mounted on a stable tripod or pillar at one end.
   - At each station, the reading $R_i$ is noted.
   - The slope between two stations is $\theta_i = (R_{i+1} - R_i) / d$, where *d* is the station spacing.
   - The cumulative height at each station is $h_i = \sum \theta_j \cdot d$.
   - The straightness error = (max height) – (min height) after best-fit rotation.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $L = 1200$ mm, $n = 6$, $d = 200$ mm
