- Readings (in mm/m): 0.00, 0.04, 0.09, 0.06, 0.11, 0.07

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute cumulative slope × distance (= slope reading × spacing).

Each reading represents slope from station 1 to that station (in mm/m). To get height in mm, multiply by distance from station 1:

| Station | Reading (mm/m) | Distance from Stn 1 (m) | Height $h_i$ (mm) |
|---------|----------------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 0.00 | 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 0.04 | 0.2 | 0.008 |
| 3 | 0.09 | 0.4 | 0.036 |
| 4 | 0.06 | 0.6 | 0.036 |
| 5 | 0.11 | 0.8 | 0.088 |
| 6 | 0.07 | 1.0 | 0.070 |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify max and min heights.

$$h_{max} = 0.088 \text{ mm (station 5)}$$
$$h_{min} = 0.000 \text{ mm (station 1)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply best-fit rotation correction.

The straightness error must be computed between two parallel lines. The slope of the best-fit straight line is found by minimizing the peak-to-valley envelope:

Best-fit slope $m_{bf}$ is found by trial. If we take the line joining station 1 (h=0) and station 6 (h=0.07), the slope is 0.07 mm/m.

Adjusting heights by $m_{bf} \times x_i$:

| Station | $h_i$ (mm) | $m_{bf} \cdot x_i$ | $h'_i$ (mm) |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 0.008 | 0.014 | -0.006 |
| 3 | 0.036 | 0.028 | 0.008 |
| 4 | 0.036 | 0.042 | -0.006 |
| 5 | 0.088 | 0.056 | 0.032 |
| 6 | 0.070 | 0.070 | 0.000 |

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute straightness error.

$$h'_{max} = 0.032 \text{ mm (station 5)}$$
$$h'_{min} = -0.006 \text{ mm (stations 2 and 4)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 0.032 - (-0.006) = 0.038 \text{ mm} = 38 \text{ µm}}$$

**Result:** The straightness error of the surface is **0.038 mm** or **38 µm**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2: Spirit Level + Straight Edge

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the procedure for measuring straightness error using a spirit level. Mention the formula used and the role of level sensitivity.

**(b) [7 marks]** A straight edge of 1 metre length is placed on a machine tool surface. The following dial indicator readings (in mm) are taken at 5 equal stations: 0.000, 0.012, 0.025, 0.008, -0.005. Calculate the straightness error and comment on whether the surface meets a tolerance of 0.025 mm per metre.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Spirit Level Procedure [7 marks]:**

1. **Principle (1 mark):** A spirit level measures the **angular tilt** of a surface relative to the horizontal (gravity direction). The bubble position changes with tilt, and the displacement is calibrated to give the slope in mm per metre.

2. **Sensitivity of level (1.5 marks):**
   - Sensitivity *s* is defined as the **bubble movement (in mm) per division** when the level is tilted by 1 mm per metre.
   - Typical values: $s = 0.01$ to $0.05$ mm/m per division.
   - The sensitivity is engraved on the level vial or specified in the calibration certificate.

3. **Procedure (3.5 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Clean the surface and the bottom of the spirit level.
   - **Step 2:** Divide the surface length *L* into *n* equal stations of spacing *d* = *L/n*.
   - **Step 3:** Place the level at station 1 along the direction of measurement. Wait 30 seconds for the bubble to stabilize.
   - **Step 4:** Note the bubble reading $r_1$ (left-end and right-end readings; their average is the mean reading).
   - **Step 5:** Lift and reposition the level at the next station (or slide if using a continuous reading level). Note $r_2$, $r_3$, …, $r_n$.
   - **Step 6:** Compute the slope of each segment: $\theta_i = (r_{i+1} - r_i) \cdot s / d$ in mm/m.
   - **Step 7:** Compute cumulative height: $h_i = \sum \theta_j \cdot d$ (with $h_1 = 0$).
   - **Step 8:** Apply best-fit rotation correction.
   - **Step 9:** Compute $E = h'_{max} - h'_{min}$.

4. **Formula (1 mark):**
   $$h_i = (r_i - r_1) \cdot s \cdot x_i$$
   where $x_i$ is the distance from station 1 to station *i* in metres.

**(b) Straight Edge Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $L = 1000$ mm, $n = 5$, $d = 200$ mm
- Dial readings (mm): 0.000, 0.012, 0.025, 0.008, -0.005

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Tabulate readings.

| Station | $D_i$ (mm) |
|---------|-------------|
| 1 | 0.000 |
| 2 | +0.012 |
| 3 | +0.025 |
| 4 | +0.008 |
| 5 | -0.005 |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Since the straight edge is the direct reference (no rotation correction needed for a certified straight edge on equal supports), the readings give direct deviation from straight:

$$y_{max} = +0.025 \text{ mm (station 3)}$$
$$y_{min} = -0.005 \text{ mm (station 5)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute straightness error.

$$E = y_{max} - y_{min} = 0.025 - (-0.005) = 0.030 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compare with tolerance.

- Tolerance specified: 0.025 mm per metre
- Measured error: 0.030 mm per metre
- Since **0.030 mm > 0.025 mm**, the surface **DOES NOT MEET** the specified tolerance.

**Result:** The straightness error is **0.030 mm (30 µm)**, which exceeds the tolerance of 0.025 mm. The surface must be re-machined (scraped or ground) to meet the specification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (Examiner's Red Flags):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting rotation correction (lose 2-3 marks):** Many students compute $E = y_{max} - y_{min}$ directly without applying the best-fit line correction. In KTU valuation, examiners explicitly check for the **two parallel lines oriented to minimise the peak-to-valley distance**. Always apply correction.
>
> 2. **Unit confusion (lose 1 mark):** Mixing arc-seconds with mm, or mm/m with mm. Always carry units in every step.
>
> 3. **Station spacing error (lose 1 mark):** If $L = 1000$ mm and $n = 5$, then $d = 200$ mm. Many students mistakenly use $d = 1000$ mm.
>
> 4. **No diagram / poor sketch (lose 2 marks):** In long answers, a neat labelled diagram of the autocollimator or setup is mandatory. Use a proper rectangle for the instrument and mirror, label all components.
>
> 5. **Wrong formula for autocollimator (lose 2 marks):** Remember the law of reflection — the reflected beam deflects by *2θ*, not *θ*. The reading must be divided by 2 if it gives the reflected beam deviation.
>
> 6. **Not stating sensitivity value (lose 1 mark):** In spirit level problems, always mention the level sensitivity *s* and use it explicitly in the calculation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Straightness error =** (max adjusted height) – (min adjusted height), measured between two parallel enclosing lines (envelope principle).
- **IS 2063 / ISO 1101** are the governing standards for form tolerances.
- **Station spacing:** $d = L / n$. Always equal spacing for KTU problems.
- **Three methods:**
  1. **Autocollimator** → angular measurement, most accurate.
  2. **Spirit level** → gravitational, field-portable.
  3. **Straight edge** → mechanical, simplest.
- **Autocollimator formula:** height $h_i = \sum (\theta_j \cdot d)$; reflect angle doubles.
- **Spirit level formula:** $h_i = (r_i - r_1) \cdot s \cdot x_i$ where *s* = sensitivity (mm/m per division).
- **Straight edge formula:** $E = D_{max} - D_{min}$ (direct, no integration).
- **Rotation correction** is mandatory for autocollimator and spirit level methods.
- **Typical units:** micrometres (µm) for final error, mm/m for slope, mm for station spacing.
- **Tolerance check:** Compare computed *E* with the spec; report pass/fail.
- **Sources of error:** Mirror flatness (autocollimator), level parallax (spirit level), straight edge bow (straight edge), temperature gradients, dirt on surface.
- **Best practice:** Always clean the surface, allow thermal stabilization, and use a shielded environment for optical measurements.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme weightage:** This experiment typically carries **8-12 marks** in the practical exam and **7-14 marks** in the ESE theory questions for related topics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration and Determination of Uncertainties of Tachometers and Stroboscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration and Determination of Uncertainties of Tachometers and Stroboscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Tachometer?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *tachometer* (from Greek *tachos* = speed + *metron* = measure) is an instrument designed to measure the **instantaneous rotational speed** of a shaft or disk, expressed in revolutions per minute (**rpm**) or revolutions per second (**rps**).

A tachometer is, in essence, the "speedometer" of the rotating-machinery world. Just as a car's speedometer tells you how fast the wheels are turning the road, a tachometer tells an engineer how fast a motor shaft, turbine rotor, or grinding wheel is spinning. Without it, we cannot verify whether a centrifugal pump is operating at its design point, whether a CNC spindle is at the correct cutting speed, or whether a generator is synchronized to the grid frequency.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Tachometers (KTU Syllabus Focus)

Tachometers fall into two broad engineering categories:

| Category | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Tachometer** | Centrifugal force on a flyweight, or liquid-column head | Hand-held, low-precision, portable |
| **Electrical / Electronic Tachometer** | EMF induced in a coil by a rotating magnet (DC/AC tachogenerator), pulses from a magnetic/optical pickup, or frequency-counting | Industrial panel-mount, high-accuracy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **PCNSL408 lab**, the most commonly calibrated instruments are the **contact-type digital tachometer** (handheld, with a rubber cone that engages the shaft) and the **non-contact photo/optical tachometer** (uses a reflective tape and light beam).

## 1.2 What is a Stroboscope?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *stroboscope* is a non-contact optical instrument that emits **flashes of light at a precisely controlled, adjustable frequency**. When this flash frequency matches (or an integer sub-multiple of) the rotational frequency of a moving object, the object appears **stationary** to the human eye due to the *stroboscopic effect*.

### The Real-World Analogy

Imagine spinning a ceiling fan in a dark room while flicking a torch on and off in perfect rhythm with the blades. If you time each flash for the moment a specific blade is at the "12 o'clock" position, the fan **looks frozen** — even though it is actually spinning at full speed. Your brain is fooled because every flash paints the same image on your retina.

That is exactly what a stroboscope does — except it flashes thousands of times per second with millisecond precision. If the flash rate $f_s$ exactly equals the rotation frequency $f_r$, the shaft looks stationary. If $f_s = f_r/2$, the shaft looks like it is rotating at half speed; if $f_s = 2f_r$, it looks like it is rotating twice as fast. This makes stroboscopes **invaluable for observing high-speed machinery without touching it** — which is critical for hot, dangerous, or sealed systems.

## 1.3 What is "Calibration"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Calibration** is the experimental procedure of comparing the *output (indicated value)* of an instrument against the *input value* of a **traceable reference standard**, under specified conditions, to quantify the **accuracy, linearity, and uncertainty** of the instrument.

Every measuring instrument drifts with time, temperature, vibration, and mechanical wear. Calibration answers three questions:

1. **How close** is the reading to the truth? (Bias / Offset)
2. **Is the scale linear?** (Linearity error)
3. **What is my doubt band?** (Uncertainty)

## 1.4 What is "Uncertainty"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Measurement Uncertainty** is a **quantitative parameter, associated with the result of a measurement, that characterizes the dispersion of values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand** (per ISO/IEC Guide 98-3, the *Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement* — commonly called **GUM**).

The key insight for students: **uncertainty is NOT the same as error.** Error is the *difference* between the measured value and the true value (a single number, often unknown). Uncertainty is a *range* — a confidence interval — that we can statistically defend. In every well-conducted KTU lab, you **must** report your result as $X = \bar{x} \pm U$ with a stated coverage probability (typically **95%**, $k = 2$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Contact Tachometer (Mechanical/Electronic)

A typical lab **contact tachometer** has three functional sub-systems:

1. **Input shaft / rubber cone** — engages with the rotating shaft of the motor under test and rotates at the same rpm.
2. **Speed-sensing transducer** — converts this rotation into an electrical signal.
3. **Display / counter** — counts pulses per unit time and displays the rpm.

Modern contact tachometers use a **slotted disc + photodiode** or a **magnetic pickup (variable reluctance)** inside. The transducer generates $N$ pulses per revolution (often **N = 60**, so that pulse rate in Hz *directly equals rpm*).

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Photo/Optical Tachometer

1. A strip of **reflective tape** (or a small piece of white paint) is applied to the rotating shaft.
2. The instrument shines an **LED** onto the shaft.
3. Once per revolution, light is reflected back into a **photodetector**, generating one pulse.
4. Internal electronics measure the **period** (or count pulses over a **gating time**) and display rpm.

## 2.3 Operating Principle — Stroboscope

The strobe lamp is driven by an **internal oscillator** (often a 555 timer IC or a microcontroller with crystal reference). The flash frequency $f_s$ is adjustable by the user via a knob or digital keypad.

Two operation modes are possible:

- **Synchronous (resonance) mode:** The user adjusts $f_s$ until the rotating shaft **visually freezes**. Then $f_s = f_r / n$, where $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ is the order of the visible image.
- **Internal-mode / digital-strobe tachometer:** A microcomputer measures the back-reflected light from the rotating shaft while flashing, deduces the true rpm, and **directly displays the rpm on a screen**.

## 2.4 Calibration Procedure — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why calibrate against a reference standard?**
> Because all instruments have systematic offset, gain error, and non-linearity. Calibration maps these defects so they can be corrected or reported.

The calibration chain in this KTU experiment is:

$$\text{Master Reference (Precision Motor Controller)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Device Under Test (DUT)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Reading}$$

The procedure is:

1. Set the **variable-speed DC motor** (driven by a calibrated power supply) at a known reference speed $N_{\text{ref},i}$.
2. Measure the indicated speed on the **tachometer** (or read the strobe frequency $f_{s,i}$ at the "frozen" condition).
3. Repeat for **at least 5–6 different speeds** spanning the range of the DUT (e.g., 200, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500 rpm).
4. Compute mean indicated value, deviation, and uncertainty.
5. Plot calibration curve: $N_{\text{ind}}$ (y-axis) vs $N_{\text{ref}}$ (x-axis); fit a straight line $N_{\text{ind}} = a \cdot N_{\text{ref}} + b$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — every KTU valuation key has at least one of them.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed in Hz from rpm | $f = N/60$ | $N$ = rpm, $f$ = rev/s |
| 2 | Linear speed at radius $r$ | $v = \pi D N / 60$ | $D = 2r$ = shaft diameter |
| 3 | **Stroboscope relation** | $N_{\text{shaft}} = 60 \, f_s / n$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ (order of image) |
| 4 | **Absolute error at point $i$** | $e_i = N_{\text{ind},i} - N_{\text{ref},i}$ | Single-point deviation |
| 5 | **Mean indicated value** | $\bar{N}_{\text{ind}} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_{\text{ind},i}$ | Average of repeat readings |
| 6 | **Experimental standard deviation** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(N_{\text{ind},i} - \bar{N}_{\text{ind}})^2}$ | Repeatability spread |
| 7 | **Standard uncertainty (Type A)** | $u_A = s / \sqrt{n}$ | From repeatability |
| 8 | **Resolution-limited Type-B uncertainty** | $u_B = \dfrac{\Delta x}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | $\Delta x$ = smallest division / display resolution |
| 9 | **Reference-standard uncertainty** | $u_{\text{ref}}$ | From calibration certificate of master |
| 10 | **Combined standard uncertainty** | $u_c = \sqrt{u_A^2 + u_B^2 + u_{\text{ref}}^2}$ | RSS of all sources |
| 11 | **Expanded uncertainty (95%)** | $U = k \, u_c$ with $k = 2$ | Final reported uncertainty |
| 12 | **Final result format** | $N = \bar{N}_{\text{ind}} \pm U$ | Always report this way |
| 13 | **Linear regression slope** | $a = \dfrac{\sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}}{\sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2}$ | Calibration curve fit |
| 14 | **Linear regression intercept** | $b = \bar{y} - a\bar{x}$ | Calibration curve fit |
| 15 | **Correlation coefficient** | $r = \dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2}}$ | Linearity check |

**Critical note on display resolution:** If a digital tachometer displays **±1 LSD** (least significant digit), then $\Delta x = 1$ LSD. The half-width of the rectangular probability distribution is $a = \Delta x / 2$, and dividing by $\sqrt{3}$ gives the standard uncertainty (rectangular distribution, GUM).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tachometers** are mandatory in **CNC machines, BLDC motors, wind turbines, hard-disk spindles, gyroscopes, and automotive dashboards**. ISO 9001 quality systems require periodic tachometer calibration.
- **Stroboscopes** are used for **motion analysis of high-speed machinery**, inspecting **printing-press rollers, textile spindles, fan blades, gear-mesh dynamics, and vibration studies** — all without stopping the machine.
- **Uncertainty analysis** is mandatory in any ISO/IEC **17025-accredited calibration lab**, and is the foundational skill behind **Six-Sigma metrology and Design-of-Experiments (DoE)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $u_B = \Delta x / (2\sqrt{3})$ ?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation question.** Be ready to reproduce it.

When an instrument shows a digital reading, the **true value lies somewhere within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSD** of the displayed number. We have **zero information** about *where* inside that window the true value lies — so by the Principle of Maximum Entropy (Jaynes), we assign a **uniform (rectangular) probability distribution** of half-width $a = \Delta x / 2$.

The variance of a rectangular distribution of half-width $a$ over $[-a, +a]$ is:

$$\sigma^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{2a}\int_{-a}^{+a} x^2 \, dx$$

We evaluate the integral:

$$\sigma^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{2a}\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-a}^{+a} \;=\; \frac{1}{2a}\left(\frac{a^3}{3} - \frac{-a^3}{3}\right) \;=\; \frac{1}{2a}\cdot\frac{2a^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 \;=\; \frac{a^2}{3}}$$

Therefore the **standard uncertainty** is:

$$u_B \;=\; \sigma \;=\; \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta x / 2}{\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta x}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

This is the canonical GUM expression for resolution-limited uncertainty.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full Calibration of a Contact Tachometer

### Given (typical KTU lab data)

A handheld contact digital tachometer (resolution = **1 rpm**, $N = 6$ test points, $k = 2$ for 95% confidence) is calibrated against a master reference tachometer whose standard uncertainty is $u_{\text{ref}} = 0.5$ rpm. The readings are:

| Trial $i$ | Reference $N_{\text{ref},i}$ (rpm) | Indicated $N_{\text{ind},i}$ (rpm) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 200 | 201 |
| 2 | 500 | 498 |
| 3 | 1000 | 1002 |
| 4 | 1500 | 1499 |
| 5 | 2000 | 2004 |
| 6 | 2500 | 2498 |

### Step 1 — Compute individual errors

$$e_1 = 201 - 200 = +1$$
$$e_2 = 498 - 500 = -2$$
$$e_3 = 1002 - 1000 = +2$$
$$e_4 = 1499 - 1500 = -1$$
$$e_5 = 2004 - 2000 = +4$$
$$e_6 = 2498 - 2500 = -2$$

### Step 2 — Mean indicated value

$$\bar{N}_{\text{ind}} \;=\; \frac{201 + 498 + 1002 + 1499 + 2004 + 2498}{6} \;=\; \frac{7702}{6} \;=\; 1283.67\ \text{rpm}$$

### Step 3 — Sum of squared deviations

$$\sum (N_{\text{ind},i} - \bar{N}_{\text{ind}})^2$$

We compute each deviation squared:

$$(201 - 1283.67)^2 = (-1082.67)^2 = 1{,}172{,}175.0$$
$$(498 - 1283.67)^2 = (-785.67)^2 = 617{,}277.4$$
$$(1002 - 1283.67)^2 = (-281.67)^2 = 79{,}338.1$$
$$(1499 - 1283.67)^2 = (215.33)^2 = 46{,}367.0$$
$$(2004 - 1283.67)^2 = (720.33)^2 = 518{,}875.4$$
$$(2498 - 1283.67)^2 = (1214.33)^2 = 1{,}474{,}597.7$$

Sum:

$$\sum (.)^2 = 1{,}172{,}175.0 + 617{,}277.4 + 79{,}338.1 + 46{,}367.0 + 518{,}875.4 + 1{,}474{,}597.7$$

$$\boxed{\sum (.)^2 = 3{,}908{,}630.6}$$

### Step 4 — Experimental standard deviation (Type A)

$$s \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum (N_{\text{ind},i} - \bar{N}_{\text{ind}})^2} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3{,}908{,}630.6}{5}}$$

$$s \;=\; \sqrt{781{,}726.1} \;=\; 884.15\ \text{rpm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Notice the **standard deviation is huge** because we are pooling readings taken at *very different* setpoints. In a proper KTU lab report, the **Type-A uncertainty is computed per setpoint** (e.g., repeat each speed 5 times) — not across the whole range. We are demonstrating the *algebraic procedure* here.

### Step 5 — Type-A uncertainty (standard uncertainty of the mean)

$$u_A \;=\; \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \;=\; \frac{884.15}{\sqrt{6}} \;=\; \frac{884.15}{2.449} \;=\; 360.97\ \text{rpm}$$

### Step 6 — Type-B uncertainty (resolution)

The display reads to the nearest **1 rpm**, so $\Delta x = 1$ rpm.

$$u_B \;=\; \frac{\Delta x}{2\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \frac{1}{3.464} \;=\; 0.2887\ \text{rpm}$$

### Step 7 — Reference standard uncertainty

Given: $u_{\text{ref}} = 0.5$ rpm.

### Step 8 — Combined standard uncertainty

$$u_c \;=\; \sqrt{u_A^2 + u_B^2 + u_{\text{ref}}^2}$$

$$u_c \;=\; \sqrt{(360.97)^2 + (0.2887)^2 + (0.5)^2}$$

$$u_c \;=\; \sqrt{130{,}299.3 + 0.0834 + 0.25}$$

$$u_c \;=\; \sqrt{130{,}299.6} \;=\; 360.97\ \text{rpm}$$

Notice $u_A$ dominates by orders of magnitude — this confirms the **practical lab takeaway**: the dominant uncertainty comes from the **spread of setpoints**, not from the resolution of the instrument.

### Step 9 — Expanded uncertainty (95% confidence, $k = 2$)

$$U \;=\; k \cdot u_c \;=\; 2 \times 360.97 \;=\; 721.94\ \text{rpm}$$

### Step 10 — Final reported result

$$\boxed{N \;=\; (1283.7 \pm 721.9)\ \text{rpm} \quad (k = 2,\ 95\%\ \text{confidence})}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Stroboscope Frequency Calculation

The strobe is set to "freeze" a shaft rotating at unknown speed $N$. The operator notes that **the shaft first appears stationary at $f_s = 50.0$ Hz**, then again at $f_s = 100.0$ Hz (where it looks like a 2-blade ghost), and again at $f_s = 25.0$ Hz (where it appears to rotate slowly backwards at 25 rpm). Determine the true shaft speed.

### Solution

Using $N_{\text{shaft}} = 60\,f_s / n$:

- At $n = 1$: $N = 60 \times 50.0 / 1 = 3000$ rpm
- At $n = 2$: $N = 60 \times 100.0 / 2 = 3000$ rpm ✓ (consistent)
- At $n = 1/2$ (i.e. $f_s = 25$ Hz, shaft rotates once per 2 flashes): the *visible* pattern moves, but the underlying shaft speed is still 3000 rpm.

**The shaft speed is 3000 rpm**, and 3000 rpm corresponds exactly to the **synchronous speed of a 4-pole AC motor on 50 Hz mains** ($n_s = 120f/P = 120 \times 50/2 = 3000$ rpm). This is a beautiful cross-check that proves our calibration.

If the strobe's internal oscillator has uncertainty $u_{f_s} = 0.1$ Hz, then:

$$u_N \;=\; \frac{\partial N}{\partial f_s}\,u_{f_s} \;=\; \frac{60}{n}\,u_{f_s} \;=\; \frac{60}{1}\times 0.1 \;=\; 6\ \text{rpm}$$

Expanded: $U = 2 \times 6 = 12$ rpm, so $N = (3000 \pm 12)$ rpm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Calibration Test Bench

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mainsAC["Mains AC 230V 50Hz"] --> vfd["Variable Frequency Drive"]
    vfd --> refSig["Reference Tachogenerator"]
    vfd --> motor["Variable Speed DC Motor"]
    motor --> shaft["Common Shaft Coupling"]
    shaft --> refTach["Master Reference Tachometer"]
    shaft --> dut1["DUT Contact Tachometer"]
    shaft --> dut2["DUT Stroboscope"]
    strobeRef["Strobe Lamp"] --> dut2
    refSig --> reader["Reader / Display"]
    refTach --> reader
    dut1 --> reader
    dut2 --> reader
    reader --> pc["Data Logger / PC"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Optical Tachometer Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    shaftA["Rotating Shaft with Reflective Tape"] --> led["IR LED Light Source"]
    led --> photodiode["Photodiode Detector"]
    photodiode --> amp["Amplifier and Comparator"]
    amp --> gate["Gating Counter 1 Second Window"]
    osc["Crystal Oscillator 1 Hz Gate"] --> gate
    gate --> pulseCount["Pulse Count Register"]
    pulseCount["Pulse Count Register"] --> lcd["LCD Display in RPM"]
    battery["Battery 9V DC"] --> reg["Voltage Regulator 5V"]
    reg --> amp
    reg --> osc
    reg --> lcd
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stroboscope Functional Architecture

```mermaid

    xtal["Crystal Oscillator 1 MHz"] --> dds
    dds --> driver["Xenon Lamp Trigger Driver"]
    driver --> xenon["Xenon Flash Lamp"]
    xenon -.illuminates.-> rotor["Rotating Shaft Under Test"]
    rotor -.reflects.-> sensor["Optical Pickup Sensor"]

```

## 4.4 Uncertainty Propagation Flow (GUM Methodology)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input["Measurand Input N"] --> typeA["Type A: Repeatability uA"]
    input --> typeB1["Type B: Resolution uB"]
    input --> typeB2["Type B: Reference Standard uRef"]
    input --> typeB3["Type B: Calibration Drift uDrift"]
    typeA --> rss1["RSS Combination"]
    typeB1 --> rss1
    typeB2 --> rss1
    typeB3 --> rss1
    rss1 --> uc["Combined Standard Uncertainty uc"]
    uc --> kfactor["Multiply by Coverage Factor k=2"]
    kfactor --> U["Expanded Uncertainty U 95 percent"]
    U --> report["Final Result N = Nbar plus minus U"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a tachometer and a stroboscope. State one engineering application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tachometer | Stroboscope |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Measures rotational speed directly in rpm | Produces flashing light to *visually freeze* rotating object; rpm is inferred from flash frequency |
| Contact | May be contact-type or non-contact | Always non-contact |
| Output | Digital / analog numerical display | Visual stationary image; digital models also display rpm |
| Typical use | Speed verification in motors, CNC spindles | Inspection of high-speed fans, gear mesh, printing rollers, vibration analysis |

**Application (any one, 1 mark):**
- *Tachometer:* RPM measurement of a centrifugal pump motor in a process plant.
- *Stroboscope:* Inspection of fan-blade tip wear in a running turbine.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define measurement uncertainty. State the GUM expression for resolution-limited (Type-B) uncertainty.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Measurement uncertainty is a quantitative parameter that characterizes the **dispersion of values** that can reasonably be attributed to the measurand, as defined in the ISO/IEC Guide 98-3 (GUM).

> **Formula (1 mark):**
> $$\boxed{u_B \;=\; \frac{\Delta x}{2\sqrt{3}}}$$
> where $\Delta x$ is the resolution (smallest division or 1 LSD) of the instrument.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**During calibration of a digital contact tachometer (resolution = 2 rpm) against a master reference tachometer (calibration certificate $u_{\text{ref}}$ = 1.5 rpm), the following readings were obtained:**

| Trial | $N_{\text{ref}}$ (rpm) | $N_{\text{ind}}$ (rpm) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 300 | 302 |
| 2 | 600 | 597 |
| 3 | 900 | 904 |
| 4 | 1200 | 1196 |
| 5 | 1500 | 1503 |
| 6 | 1800 | 1798 |

**(a)** Compute the **mean indicated speed**, the **experimental standard deviation**, and the **Type-A uncertainty** $u_A$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **Type-B uncertainty**, **combined uncertainty**, and the **expanded uncertainty** at $k = 2$. Report the final measurement result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mean indicated value (1 mark):**

$$\bar{N}_{\text{ind}} \;=\; \frac{302 + 597 + 904 + 1196 + 1503 + 1798}{6} \;=\; \frac{6300}{6} \;=\; 1050\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Deviations $d_i = N_{\text{ind},i} - \bar{N}_{\text{ind}}$ (1 mark):**

$$d_1 = -748,\ d_2 = -453,\ d_3 = -146,\ d_4 = 146,\ d_5 = 453,\ d_6 = 748$$

**Step 3 — Squared deviations (1.5 marks):**

$$d_1^2 = 559{,}504,\ d_2^2 = 205{,}209,\ d_3^2 = 21{,}316$$
$$d_4^2 = 21{,}316,\ d_5^2 = 205{,}209,\ d_6^2 = 559{,}504$$
$$\sum d_i^2 = 1{,}572{,}058$$

**Step 4 — Standard deviation (1.5 marks):**

$$s \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\sum d_i^2}{n-1}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1{,}572{,}058}{5}} \;=\; \sqrt{314{,}411.6} \;=\; 560.7\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Type-A uncertainty (2 marks):**

$$u_A \;=\; \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \;=\; \frac{560.7}{\sqrt{6}} \;=\; \frac{560.7}{2.449} \;=\; 228.9\ \text{rpm}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Type-B uncertainty (resolution $\Delta x = 2$ rpm) (2 marks):**

$$u_B \;=\; \frac{\Delta x}{2\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \frac{2}{2\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \;=\; 0.577\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Combined standard uncertainty (2 marks):**

$$u_c \;=\; \sqrt{u_A^2 + u_B^2 + u_{\text{ref}}^2}$$

$$u_c \;=\; \sqrt{(228.9)^2 + (0.577)^2 + (1.5)^2}$$

$$u_c \;=\; \sqrt{52{,}395.2 + 0.333 + 2.25} \;=\; \sqrt{52{,}397.8} \;=\; 228.9\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Expanded uncertainty (1.5 marks):**

$$U \;=\; k \cdot u_c \;=\; 2 \times 228.9 \;=\; 457.8\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Final reported result (1.5 marks):**

$$\boxed{N \;=\; (1050 \pm 458)\ \text{rpm} \quad \text{at}\ k=2\ (95\%\ \text{confidence})}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **operating principle of a stroboscope** with a neat sketch. Derive the relation $N_{\text{shaft}} = 60 f_s / n$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stroboscope is used to "freeze" a rotating shaft at flash frequencies of $f_s = 25$ Hz, $50$ Hz, and $100$ Hz for the *first*, *second*, and *third* stationary images respectively. If the strobe oscillator's standard uncertainty is $u_{f_s} = 0.2$ Hz, determine the **true shaft rpm and its expanded uncertainty** at $k = 2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Principle (3 marks):** A stroboscope produces flashes of light at a precisely adjustable frequency $f_s$. When the rotating object is illuminated by these flashes, the **persistence of vision** (≈ 1/16 s) causes the eye to integrate successive flashes. If the object rotates by an integer fraction $1/n$ of a full revolution between successive flashes, the eye perceives the object as **stationary**.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Let the shaft rotate at $f_r$ revolutions per second. The shaft advances by an angle

$$\theta \;=\; 2\pi f_r \, T_s$$

where $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the flash period. For the image to appear stationary, this advance must equal $2\pi / n$:

$$\frac{2\pi f_r}{f_s} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{n} \;\Longrightarrow\; f_r \;=\; \frac{f_s}{n}$$

Multiplying by 60 to convert rev/s → rpm:

$$\boxed{N_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; \frac{60\,f_s}{n},\quad n = 1,2,3,\dots}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the correct order $n$ (2 marks):**

The shaft freezes at three different flash frequencies. The shaft speed must be **the same** in each case. Test:

- $f_s = 25$ Hz, suppose $n = 1$: $N = 60 \times 25 / 1 = 1500$ rpm
- $f_s = 50$ Hz, suppose $n = 2$: $N = 60 \times 50 / 2 = 1500$ rpm ✓
- $f_s = 100$ Hz, suppose $n = 4$: $N = 60 \times 100 / 4 = 1500$ rpm ✓

**All three are consistent → $N_{\text{shaft}} = 1500$ rpm.** (1.5 marks)

**Step 2 — Uncertainty propagation (2 marks):**

$$u_N \;=\; \left|\frac{\partial N}{\partial f_s}\right| \, u_{f_s} \;=\; \frac{60}{n}\,u_{f_s}$$

Using $n = 1$ (fundamental):

$$u_N \;=\; \frac{60}{1} \times 0.2 \;=\; 12\ \text{rpm}$$

(If $n = 4$ had been used: $u_N = 15 \times 0.2 = 3$ rpm — even smaller. Report the conservative maximum.)

**Step 3 — Expanded uncertainty and final result (1.5 marks):**

$$U \;=\; k \cdot u_N \;=\; 2 \times 12 \;=\; 24\ \text{rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; (1500 \pm 24)\ \text{rpm} \quad (k=2,\ 95\%\ \text{confidence})}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing error with uncertainty** — Error is a single (often unknown) number; uncertainty is a *defensible range*. (−1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Forgetting the divisor $(n-1)$** in $s$. Use $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) for sample standard deviation, not $n$. (−2 marks.)
> 3. **Using $k = 1$ instead of $k = 2$** for 95% expanded uncertainty. Always state the value of $k$. (−1 mark.)
> 4. **Neglecting to convert units**: strobe frequency is in Hz but tachometer reading is in rpm — remember the factor of 60. (−1 mark.)
> 5. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ in $u_B = \Delta x/(2\sqrt{3})$**. Students often write $\Delta x/2$ — that is the *maximum* error bound, **not** the standard uncertainty. (−1 mark.)
> 6. **Reporting the calibration curve slope $a$ and intercept $b$** without stating units or explaining their physical meaning. Always state: "slope $a$ = sensitivity error (gain), intercept $b$ = zero-error (offset)." (−1 mark.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Tachometer** = direct rpm measuring instrument (contact or non-contact).
- ✅ **Stroboscope** = flashing light instrument; rpm inferred via $N = 60 f_s / n$.
- ✅ **Calibration** = comparison against a traceable reference; outputs a calibration curve $y = ax + b$.
- ✅ **Uncertainty** (GUM) has **Type A** (statistical, from repeats) and **Type B** (other knowledge — resolution, reference, drift).
- ✅ **Type A:** $u_A = s / \sqrt{n}$, where $s$ uses **Bessel's divisor $(n-1)$**.
- ✅ **Type B (resolution):** $u_B = \Delta x / (2\sqrt{3})$.
- ✅ **Combined:** $u_c = \sqrt{\sum u_i^2}$ — root-sum-square (RSS), **not** linear sum.
- ✅ **Expanded:** $U = k\,u_c$, with $k = 2$ giving **95% confidence**.
- ✅ **Result format:** $X = \bar{X} \pm U$ at stated coverage factor $k$.
- ✅ **Strobe order $n$:** $n = 1$ is the fundamental image; always verify consistency across multiple $f_s$.
- ✅ **Persistence of vision** (~1/16 s) is the human-physiological basis of the stroboscopic effect.
- ✅ **Synchronous motor check:** $n_s = 120f/P$ — cross-validate strobe readings against mains-driven motor theory.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** (rectangular distribution, independence of inputs, $k = 2$, etc.) in your report.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Temperature Using Thermocouple Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Temperature Using Thermocouple Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermocouple (Per KTU 2024 Scheme - Measurements Lab):**
> A **thermocouple** is a temperature-measuring transducer consisting of two dissimilar conductors joined at one end (the *measuring* or *hot junction*) and connected to a measuring instrument at the other end (the *reference* or *cold junction*). It generates an electromotive force (EMF) proportional to the temperature difference between the two junctions, governed by the **Seebeck Effect**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration of a Thermocouple (KTU Definition):**
> Calibration is the experimental procedure of establishing the functional relationship between the **EMF output (in millivolts)** of the thermocouple and the **actual temperature (in °C)** at the hot junction by comparing it against a traceable standard reference instrument (such as a Standard Platinum Resistance Thermometer – SPRT, or a Reference RTD PT-100) maintained at known temperature points in a **dry block calibrator** or **liquid bath**.

The output characteristic curve obtained is called the **Calibration Curve** and is plotted as **EMF (mV) vs Temperature (°C)**. The deviation between the measured and reference temperature at any point yields the **instrumental error**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have two long strips of **different metals** (say, copper and iron) riveted together at one end. You put the riveted end inside a furnace. The other end is connected to a sensitive **voltmeter** kept at room temperature.

Now, here's the magic:
- The *hot* end (in furnace) and *cold* end (in your hand) are at different temperatures.
- Because the two metals have different electron "mobility", electrons migrate from one metal to another — creating a small **voltage** (a few millivolts).
- This voltage **increases** as the temperature difference increases.

This is exactly how a **thermocouple** works! It is essentially a "thermometer that translates heat into voltage."

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Tower Pressure Analogy:**
> Think of the **hot junction** as water at the top of a tower (high temperature = high electron "pressure"). The **cold junction** is water at the bottom (low temperature = low electron "pressure"). The dissimilar metals are like two pipes of different diameters connecting top and bottom. Because the pipes differ, an unequal flow creates a measurable pressure difference — this is your **EMF output**, directly proportional to the temperature difference between top and bottom.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Reference Temperature for Cold Junction:** **0 °C (273.15 K)** — established using an **ice-water bath** (50% distilled water + 50% crushed ice).
- **Seebeck Coefficient Range:** Typically **10 µV/°C to 80 µV/°C** depending on thermocouple type.
- **Standard EMF Tables:** Referenced to the **ITS-90 (International Temperature Scale of 1990)** published by NIST.
- **Common Thermocouple Types used in KTU Labs:** **Type K (Chromel–Alumel)** and **Type T (Copper–Constantan)** due to wide availability and good linearity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — The Seebeck Effect

The **Seebeck Effect** (discovered by **Thomas Johann Seebeck** in **1821**) states that when two dissimilar conductors (A and B) form a closed loop and their junctions are maintained at different temperatures, an EMF is induced, and a current flows in the loop.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Seebeck Effect

1. **Electron Density Difference:** Different metals have different free-electron densities at a given temperature.
2. **Temperature Gradient:** When one junction is heated, electrons in the hotter metal gain kinetic energy and diffuse toward the cooler end.
3. **Net Diffusion:** Because metals A and B have different electron energies, the net flow of electrons is **unequal** in the two wires.
4. **EMF Generation:** This unbalanced diffusion produces a **net contact potential** at each junction, with the algebraic sum giving the measurable **Seebeck EMF** $E_{AB}$.
5. **Measurable Quantity:** The total EMF is a function of the *temperature difference* between the hot and cold junctions, **not** the absolute temperature distribution along the wires.

## 2.2 Three Fundamental Laws of Thermocouples (Must-Know for KTU)

### Law 1 — Law of Homogeneous Circuit
> The EMF generated depends **only on the materials** of the two conductors and the **temperatures of the two junctions**. It is **independent of the temperature distribution along the wires** or the cross-section of the conductors.

*Practical Implication:* A thermocouple can be extended using lead wires of the same material without introducing error.

### Law 2 — Law of Intermediate Metals
> If a third metal C is introduced anywhere in the circuit, **no net EMF is generated** provided the two junctions formed with C are at the **same temperature**.

*Practical Implication:* This allows insertion of a **voltmeter, switch, or connector** (typically copper) into the thermocouple circuit without compensation, as long as its terminals are isothermal.

### Law 3 — Law of Intermediate (Successive) Temperatures
> If a thermocouple produces EMF $E_1$ between temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, and EMF $E_2$ between $T_2$ and $T_3$, then the EMF between $T_1$ and $T_3$ is:
$$E_{T_1 \to T_3} \;=\; E_{T_1 \to T_2} \;+\; E_{T_2 \to T_3}$$

*Practical Implication:* **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** is mathematically valid.

## 2.3 Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) — The Heart of Calibration

In an ideal thermocouple circuit, the cold junction is held at **0 °C** using an ice bath (mixture of pure water and crushed ice in a Dewar flask). However, in modern calibrators and industrial setups, an **electronic CJC** is used.

The compensation formula (derived from Law 3):

$$E_{\text{meas}}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{ref}}) \;=\; E_{\text{meas}}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{cold}}) \;-\; E_{\text{table}}(T_{\text{cold}}, 0\,^\circ\text{C})$$

Where:
- $E_{\text{meas}}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{ref}})$ = Corrected EMF referred to a 0 °C reference
- $E_{\text{meas}}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{cold}})$ = Actual measured EMF (cold junction not at 0 °C)
- $E_{\text{table}}(T_{\text{cold}}, 0\,^\circ\text{C})$ = EMF from standard ITS-90 reference tables for the cold junction temperature

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use `\vert` instead of `\mid` or `|` inside LaTeX within tables to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seebeck EMF (Integral) | $E_{AB} \;=\; \displaystyle\int_{T_{\text{cold}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} S_{AB}(T)\,dT$ | $S_{AB}$ = Seebeck coefficient (µV/°C); $T$ in K or °C | Fundamental basis of thermocouple |
| 2 | Differential Form | $dE_{AB} \;=\; S_{AB}(T)\,dT$ | Same as above | Instantaneous sensitivity |
| 3 | Cold Junction Compensation | $E_{\text{corr}} \;=\; E_{\text{meas}} \;-\; E_{\text{cold}}$ | All in mV | Corrects EMF to 0 °C reference |
| 4 | Temperature from EMF (NIST polynomial) | $T \;=\; \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n} c_i \,(E)^i$ | $c_i$ = polynomial coefficients (from NIST ITS-90) | Used in digital thermocouple readers |
| 5 | Absolute Static Error | $e_a \;=\; T_{\text{ind}} \;-\; T_{\text{ref}}$ | All in °C | Indicates deviation of thermocouple |
| 6 | Percentage Error (Full-Scale) | $\%e \;=\; \dfrac{\vert e_a \vert}{T_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | $T_{\text{FS}}$ = full-scale temperature | Standard KTU error metric |
| 7 | Sensitivity (for Type K) | $S \approx 41\,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ | Constant in mid-range (0–1000 °C) | Used for slope verification |
| 8 | ITS-90 Reference EMF (Type K) | $E(100\,^\circ\text{C}) = 4.096\,\text{mV}$ | Standard reference value | Most commonly tested point |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Power Plants:** Monitoring steam temperature in boilers (Type K, up to 1260 °C).
- **Aerospace:** Surface temperature of turbine blades (Type R/S, up to 1480 °C).
- **Automotive:** Exhaust gas temperature (EGT) sensing (Type K).
- **Pharmaceuticals & Food:** Pasteurization validation (Type T, –200 to 350 °C).
- **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Low-temperature monitoring (Type T).
- **Metallurgical Furnaces:** High-temperature batch process control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Seebeck EMF from Basic Thermoelectric Theory

Consider two dissimilar metals A and B joined at junction $J_1$ (hot) at temperature $T_{\text{hot}}$, with the open terminals at $J_2$ and $J_3$ (cold) both at temperature $T_{\text{cold}}$.

### Step 1 — Define the Thomson Effect
Each conductor individually develops an EMF due to the Thomson effect:

$$dE_{A} \;=\; \sigma_{A}(T)\,dT \quad\text{and}\quad dE_{B} \;=\; \sigma_{B}(T)\,dT$$

where $\sigma_A, \sigma_B$ are Thomson coefficients of metals A and B.

### Step 2 — Define Peltier Contribution at Each Junction
At the hot junction $J_1$, a Peltier EMF is generated:

$$dE_{P} \;=\; \pi_{AB}(T_{\text{hot}})\,dT$$

### Step 3 — Combine to Get Total Seebeck EMF
By the **Principle of Superposition** applied to thermoelectric effects:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{AB}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{cold}}) 
&= \int_{T_{\text{cold}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} \bigl[\sigma_A(T) - \sigma_B(T)\bigr]\,dT \;+\; \bigl[\pi_{AB}(T_{\text{hot}}) - \pi_{AB}(T_{\text{cold}})\bigr] \\[6pt]
&= \int_{T_{\text{cold}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} S_{AB}(T)\,dT
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Thomson and Peltier terms combine to yield the **Seebeck coefficient** $S_{AB}(T) = \dfrac{dE_{AB}}{dT}$, which is material-dependent.

### Step 4 — Simplification for Limited Range
For **narrow temperature spans** (typical in KTU lab experiments, e.g., 50–250 °C), $S_{AB}(T)$ is approximately constant, giving the **linear approximation**:

$$E_{AB} \;\approx\; S_{AB} \cdot (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$

This linear form is **essential** for KTU lab calculation purposes and quick verification.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cold Junction Compensation Formula

Let:
- $T_h$ = Hot junction temperature (unknown we want to measure)
- $T_c$ = Cold junction temperature (measured by auxiliary sensor, e.g., RTD)
- $T_{\text{ref}} = 0\,^\circ\text{C}$ (ice bath reference)

### Step 1 — Write Two Measured EMF Equations

From Law of Intermediate Temperatures, if we measure the EMF between the hot junction ($T_h$) and the actual cold junction ($T_c$):

$$E(T_h, T_c) \;=\; E(T_h, T_{\text{ref}}) \;-\; E(T_c, T_{\text{ref}})$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Referenced EMF

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(T_h, T_{\text{ref}}) 
&= E(T_h, T_c) \;+\; E(T_c, T_{\text{ref}})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Final Calibration Equation

$$
\boxed{\,E_{\text{corrected}} \;=\; E_{\text{measured}} \;+\; E_{\text{table}}(T_c)\,}
$$

where $E_{\text{table}}(T_c)$ is the EMF value obtained from **standard ITS-90 reference tables** for the thermocouple type at the cold junction temperature $T_c$.

**Conversion Logic:** This corrected EMF is then converted back to temperature using the same NIST polynomial tables, giving the true hot junction temperature $T_h$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Calibration Error & Best-Fit Line (Linear Regression)

When the calibration is performed at $n$ discrete temperature points, the data may not perfectly fit a straight line due to material non-linearity. We use the **Least Squares Method** to obtain the best-fit calibration line:

$$T_{\text{calc}} \;=\; a \cdot E \;+\; b$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a \;=\; \frac{n\,\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(E_i T_i) \;-\; \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \cdot \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n\,\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i^2 \;-\; \Bigl(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i\Bigr)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i \;-\; a\,\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- $a$ = slope (°C/mV) — physically corresponds to the reciprocal of the average Seebeck coefficient $\dfrac{1}{S_{AB}}$.
- $b$ = intercept (°C) — represents the offset/zero error of the thermocouple system.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Format)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Data Set (Type K Thermocouple, Cold Junction in Ice Bath at 0 °C):**

| Sl. No. | Reference Temp $T_{\text{ref}}$ (°C) | Measured EMF $E$ (mV) | Std. EMF from Tables $E_{\text{std}}$ (mV) | Error $e = T_{\text{ind}} - T_{\text{ref}}$ (°C) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 50.0 | 2.022 | 2.036 | –0.5 |
| 2 | 100.0 | 4.096 | 4.096 | 0.0 |
| 3 | 150.0 | 6.138 | 6.138 | 0.0 |
| 4 | 200.0 | 8.138 | 8.137 | +0.1 |
| 5 | 250.0 | 10.153 | 10.151 | +0.2 |

### Step 1 — Calculate Indicated Temperature
For each row, convert measured EMF to temperature using the **inverse ITS-90 polynomial** for Type K. Example for row 1:

$$
T_{\text{ind}} \;=\; f_{K}^{-1}(2.022\,\text{mV}) \;\approx\; 49.5\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Absolute Error
$$
e_{a,1} \;=\; T_{\text{ind}} - T_{\text{ref}} \;=\; 49.5 - 50.0 \;=\; -0.5\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Percentage Error (Full-Scale, FS = 300 °C)
$$
\%e_1 \;=\; \frac{\vert -0.5 \vert}{300} \times 100 \;=\; 0.1667\%
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Average Error & Repeatability
$$
\bar{e}_a \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_{a,i} \;=\; \frac{(-0.5 + 0 + 0 + 0.1 + 0.2)}{5} \;=\; -0.04\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\sigma_e \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(e_{a,i} - \bar{e}_a)^2} \;\approx\; 0.27\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

This **standard deviation** $\sigma_e$ represents the **repeatability** of the thermocouple system — a key KTU lab evaluation metric.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the actual physical setup involves laboratory instruments, fluid baths, and wiring — which cannot be drawn using Mermaid graph syntax — a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** is provided to map all signal interactions, cold-junction compensation logic, and calibration feedback path.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Thermocouple Calibration Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["HOT JUNCTION - Measuring Tip Inside Dry Block Calibrator or Liquid Bath"] --> node2["THERMOCOUPLE WIRES - Type K or Type T Dissimilar Metal Pair"]
    node2 --> node3["COLD JUNCTION - Ice Bath at 0 Degree Celsius OR Electronic CJC Sensor"]
    node3 --> node4["CJC CORRECTION BLOCK - Software or Hardware Compensation Adds Table EMF"]
    node4 --> node5["DIGITAL NANOVOLTMETER - Reads Corrected EMF in millivolts"]
    node5 --> node6["DATA ACQUISITION - Stores EMF Versus Temperature Readings"]
    node6 --> node7["CALIBRATION CURVE GENERATOR - Plots EMF Versus Temperature Graph"]
    node7 --> node8["ERROR ANALYSIS BLOCK - Computes Absolute Error Standard Deviation and Percentage Error"]
    node8 --> node9["CALIBRATION REPORT - Final Best Fit Equation and Uncertainty Statement"]

    node10["REFERENCE STANDARD - SPRT or PT100 RTD Inside Same Bath"] --> node11["REFERENCE TEMPERATURE READOUT - High Precision Digital Indicator"]
    node11 --> node8

    node12["TEMPERATURE CONTROLLER - Sets and Stabilizes Bath to Target Setpoint"] --> node1

    style node1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node10 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow Explanation (Block-by-Block)

| Block | Function | Key Specification |
|---|---|---|
| Hot Junction | Senses true process temperature | Insertion depth ≥ 15 × wire diameter |
| Thermocouple Wires | Two dissimilar metals (e.g., Chromel–Alumel for Type K) | ANSI color-coded: Yellow (+) / Red (–) for Type K |
| Cold Junction | Reference temperature; ideally 0 °C | Ice bath or electronic CJC sensor (e.g., LM35) |
| CJC Correction Block | Adds standard EMF corresponding to $T_c$ | Polynomial from NIST ITS-90 tables |
| Digital Nanovoltmeter | Measures µV-range EMF with high resolution | Resolution ≤ 0.1 µV, input impedance ≥ 10 MΩ |
| Reference Standard | Provides traceable "true" temperature | SPRT or PT-100 with calibration certificate |
| Temperature Controller | Stabilizes bath at each test point | Stability ≤ ±0.1 °C |

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Diagram (Text-Based Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["T_HOT - Hot Junction at T_hot"] --- nodeB["METAL A - Chromel Wire"]
    nodeB --- nodeC["TERMINAL BLOCK - Isothermal at T_cold"]
    nodeC --- nodeD["METAL B - Alumel Wire"]
    nodeD --- nodeA

    nodeC --- nodeE["VOLTMETER - High Impedance Microvoltmeter"]
    nodeC --- nodeF["ICE BATH or CJC SENSOR - Reference at 0 Degree Celsius"]

    style nodeA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style nodeF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Operational Note:**
- The voltmeter terminals are physically connected at the **terminal block**, which is the cold junction.
- Because voltmeter leads are typically **copper**, the Law of Intermediate Metals guarantees no extra EMF is added **as long as both voltmeter terminals are at the same temperature**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define the Seebeck Effect. Mention the three laws of thermocouples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1):**

**Seebeck Effect (1 Mark):** When two dissimilar metals are joined to form a closed loop and the two junctions are maintained at different temperatures, an EMF is induced in the circuit and a current flows. This phenomenon, discovered by Thomas Seebeck in 1821, is the fundamental principle behind thermocouple temperature measurement.

**Three Laws (2 Marks — 1 Mark for naming, 1 Mark for brief statement):**
1. **Law of Homogeneous Circuit:** EMF depends only on the materials and junction temperatures, not on the wire distribution.
2. **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal anywhere in the circuit introduces no net EMF if its two junctions are at the same temperature.
3. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** EMF from $T_1$ to $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs from $T_1 \to T_2$ and $T_2 \to T_3$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**What is cold junction compensation? Why is an ice bath used as the reference standard?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Cold Junction Compensation (2 Marks):** Thermocouples inherently measure the **temperature difference** between two junctions, not absolute temperature. To obtain absolute temperature, the cold (reference) junction must be held at a precisely known temperature — typically **0 °C** — and the corresponding EMF from standard reference tables is algebraically added to the measured EMF. This process is called **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)**.

**Ice Bath Reference (1 Mark):** A mixture of **50% crushed ice and 50% distilled water** in an insulated Dewar flask maintains a highly stable, reproducible temperature of **0.0 °C ± 0.1 °C**, providing a simple, accurate, and cost-free primary reference standard as per ITS-90.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulations**, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **Module Internal Choice**. Two alternatives are provided below — students may answer either one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Model Paper Style**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the procedure for **calibrating a Type K thermocouple** using a dry block calibrator and a reference PT-100 RTD. List the apparatus required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following readings were obtained during calibration of a Type K thermocouple (cold junction at 0 °C):

| Trial | $T_{\text{ref}}$ (°C) | EMF Measured (mV) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 60 | 2.436 |
| 2 | 110 | 4.509 |
| 3 | 160 | 6.540 |
| 4 | 210 | 8.539 |
| 5 | 260 | 10.555 |

Plot the calibration curve and determine: (i) the EMF at 150 °C, (ii) the slope and intercept of the best-fit line, (iii) the maximum static error observed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Apparatus Required (2 Marks):**
1. Type K thermocouple (test specimen)
2. Dry block calibrator with temperature controller
3. Reference PT-100 RTD with digital indicator (4-wire)
4. Digital microvoltmeter (resolution ≤ 1 µV)
5. Ice bath + Dewar flask (for cold junction at 0 °C)
6. Connecting wires (compensating cables)
7. NIST ITS-90 reference EMF tables for Type K

**Procedure (5 Marks — Step-wise):**

1. **Setup:** Insert the test thermocouple and the reference RTD into adjacent holes of the dry block calibrator to ensure they are at the **same temperature** (isothermal condition).
2. **Cold Junction:** Place the thermocouple's reference end in an **ice bath** maintained at 0 °C. Ensure the ice-bath mixture is well-stirred.
3. **Initial Reading:** Set the dry block to the **lowest test temperature** (e.g., 50 °C) and allow **thermal stabilization** (typically 10–15 minutes for stability ≤ ±0.1 °C).
4. **Recording:** Note (i) the RTD-indicated reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$ and (ii) the thermocouple EMF in mV.
5. **Increment:** Increase the setpoint to the next calibration point (e.g., 100 °C, 150 °C …). Repeat step 4 after stabilization.
6. **Reverse Run:** For accuracy, repeat readings while **decreasing** temperature (hysteresis check).
7. **Repeatability:** Repeat the entire cycle **2–3 times** for statistical averaging.
8. **Plot:** Draw the calibration curve (EMF vs $T_{\text{ref}}$) and compute errors.

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 — Reference it).**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Tabulate Converted Temperature from EMF (Using ITS-90 Table for Type K):**

For each EMF, look up the corresponding temperature $T_{\text{ind}}$:

| Trial | $T_{\text{ref}}$ (°C) | EMF (mV) | $T_{\text{ind}}$ (°C) | Error $e_a$ (°C) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 60 | 2.436 | 59.5 | –0.5 |
| 2 | 110 | 4.509 | 110.0 | 0.0 |
| 3 | 160 | 6.540 | 160.1 | +0.1 |
| 4 | 210 | 8.539 | 210.2 | +0.2 |
| 5 | 260 | 10.555 | 260.3 | +0.3 |

**Step 2 — Calculate Best-Fit Slope and Intercept (1 Mark):**

Using the linear regression formulas from Section 3.3:

Let $E_i$ = EMF (mV), $T_i$ = $T_{\text{ref}}$ (°C).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum E_i &= 32.579,\quad \sum T_i = 800,\quad n = 5 \\[4pt]
\sum E_i T_i &= 5645.42,\quad \sum E_i^2 = 225.249
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
a \;=\; \frac{5(5645.42) - (32.579)(800)}{5(225.249) - (32.579)^2} \;=\; \frac{28227.10 - 26063.20}{1126.245 - 1061.47} \;=\; \frac{2163.90}{64.78} \;\approx\; 33.41\,^\circ\text{C/mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b \;=\; \frac{800 - (33.41)(32.579)}{5} \;=\; \frac{800 - 1088.55}{5} \;=\; \frac{-288.55}{5} \;\approx\; -57.71\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Best-Fit Equation (1 Mark):**

$$T_{\text{calc}} \;=\; 33.41\,E \;-\; 57.71$$

**Step 3 — EMF at 150 °C (2 Marks):**

Setting $T_{\text{calc}} = 150$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
150 \;=\; 33.41\,E - 57.71 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E \;=\; \frac{150 + 57.71}{33.41} \;\approx\; 6.219\,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Static Error (2 Marks):**

From the table: $e_{a,\max} = +0.3\,^\circ\text{C}$ (at $T_{\text{ref}} = 260\,^\circ\text{C}$).

Percentage error (assuming FS = 300 °C):

$$
\%e_{\max} \;=\; \frac{0.3}{300} \times 100 \;=\; 0.10\%
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **three laws of thermocouples** in detail with suitable diagrams. How does each law justify the practical use of lead wires and connectors in industrial thermocouple installations? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Type T thermocouple with cold junction at 25 °C produces a measured EMF of **4.740 mV**. Using the ITS-90 reference tables for Type T, determine the **true hot junction temperature**, given that the standard EMF of Type T at 25 °C (vs 0 °C reference) is **0.992 mV**. Also calculate the Seebeck coefficient if the EMF becomes **9.612 mV** at the next measurement point where $T_h$ is unknown but the bath is at 110 °C. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Law 1 — Homogeneous Circuit (2 Marks):**
- *Statement:* The EMF in a thermocouple circuit depends **only** on the materials of the two conductors and the temperatures of the two junctions.
- *Diagram implication:* Any length of thermocouple wire added between the junction and the measuring instrument does not change the EMF, provided both ends of that wire are at the same temperature.
- *Practical use:* Allows **extension lead wires** of the same thermocouple grade to be run over long distances from the field sensor to the control room.

**Law 2 — Intermediate Metals (3 Marks):**
- *Statement:* Introducing a third metal C into the circuit introduces no net EMF if its two new junctions are at the same temperature.
- *Derivation check:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMF(A–B at }T_1\text{, A–C at }T_2\text{, C–B at }T_2\text{)} = E_{AB}(T_1) - E_{AC}(T_2) + E_{CB}(T_2)
\end{aligned}
$$
- Since $E_{CB} = -E_{BC}$ and using property $E_{AB} = E_{AC} - E_{BC}$, the terms cancel if $T_2$ is uniform.
- *Practical use:* Permits insertion of **copper terminal blocks, switches, and voltmeters** in the thermocouple loop without introducing errors.

**Law 3 — Intermediate Temperatures (2 Marks):**
- *Statement:* $E_{T_1 \to T_3} = E_{T_1 \to T_2} + E_{T_2 \to T_3}$
- *Practical use:* Forms the theoretical foundation of **Cold Junction Compensation** using software lookup tables.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Measured EMF: $E_{\text{meas}} = 4.740$ mV (cold junction at 25 °C, not at 0 °C)
- Standard EMF of Type T at 25 °C (vs 0 °C ref): $E_{\text{table}}(25\,^\circ\text{C}) = 0.992$ mV

**Step 1 — Apply Cold Junction Compensation (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{corrected}} 
&= E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{table}}(T_c) \\[4pt]
&= 4.740 + 0.992 \\[4pt]
&= 5.732\,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Corrected EMF to Hot Junction Temperature (2 Marks):**

From ITS-90 reference tables for Type T thermocouple, an EMF of 5.732 mV corresponds to approximately:

$$T_h \;\approx\; 138.0\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Seebeck Coefficient (2 Marks):**

At the second measurement point, $E_2 = 9.612$ mV and $T_{h,2}$ is unknown. The corresponding temperature from tables is approximately:

$$T_{h,2} \;\approx\; 220.0\,^\circ\text{C}$$

The Seebeck coefficient at this point (using the previous reference $T_{h,1} = 138.0\,^\circ\text{C}, E_1 = 5.732$ mV):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AB} \;\approx\; \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta T} \;=\; \frac{9.612 - 5.732}{220.0 - 138.0} \;=\; \frac{3.880}{82.0} \;\approx\; 47.3\,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with the **nominal Seebeck coefficient of Type T thermocouple (~40–60 µV/°C in the 0–200 °C range)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Cold Junction Compensation (–2 to –3 Marks):** Many students directly convert measured EMF to temperature without correcting for the cold junction temperature. This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU evaluations.
>
> 2. **Wrong Sign Convention for Error (–1 Mark):** The error is always calculated as $e = T_{\text{indicated}} - T_{\text{reference}}$. Some students incorrectly compute $T_{\text{ref}} - T_{\text{ind}}$.
>
> 3. **Using Fahrenheit Tables Instead of ITS-90 (–2 Marks):** Always use **ITS-90 Celsius-based** tables. Old IPTS-68 tables are obsolete.
>
> 4. **Not Mentioning Stabilization Time (–1 Mark):** In the procedure, you must mention allowing the bath to **stabilize for 10–15 minutes** at each setpoint.
>
> 5. **Plotting Without Units on Axes (–1 Mark):** The calibration curve axes must be labeled clearly: x-axis = "EMF (mV)", y-axis = "Temperature (°C)" with units.
>
> 6. **Confusing Type K and Type J Color Codes (–1 Mark):** Type K = **Yellow outer + Red negative** (USA); Type J = **Black outer + White negative**.
>
> 7. **Ignoring Hysteresis (–1 Mark):** Best calibration practice includes both **increasing and decreasing temperature runs** to detect hysteresis.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Calibration of Temperature Using Thermocouple**

- **Principle:** Seebeck Effect — EMF generated due to temperature difference between two dissimilar metal junctions.
- **Seebeck EMF (General):** $E_{AB} = \displaystyle\int_{T_c}^{T_h} S_{AB}(T)\,dT$
- **Three Laws:** Homogeneous Circuit, Intermediate Metals, Intermediate Temperatures — **must be quoted verbatim** in KTU exams.
- **Cold Junction Compensation Formula:** $E_{\text{corr}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{table}}(T_c)$
- **Standard Reference Cold Junction:** **0 °C (ice bath)** — 50% ice + 50% distilled water in Dewar flask.
- **Typical Sensitivity:** Type K ≈ **41 µV/°C**, Type T ≈ **40–60 µV/°C**, Type R/S ≈ **10 µV/°C**.
- **Calibration Curve:** Plot of **EMF (mV) on x-axis** vs **Temperature (°C) on y-axis**.
- **Error Metrics:** Absolute error $e_a = T_{\text{ind}} - T_{\text{ref}}$; Percentage error $\%e = \dfrac{\vert e_a \vert}{T_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$.
- **Standard Reference Instrument:** **PT-100 RTD** or **SPRT** (Standard Platinum Resistance Thermometer).
- **Reference Tables:** **NIST ITS-90** polynomial tables — mention this explicitly in viva.
- **Apparatus in KTU Lab:** Dry block calibrator OR liquid bath, ice bath, microvoltmeter, Type K or Type T thermocouple, PT-100 RTD, connecting wires.
- **Stabilization Time:** 10–15 minutes per setpoint for **±0.1 °C** stability.
- **Hysteresis Check:** Always perform at least one **ascending and one descending** run.
- **Best-Fit Equation:** $T_{\text{calc}} = aE + b$ obtained via least-squares regression.
- **Repeatability:** Quantified by **standard deviation** $\sigma_e$ across multiple trials.
- **Color Code for Type K (USA ANSI):** **Yellow (positive, Chromel)** and **Red (negative, Alumel)**.
- **Safety Note:** Never exceed thermocouple's maximum rated temperature (e.g., Type K: **1260 °C**) to avoid **drift, decalibration, or wire oxidation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiments on Limit and Fits
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments on Limit and Fits

# Experiments on Limit and Fits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Metrology, **Limits and Fits** refer to the standardized system (governed by **IS 919:1993 / ISO 286-1:2010**) used in engineering to define the permissible variations in the dimensions of mating parts. A **limit** is the extreme permissible size of a part (maximum or minimum), and a **fit** is the degree of tightness or looseness between two mating parts — typically a **hole** and a **shaft**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCNSL408):** The experiment aims to determine the limits, tolerances, deviations, and type of fit for given nominal dimensions using standard reference charts (IS 919), and to verify whether a given pair of hole-shaft dimensions falls under clearance, interference, or transition fit categories.

The fundamental terminology is as follows:

- **Nominal Size (d or D):** The design size used to identify the part (e.g., **50 mm**).
- **Actual Size:** The measured size obtained during inspection.
- **Maximum Limit (Upper Limit):** The largest permissible size of the part.
- **Minimum Limit (Lower Limit):** The smallest permissible size of the part.
- **Tolerance (T):** The difference between the maximum and minimum limits, indicating the permissible variation.
- **Deviation:** The algebraic difference between a limit and the corresponding nominal size.
- **Allowance (A):** The intentional difference between the maximum material condition of mating parts (minimum clearance or maximum interference).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Plug and Socket":**
> Imagine inserting a **3-pin electrical plug** into a wall **socket**. 
> - If the plug is *too tight*, you need force — this is an **interference fit** (no relative motion). 
> - If the plug *slides loosely*, contact becomes unreliable — this is a **clearance fit** (free motion). 
> - If the plug fits *snugly with a slight push*, you have a **transition fit** (precise location, no free play).
>
> **Limits and Fits is the engineering language that mathematically controls this snugness, ensuring every plug fits every socket, anywhere in the world, in any factory.**

The **hole** is the internal feature (always the containing member), and the **shaft** is the external feature (always the contained member). This nomenclature is fixed regardless of the actual physical shape — even a square peg in a square hole is described as "shaft in hole."

### The Two Standard Systems

| System | Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hole Basis System** | The **hole's minimum size** is fixed at the basic size (Lower Deviation = 0). Tolerances are applied to the shaft to obtain various fits. | Widely used in engineering (preferred by ISO/KTU). Cost-effective since standard reamers/broaches are used. |
| **Shaft Basis System** | The **shaft's maximum size** is fixed at the basic size (Upper Deviation = 0). Tolerances are applied to the hole to obtain various fits. | Used when a standard shaft of exact dimension is required (e.g., textile spindles, machine tool arbors). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Reminder:** In **Hole Basis**, the hole letter is always 'H' (Lower Deviation = 0). In **Shaft Basis**, the shaft letter is always 'h' (Upper Deviation = 0).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Limiting Dimensions

For any manufactured part of nominal size **d**, two extreme permissible dimensions are defined:

- **Maximum Limit (L_max):** The largest acceptable size.
- **Minimum Limit (L_min):** The smallest acceptable size.

The **tolerance** is the total permissible variation:

$$T = L_{max} - L_{min}$$

This is the **number of standard units (i)** for that tolerance grade.

### 2.2 Fundamental Deviation (Single-Letter Reference)

In the ISO system, each hole or shaft is identified by a **deviation letter** (uppercase for hole: A, B, C...ZC; lowercase for shaft: a, b, c...zc) followed by a **tolerance grade number** (IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2...IT18).

- For **holes H to ZC**: Fundamental deviation = **Lower Deviation (EI) = 0**
- For **shafts h to zc**: Fundamental deviation = **Upper Deviation (es) = 0**
- For deviations a–g (shafts) and A–G (holes): Fundamental deviation is on the **upper side** (negative for shafts, positive for holes).
- For deviations k–zc (shafts) and K–ZC (holes): Fundamental deviation is on the **lower side**.

### 2.3 Calculation of Limits (Hole Basis — Most Common)

For a **hole 'H'** of nominal size **d**, tolerance grade **IT_n**:

$$L_{min}^{hole} = d \quad \text{(since Lower Deviation EI = 0)}$$

$$L_{max}^{hole} = d + T_H = d + i \cdot n$$

For a **shaft** with deviation letter and grade:

$$L_{max}^{shaft} = d + es = d + \text{(Fundamental Deviation of shaft)}$$

$$L_{min}^{shaft} = L_{max}^{shaft} - T_s = d + es - i \cdot n$$

### 2.4 Calculation of Allowance and Fit Type

The **maximum clearance** (or minimum interference) is the difference between the largest hole and smallest shaft:

$$\text{Maximum Clearance} = L_{max}^{hole} - L_{min}^{shaft}$$

The **minimum clearance** (or maximum interference) is:

$$\text{Minimum Clearance} = L_{min}^{hole} - L_{max}^{shaft}$$

- If both values are **positive** → **Clearance Fit**
- If both values are **negative** → **Interference Fit**
- If one is positive and one is negative → **Transition Fit**

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tolerance (T) | $T = L_{max} - L_{min}$ | mm |
| 2 | Upper Deviation (Shaft) | $es = L_{max}^{shaft} - d$ | mm |
| 3 | Lower Deviation (Shaft) | $ei = L_{min}^{shaft} - d$ | mm |
| 4 | Upper Deviation (Hole) | $ES = L_{max}^{hole} - d$ | mm |
| 5 | Lower Deviation (Hole) | $EI = L_{min}^{hole} - d$ | mm |
| 6 | Allowance / Fit (Hole Basis) | $A = EI^{hole} - es^{shaft}$ | mm |
| 7 | Maximum Clearance | $C_{max} = L_{max}^{hole} - L_{min}^{shaft}$ | mm |
| 8 | Minimum Clearance | $C_{min} = L_{min}^{hole} - L_{max}^{shaft}$ | mm |
| 9 | Tolerance Unit (i) | $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{d} + 0.001 \cdot d$ | μm |
| 10 | Shaft Limit | $L_{max}^{shaft} = d + es$ | mm |
| 11 | Shaft Limit | $L_{min}^{shaft} = L_{max}^{shaft} - T_s$ | mm |
| 12 | Hole Limit (H-basis) | $L_{min}^{hole} = d$ | mm |
| 13 | Hole Limit (H-basis) | $L_{max}^{hole} = d + T_H$ | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Limits and Fits system is the backbone of **interchangeable manufacturing**. It allows mass production — any part conforming to a specified limit can be assembled with any mating part, regardless of the factory of origin. This is critical in the automotive, aerospace, and heavy machinery industries that Kerala engineering graduates serve through KTU-affiliated industries (HMT, BrahMos, KEL, VSSC, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem Statement:**
In a **Hole Basis System**, determine the limits, tolerances, deviations, and type of fit for a mating pair where:
- Nominal size **d = 50 mm**
- Hole tolerance grade: **H8**
- Shaft deviation letter and grade: **g7**

**Step 1: Identify the Tolerance Unit (i) for d = 50 mm**

For the size range 30 < d ≤ 50 mm, the standard formula applies:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{d} + 0.001 \cdot d \quad (\text{in μm})$$

Substituting d = 50:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50$$

Computing the cube root:

$$\sqrt[3]{50} \approx 3.6840$$

Therefore:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot 3.6840 + 0.001 \cdot 50$$

$$i = 1.6578 + 0.0500$$

$$i = 1.7078 \quad \mu m$$

**Step 2: Find Hole H8 Limits**

For Hole H8:
- Lower Deviation EI = 0
- Tolerance grade 8 multiplier: From IS 919 tables, for the 30–50 mm range, **T_H8 = 39 μm** (this is 8 × i ≈ 8 × 1.7078 ≈ 13.66 μm for the 18–30 range, but the 30–50 range gives a fixed value of 39 μm per IS 919).

To use the **i-formula directly** for grade 8 (in the 30–50 mm range), we apply:

$$T_{H8} = 25 \cdot i = 25 \cdot 1.7078 = 42.695 \quad \mu m$$

For exam simplicity (and per IS 919 tables), use the **tabulated value**: $T_{H8} = 39 \quad \mu m = 0.039 \quad mm$

Now compute hole limits:

$$L_{min}^{hole} = d + EI = 50 + 0 = 50.000 \quad mm$$

$$L_{max}^{hole} = L_{min}^{hole} + T_{H8} = 50.000 + 0.039 = 50.039 \quad mm$$

**Step 3: Find Shaft g7 Limits**

For Shaft g7 (size 30–50 mm, grade 7):
- Fundamental deviation 'g' → Upper Deviation (es) is **negative**. From IS 919 table for 30–50 mm range: **es_g = -12 μm = -0.012 mm**
- Tolerance grade 7: $T_{g7} = 16 \cdot i$ (per IS 919 standard). For the 30–50 mm range, tabulated value = **25 μm = 0.025 mm**

Computing shaft limits:

$$L_{max}^{shaft} = d + es = 50 + (-0.012) = 49.988 \quad mm$$

$$L_{min}^{shaft} = L_{max}^{shaft} - T_{g7} = 49.988 - 0.025 = 49.963 \quad mm$$

**Step 4: Determine Type of Fit**

Maximum Clearance:

$$C_{max} = L_{max}^{hole} - L_{min}^{shaft} = 50.039 - 49.963 = +0.076 \quad mm$$

Minimum Clearance:

$$C_{min} = L_{min}^{hole} - L_{max}^{shaft} = 50.000 - 49.988 = +0.012 \quad mm$$

Since **both clearances are positive**, the fit is a **CLEARANCE FIT** — specifically, this is a **sliding fit** (H8/g7 is the standard designation for a sliding fit).

**Step 5: Summary Table**

| Parameter | Hole (H8) | Shaft (g7) |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental Deviation | EI = 0 | es = -0.012 mm |
| Tolerance | T = 0.039 mm | T = 0.025 mm |
| Max Limit | 50.039 mm | 49.988 mm |
| Min Limit | 50.000 mm | 49.963 mm |

**Fit Type: Sliding Clearance Fit**

> [!NOTE]
> **Tabular Convention:** Always present the final answer in a structured table (as above) — KTU examiners explicitly reward clarity in the final steps. A neat summary table often fetches **2 extra marks** even if intermediate steps are partially correct.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Recognition of Standard Fits

| Fit Symbol (Hole/Shaft) | Nature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| H11/c11 | Loose Running Fit | Agricultural machinery, hinge pins |
| H9/d9 | Free Running Fit | Bearing sleeves, low-speed pulleys |
| H8/f7 | Easy Sliding Fit | Tool shanks, detachable levers |
| **H8/g7** | **Sliding Fit** | **Sliding gears, clutches, machine tool parts** |
| H7/h6 | Locational Clearance | Locating pins, jigs, fixtures |
| H7/k6 | Locational Transition | Couplings, gear hubs |
| H7/n6 | Locational Transition (tight) | Bearing housings, flywheels |
| H7/p6 | Locational Interference (light) | Bushings under light loads |
| H7/s6 | Medium Force Fit | Permanent couplings, rocker arms |
| H7/u8 | Force Fit | Heavy wheels on shafts, railway axle boxes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic Representation of Tolerance Zones (H8/g7 Fit)

The following Mermaid block represents the spatial relationship between the tolerance zones of the hole and shaft, showing the **sliding clearance fit** derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisA[Basic Size Line d = 50.000 mm]:::baseLine
    holeMin[Hole Minimum Limit = 50.000 mm]:::holeZone
    holeMax[Hole Maximum Limit = 50.039 mm]:::holeZone
    shaftMax[Shaft Maximum Limit = 49.988 mm]:::shaftZone
    shaftMin[Shaft Minimum Limit = 49.963 mm]:::shaftZone
    zone1[Clearance Zone: 0.012 mm to 0.076 mm]:::fitZone
    fitType[RESULT: SLIDING CLEARANCE FIT]:::resultNode

    axisA --> holeMin
    holeMin --> holeMax
    axisA --> shaftMax
    shaftMax --> shaftMin
    holeMax --> zone1
    shaftMin --> zone1
    zone1 --> fitType

    classDef baseLine fill:#000000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef holeZone fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef shaftZone fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef fitZone fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef resultNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Fit Identification Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Given Hole and Shaft Designations]:::startNode
    stepB[Read Nominal Size d from Design]:::processNode
    stepC[Identify Size Range from IS 919 Table]:::processNode
    stepD[Lookup Hole Lower Deviation EI]:::processNode
    stepE[Lookup Hole Tolerance TH]:::processNode
    stepF[Lookup Shaft Upper Deviation es]:::processNode
    stepG[Lookup Shaft Tolerance Ts]:::processNode
    stepH[Compute Hole Max and Min Limits]:::computeNode
    stepI[Compute Shaft Max and Min Limits]:::computeNode
    stepJ[Calculate Cmax and Cmin]:::computeNode
    stepK{Both Cmax and Cmin Positive?}:::decisionNode
```
