```mermaid
    stepL[Both Cmax and Cmin Negative?]:::decisionNode
    res1[Clearance Fit]:::resultPos
    res2[Interference Fit]:::resultNeg
    res3[Transition Fit]:::resultMix

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepC --> stepE
    stepC --> stepF
    stepC --> stepG
    stepD --> stepH
    stepE --> stepH
    stepF --> stepI
    stepG --> stepI
    stepH --> stepJ
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK -- Yes --> res1
    stepK -- No --> stepL
    stepL -- Yes --> res2
    stepL -- No --> res3

    classDef startNode fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef processNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef computeNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef resultPos fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef resultNeg fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef resultMix fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Hole vs Shaft Systems

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HBS[Hole Basis System - Preferred]
        H1[Basic Line]:::base
        H2[Hole H at EI = 0]:::holeZ
        H3[Shaft varies: a to zc]:::shaftVar
        H1 --- H2
        H1 --- H3
    end

    subgraph SBS[Shaft Basis System]
        S1[Basic Line]:::base
        S2[Shaft h at es = 0]:::shaftZ
        S3[Hole varies: A to ZC]:::holeVar
        S1 --- S2
        S1 --- S3
    end

    classDef base fill:#000000,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef holeZ fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef shaftZ fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000000
    classDef shaftVar fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    classDef holeVar fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Aid Interpretation:** In the Hole Basis diagram, the hole's lower tolerance line is anchored exactly to the basic line (EI = 0), while the shaft tolerance zones can shift across the range from a to zc. In Shaft Basis, the shaft's upper line is anchored (es = 0) and the hole shifts. The clearance, transition, and interference fits emerge from the relative overlap of these zones.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** Define the terms: (i) Nominal Size, (ii) Tolerance, (iii) Allowance. How are they interrelated for a clearance fit?

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Nominal Size (d):** It is the design size of a part used for identification, e.g., 50 mm. It is a reference size from which limits are derived.
- **(ii) Tolerance (T):** The total permissible variation in a part's dimension, given by $T = L_{max} - L_{min}$. It is always positive and indicates manufacturing precision.
- **(iii) Allowance (A):** The minimum clearance or maximum interference intentionally designed between mating parts, given by $A = L_{max}^{shaft} - L_{min}^{hole}$ (negative for clearance). It is the difference between the **maximum material conditions** of the two parts.

**Interrelation for a Clearance Fit:** The minimum clearance equals the allowance. The tolerance zones of the hole and shaft do not overlap. Allowance = -(Hole Lower Deviation − Shaft Upper Deviation) = $-(EI - es) > 0$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** Differentiate between **Hole Basis System** and **Shaft Basis System**. State where each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hole Basis System | Shaft Basis System |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed Member | Hole (H, EI = 0) | Shaft (h, es = 0) |
| Variable Member | Shaft (varies) | Hole (varies) |
| Preferred For | General engineering, ISO standard | When a standard shaft is needed (e.g., spindles, pins) |
| Cost | Economical (standard reamers) | Costly (custom broaches) |

**Hole Basis** is the international default (used in 80%+ of industries). **Shaft Basis** is used in textile machinery, machine tool spindles, and where a continuous shaft passes through multiple holes.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose **3–4 marks** by:
> 1. Not showing the cube-root step in computing **i** — always write $\sqrt[3]{d}$ explicitly.
> 2. Mixing up upper/lower deviation signs for **shaft** vs **hole** (shaft uses lowercase es/ei; hole uses uppercase ES/EI).
> 3. Forgetting the conversion of **μm to mm** when final limits are reported in mm (e.g., 39 μm = 0.039 mm).
> 4. Not labeling the **fit type** explicitly with its standard name (e.g., "Sliding Fit" not just "Clearance Fit").

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]** For a **Hole Basis System** with nominal diameter **d = 80 mm**, hole designation **H8**, and shaft designation **h7**:

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the tolerance unit, the tolerance for H8, and the limits of the hole.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the limits of the shaft, the maximum and minimum clearance, and identify the fit type.

### Model Solution (Q3A)

**(a) Step 1: Tolerance Unit (i) for d = 80 mm**

For size range 50 < d ≤ 80 mm:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{d} + 0.001 \cdot d$$

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{80} + 0.001 \cdot 80$$

$$\sqrt[3]{80} \approx 4.3089$$

$$i = 0.45 \cdot 4.3089 + 0.08 = 1.9390 + 0.0800 = 2.0190 \quad \mu m$$

**Step 2: Tolerance for H8 (50–80 mm range)**

Using IS 919 table: For 50–80 mm range, $T_{H8} = 46 \quad \mu m = 0.046 \quad mm$.

(Using the i-formula approximation: $T_{H8} = 25 \cdot i \approx 50.5 \quad \mu m$, but tabulated value is preferred for KTU.)

**Step 3: Hole Limits**

$$L_{min}^{hole} = d + EI = 80 + 0 = 80.000 \quad mm$$

$$L_{max}^{hole} = 80.000 + 0.046 = 80.046 \quad mm$$

**(b) Step 1: Shaft h7 Limits**

For shaft h7 in 50–80 mm range:
- $es = 0$ (since h has fundamental deviation at upper limit = 0)
- $T_{h7} = 30 \quad \mu m = 0.030 \quad mm$ (per IS 919)

$$L_{max}^{shaft} = d + es = 80.000 \quad mm$$

$$L_{min}^{shaft} = 80.000 - 0.030 = 79.970 \quad mm$$

**Step 2: Clearances**

$$C_{max} = L_{max}^{hole} - L_{min}^{shaft} = 80.046 - 79.970 = +0.076 \quad mm$$

$$C_{min} = L_{min}^{hole} - L_{max}^{shaft} = 80.000 - 80.000 = 0.000 \quad mm$$

**Step 3: Fit Identification**

Since $C_{max} = +0.076$ (positive) and $C_{min} = 0$, the fit has zero to positive clearance throughout. This is a **LOCATIONAL CLEARANCE FIT** (H7/h6 standard, or H8/h7 variant), used for locating pins, jigs, and precision alignment applications.

---

**Q3B. [Alternative Choice — Same Module]** In a **Shaft Basis System**, the nominal size is **d = 40 mm**, the shaft is designated **h6**, and the hole is designated **H8**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the shaft limits, tolerance, and upper/lower deviations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the hole limits, the maximum and minimum clearances, and state the engineering application of this fit.

### Model Solution (Q3B)

**(a) Shaft h6 Limits (Shaft Basis)**

For size range 30 < d ≤ 50 mm, shaft h6:
- $es = 0$ (fundamental deviation at upper limit for shaft basis)
- $T_{h6} = 16 \quad \mu m = 0.016 \quad mm$ (per IS 919, 30–50 mm range)

$$L_{max}^{shaft} = 40 + 0 = 40.000 \quad mm$$

$$L_{min}^{shaft} = 40.000 - 0.016 = 39.984 \quad mm$$

- **Upper Deviation (es)** = 0
- **Lower Deviation (ei)** = $L_{min}^{shaft} - d = 39.984 - 40.000 = -0.016 \quad mm$
- **Tolerance** = 0.016 mm

**(b) Hole H8 Limits (Shaft Basis)**

For hole H8 in 30–50 mm range:
- $EI = +0.025 \quad mm$ (shaft basis — fundamental deviation for H is shifted)
  - More precisely, for shaft basis, EI of hole = -(tolerance of h) for matching fit. For the H8/h6 locational clearance fit: $EI = 0$ in hole basis, but in shaft basis the H designation's EI = $-T_h = -0.016$ mm. However, by strict IS convention, the H hole symbol retains EI = 0 in the standard, and the shaft has es = 0 in shaft basis.
- For this calculation, we use the standard values: $T_{H8} = 39 \quad \mu m = 0.039 \quad mm$, with $EI = -0.016 \quad mm$ (set to give a locational clearance fit).

$$L_{min}^{hole} = 40 + (-0.016) = 39.984 \quad mm$$

$$L_{max}^{hole} = 39.984 + 0.039 = 40.023 \quad mm$$

**Clearances:**

$$C_{max} = 40.023 - 39.984 = +0.039 \quad mm$$

$$C_{min} = 39.984 - 40.000 = -0.016 \quad mm$$

**Fit Type:** Since $C_{max} > 0$ and $C_{min} < 0$, this is a **TRANSITION FIT** (locational transition). **Application:** Used for removable couplings, gear hubs on shafts, and timing pulleys where precise location and moderate disassembly are needed.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Quick Revision Checklist for Limit and Fits Lab Viva & Exam:**

- **Basic Size vs Nominal Size:** Both are the same design reference dimension.
- **Hole ALWAYS uppercase letters; Shaft ALWAYS lowercase letters** (e.g., H8, g7).
- **Hole Basis:** H hole has EI = 0 (lower limit at basic line). Shafts can have es from negative to positive.
- **Shaft Basis:** h shaft has es = 0 (upper limit at basic line). Holes can have EI from positive to negative.
- **Tolerance Unit (i)** formula valid for 0 < d ≤ 500 mm; for d > 500 mm, use a slightly modified formula.
- **Tolerance Grade Numbers (IT01 to IT18):** Higher the grade number, **larger** the tolerance (looser fit). IT01 is the tightest, IT18 the loosest.
- **Clearance Fit:** All clearances positive ($C_{min} > 0$).
- **Interference Fit:** All clearances negative (must use force/heat to assemble).
- **Transition Fit:** $C_{max} > 0$ and $C_{min} < 0$ (slight overlap of tolerance zones).
- **Standard Fits to Remember:** H8/f7 (easy sliding), H8/g7 (sliding), H7/h6 (locational clearance), H7/k6 (locational transition), H7/p6 (locational interference), H7/s6 (medium force), H7/u8 (heavy force).
- **The "i" Formula:** Memorize $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{d} + 0.001 \cdot d$ in μm — it is asked almost every KTU cycle.
- **Unit Conversions:** 1 μm = 0.001 mm. Always convert when reporting final limits in mm.
- **Practical Lab Tip:** When performing the experiment, use **vernier caliper** (LC = 0.02 mm) or **micrometer** (LC = 0.01 mm). Take **at least 5 readings** at different positions. Calculate the **mean**, then compare with calculated tolerance limits to verify if the actual part conforms to the specified fit.
- **Common Viva Questions:** "Why is Hole Basis preferred?" (Standard tooling economy), "What is the difference between fit and tolerance?" (Fit is between two parts, tolerance is on a single part), "What is an allowance?" (Intentional dimensional difference at MMC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Final Note:** In the KTU 2024 Lab Exam for PCNSL408, the experiment on Limits and Fits typically awards **5 marks for viva voce**, **5 marks for the calculation sheet**, and **5 marks for the neat tabular presentation of results**. Always bring a printed IS 919 tolerance chart to the lab — it is your lifeline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Vibration Using Vibration Analyzer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Vibration Using Vibration Analyzer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration Analysis** is the discipline of measuring, analyzing, and interpreting oscillatory motion of a mechanical system using specialized instrumentation. A **Vibration Analyzer** is an electronic instrument that acquires vibration signals from transducers, conditions the signal, performs time-domain and frequency-domain analysis (typically via Fast Fourier Transform), and displays critical parameters such as **displacement (µm or mils)**, **velocity (mm/s or ips)**, and **acceleration (g or m/s²)**.

In the context of the KTU Measurements Lab (PCNSL408), the experiment involves:
- Generating controlled mechanical vibration (using a vibration exciter/shaker)
- Capturing the response using a **piezoelectric accelerometer**
- Processing the signal through a **FFT-based vibration analyzer**
- Extracting amplitude, frequency, and phase information
- Interpreting the spectral content for diagnostics

## 1.2 Key Vibration Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Engineering Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------|------------------|
| Displacement | $x$ | m | **µm (micrometers) / mils** |
| Velocity | $v$ | m/s | **mm/s / ips (inches per second)** |
| Acceleration | $a$ | m/s² | **g (9.81 m/s²) / ft/s²** |
| Frequency | $f$ | Hz | Hz / CPM (cycles per minute) |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | rad/s | rad/s |
| Phase | $\phi$ | rad / deg | rad / deg |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Washing Machine Imbalance**
> Imagine a **washing machine during the spin cycle**. When clothes clump on one side, the drum vibrates violently. Your *ears* sense this as a low rumble (velocity), and your *hand* placed on the chassis feels a rapid jiggle (acceleration). A vibration analyzer does the same job — but with mathematical precision. It "listens" to the machine with an accelerometer (the artificial "ear + hand"), converts the mechanical jiggle into an electrical signal, and translates the *rumble* into numbers a maintenance engineer can act upon. **If vibration amplitude doubles in 6 months, the engineer knows a bearing is failing — long before it seizes.**

## 1.4 Classification of Vibrations (Foundation Concepts)

- **Free Vibration**: Occurs when a system oscillates naturally after a single disturbance (e.g., plucking a guitar string).
- **Forced Vibration**: Sustained oscillation under an external periodic force (e.g., motor running on a defective bearing).
- **Damped Vibration**: Energy dissipates over time due to friction/damping (most real-world cases).
- **Undamped Vibration**: Idealized case with no energy loss (theoretical reference).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

The bedrock of vibration analysis is **Simple Harmonic Motion**, since any periodic vibration can be decomposed into a sum of SHM components (Fourier's Theorem).

For a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system undergoing SHM:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

where:
- $X$ = Peak amplitude (maximum displacement, m)
- $\omega$ = Angular frequency (rad/s)
- $t$ = Time (s)
- $\phi$ = Phase angle (rad)

The **velocity** is the first time-derivative of displacement:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = X \omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

The **acceleration** is the second time-derivative:

$$a(t) = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -X \omega^2 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

## 2.2 The Velocity-Leads-Displacement Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight**: Velocity **leads** displacement by **90°** (π/2 rad), and acceleration **leads** velocity by another **90°**. Therefore, acceleration leads displacement by **180°** (i.e., they are anti-phase for SHM).

This is critical for **phase-based diagnostics** — for example, distinguishing between:
- **Unbalance** (1× running frequency, phase-stable)
- **Misalignment** (2× running frequency, phase difference ≈ 180° across coupling)
- **Looseness** (multiple harmonics, random phase)

## 2.3 Amplitude Relationships (Conversion Formulas)

These formulas are the **most heavily tested** in KTU lab exams.

| Quantity | Relationship to Displacement ($X$) | Formula |
|----------|-----------------------------------|---------|
| Peak Velocity | $V = \omega X$ | $V = 2\pi f X$ |
| Peak Acceleration | $A = \omega^2 X$ | $A = (2\pi f)^2 X$ |
| Peak Velocity in terms of Peak Acceleration | $V = A / \omega$ | $V = A / (2\pi f)$ |
| Displacement in terms of Peak Velocity | $X = V / \omega$ | $X = V / (2\pi f)$ |

## 2.4 RMS, Peak, and Peak-to-Peak Conversions

For a **pure sinusoidal** vibration signal (single frequency):

$$X_{rms} = \frac{X_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot X_{peak}$$

$$X_{peak\text{-}to\text{-}peak} = 2 \cdot X_{peak}$$

$$X_{peak} = \frac{X_{peak\text{-}to\text{-}peak}}{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Using the $\sqrt{2}$ factor for **non-sinusoidal** (complex) vibration signals. The true RMS is calculated as $X_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2(t)\,dt}$, not by dividing peak by $\sqrt{2}$. Modern analyzers perform this integration directly.

## 2.5 Natural Frequency of SDOF System

For a spring-mass-damper system (relevant when analyzing machine-tool vibration, structural vibration):

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

where:
- $k$ = Stiffness of the spring (N/m)
- $m$ = Mass of the vibrating body (kg)
- $f_n$ = Natural frequency (Hz)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Time-domain displacement |
| 2 | $v(t) = X\omega\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | Time-domain velocity |
| 3 | $a(t) = -X\omega^2\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Time-domain acceleration |
| 4 | $V_{peak} = 2\pi f \cdot X_{peak}$ | Velocity from displacement |
| 5 | $A_{peak} = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot X_{peak}$ | Acceleration from displacement |
| 6 | $A_{peak} = 2\pi f \cdot V_{peak}$ | Acceleration from velocity |
| 7 | $X_{rms} = X_{peak}/\sqrt{2}$ | RMS for pure sinusoid |
| 8 | $X_{pp} = 2 X_{peak}$ | Peak-to-peak conversion |
| 9 | $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency (SDOF) |
| 10 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency |
| 11 | $T = 1/f$ | Period |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: Vibration analysis is used in **rotating machinery health monitoring** (motors, pumps, turbines), **structural health monitoring** (bridges, buildings during earthquakes), **automotive NVH** (Noise, Vibration, Harshness testing), **aerospace** (flutter analysis), and **ISO 10816 / ISO 20816** compliance checks for industrial machinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Displacement to Acceleration

**Starting point** — assume a body undergoes SHM with displacement:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 1: Differentiate to obtain velocity.**

By the chain rule of differentiation:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[X \sin(\omega t)\right]$$

$$v(t) = X \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot \omega = X\omega \cos(\omega t)$$

The peak velocity is therefore $V = X\omega$.

**Step 2: Differentiate velocity to obtain acceleration.**

$$a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[X\omega \cos(\omega t)\right]$$

$$a(t) = X\omega \cdot \left(-\sin(\omega t)\right) \cdot \omega = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$

The peak acceleration is therefore $A = X\omega^2$.

**Step 3: Establish the canonical relationship.**

Eliminating $X$ from the velocity and acceleration peaks:

$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{X\omega^2}{X\omega} = \omega$$

$$\boxed{A = \omega V = \omega^2 X}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement**:
> A machine component is vibrating sinusoidally. The vibration analyzer reports a **displacement peak-to-peak value of 200 µm** at a frequency of **50 Hz**. Calculate:
> (a) Peak displacement
> (b) Peak velocity
> (c) Peak acceleration
> (d) RMS velocity
> (e) Convert acceleration to units of **g**.

---

### **Solution**

**Given:**
- $X_{pp} = 200\ \mu m = 200 \times 10^{-6}\ m = 2 \times 10^{-4}\ m$
- $f = 50\ Hz$

**Step (a) — Peak Displacement:**

$$X_{peak} = \frac{X_{pp}}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{X_{peak} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ m = 100\ \mu m}$$

**Step (b) — Peak Velocity:**

$$V_{peak} = 2\pi f \cdot X_{peak}$$

$$V_{peak} = 2\pi (50) \times (1 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$V_{peak} = 100\pi \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{peak} \approx 0.0314\ m/s = 31.4\ mm/s}$$

**Step (c) — Peak Acceleration:**

$$A_{peak} = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot X_{peak}$$

$$A_{peak} = (2\pi \cdot 50)^2 \times (1 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$A_{peak} = (100\pi)^2 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$A_{peak} = 98696 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{A_{peak} \approx 9.87\ m/s^2}$$

**Step (d) — RMS Velocity:**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.0314}{1.414}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 0.0222\ m/s = 22.2\ mm/s}$$

**Step (e) — Acceleration in g-units:**

$$A_{peak}(g) = \frac{A_{peak}(m/s^2)}{9.81} = \frac{9.87}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{A_{peak} \approx 1.006\ g}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Selection for Vibration Parameter Measurement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Rule of Thumb (ISO 10816-3 / Brüel & Kjær Guidelines):**

| Frequency Range | Dominant Parameter | Why? |
|-----------------|-------------------|------|
| Low frequency (**< 10 Hz**) | **Displacement** | Slow swings cause structural fatigue from large excursions |
| Mid frequency (**10 Hz – 1000 Hz**) | **Velocity** | Proportional to vibrational energy; good general-purpose indicator |
| High frequency (**> 1000 Hz**) | **Acceleration** | High-frequency excitations (bearing defects, gear mesh) are best detected as g-levels |

This is why a vibration analyzer displays **all three parameters** and lets the operator switch — each frequency band of fault energy has a different "best" representation.

## 3.4 Derivation: Natural Frequency from Spring-Mass System

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$, displaced by $x$ and released.

**Newton's Second Law:**

$$m \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -k x$$

(The negative sign indicates the restoring force opposes displacement.)

**Rearranging:**

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Comparing with the standard SHM equation** $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Converting to Hz:**

$$\boxed{f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

---

## 3.5 Sample Experimental Data Table (KTU Lab Record Format)

| Trial | Frequency (Hz) | Displacement $X_{pp}$ (µm) | Velocity $V_{rms}$ (mm/s) | Acceleration $A_{peak}$ (g) | Phase (°) |
|-------|---------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | 25 | 240 | 8.42 | 0.094 | 45 |
| 2 | 50 | 200 | 14.0 | 0.252 | 50 |
| 3 | 75 | 160 | 16.8 | 0.378 | 55 |
| 4 | 100 | 120 | 16.8 | 0.504 | 60 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation Insight**: As frequency increases, displacement **decreases** (since input energy is constant), while velocity and acceleration **increase** (since $V \propto f$ and $A \propto f^2$ at constant displacement). This trend is the experimental signature of the inverse relationships derived in Section 3.1.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Vibration Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vibrating Machine\nor Test Specimen] --> B[Accelerometer\nPiezoelectric Sensor]
    B --> C[Charge Amplifier\nSignal Conditioner]
    C --> D[Anti-Aliasing\nLow-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[ADC\nAnalog-to-Digital Converter]
    E --> F[FFT Processor\nDigital Signal Processing]
    F --> G1[Time Domain Display\nWaveform]
    F --> G2[Frequency Domain Display\nSpectrum]
    F --> G3[Vibration Parameter Readout\nmm/s, g, um]
    G1 --> H[Operator Analysis\nand Diagnosis]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

**Description of Flow:**
1. The **vibrating machine** generates mechanical oscillation.
2. The **piezoelectric accelerometer** converts mechanical motion into an electrical charge signal proportional to acceleration.
3. The **charge amplifier** converts the high-impedance charge signal into a low-impedance voltage signal and provides gain.
4. The **anti-aliasing filter** removes frequencies above the Nyquist limit (half the sampling rate) to prevent spectral aliasing artifacts.
5. The **ADC** digitizes the analog voltage.
6. The **FFT processor** transforms the time-domain signal into a frequency-domain spectrum.
7. The display presents **waveform**, **spectrum**, and **computed parameters** (velocity, displacement, acceleration) simultaneously.
8. The **operator** interprets the data for condition monitoring.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Modern FFT Vibration Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT ["Input Stage"]
        A1[Accelerometer Input Channel 1] --> A2[Charge / IEPE Amplifier]
        A3[Tacho / Keyphasor Input] --> A4[Frequency Counter]
    end
    subgraph COND ["Signal Conditioning"]
        A2 --> A5[Programmable Gain Amplifier]
        A5 --> A6[Low-Pass Anti-Alias Filter]
        A6 --> A7[16-bit ADC]
    end
    subgraph DSP ["Digital Signal Processing"]
        A7 --> A8[Window Function\nHanning / Flat-Top]
        A8 --> A9[FFT Engine]
        A9 --> A10[Average, Peak Hold]
        A10 --> A11[Parameter Calculator\nRMS, Peak, Crest Factor]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT ["Output Stage"]
        A11 --> A12[Spectrum Display]
        A11 --> A13[Waveform Display]
        A11 --> A14[Overall Value Display]
        A4 --> A11
    end
```

## 4.3 Experimental Setup Schematic (Lab Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Function Generator\nor Power Amplifier] --> S[Vibration Shaker\nElectromagnetic Exciter]
    S --> T[Test Specimen\nCantilever Beam or Rotor]
    T --> M[Accelerometer Mounted\non Specimen]
    M --> A[Vibration Analyzer\nFFT Instrument]
    A --> DIS[Display Unit\nLaptop / Built-in Screen]
    A --> PR[Printer / Data Logger]
```

**Component Descriptions:**

| Label | Component | Function in Setup |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|
| P | Function Generator / Power Amplifier | Drives the shaker with a sinusoidal signal of controlled frequency and amplitude |
| S | Vibration Shaker | Converts electrical signal into mechanical force; produces controlled vibration |
| T | Test Specimen | The body whose vibration is being measured (beam, motor housing, rotor) |
| M | Accelerometer | Sensing element — converts mechanical motion to electrical signal |
| A | Vibration Analyzer | Performs FFT and displays time/frequency domain data |
| DIS | Display | Visual output of measured vibration |
| PR | Printer | Hard-copy record for lab submission |

## 4.4 Signal Flow — Time Domain to Frequency Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time-Domain Signal\nx t = X sin wt] --> B[Windowing\nApply Hanning]
    B --> C[FFT Algorithm]
    C --> D[Frequency-Domain Spectrum\nAmplitude vs Frequency]
    D --> E{Analysis Type}
    E --> E1[Identify Peak Frequencies]
    E --> E2[Compute RMS Overall]
    E --> E3[Phase Extraction]
    E --> E4[Harmonic Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Windowing?** When the captured signal length is not an integer multiple of the signal period, sharp discontinuities at the edges of the captured block cause **spectral leakage** — energy "spreads" to adjacent frequency bins. Applying a **Hanning window** tapers the signal smoothly to zero at the edges, dramatically reducing leakage at the cost of a slightly wider main lobe.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define a piezoelectric accelerometer and explain why it is preferred for vibration measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **piezoelectric accelerometer** is a vibration transducer that uses the **piezoelectric effect** — when certain crystals (e.g., quartz, PZT — lead zirconate titanate) are subjected to mechanical stress, they generate a proportional electrical charge.

It is preferred for vibration measurement because:
1. **Wide frequency range** (typically 0.5 Hz to 20 kHz), covering almost all industrial machinery fault frequencies.
2. **Wide amplitude range** (from fractions of a g to several thousand g).
3. **Compact, rugged, and lightweight** — minimal mass loading effect on the test specimen.
4. **Self-generating** — does not require an external power source (though IEPE versions need constant-current excitation).
5. **Excellent linearity** and long-term stability.

> *Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the five reasons (justify any 4 with brief explanation).*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an FFT analyzer? Why is FFT used in vibration analysis instead of direct frequency measurement filters?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) Analyzer** is an instrument that mathematically converts a time-domain vibration signal into its constituent frequency components, displaying amplitude (and optionally phase) versus frequency.

FFT is preferred over analog band-pass filter banks because:
1. **Speed** — A single FFT computes hundreds or thousands of frequency lines simultaneously, far faster than sweeping a filter.
2. **Resolution** — FFT provides uniform, high resolution across the entire frequency range.
3. **Phase information** is preserved; analog filter banks often lose phase.
4. **Flexibility** — Different frequency ranges, resolutions, and averaging schemes can be selected by software.
5. **Cost-effective** — A single ADC plus DSP replaces dozens of precision analog filters.

> *Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantages.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **vibration analyzer** used for the measurement of vibration. List the key specifications of a typical FFT analyzer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine part is vibrating with a peak displacement of **80 µm** at a frequency of **60 Hz**. Calculate the **(i)** peak velocity, **(ii)** peak acceleration, and **(iii)** RMS velocity. Also express the peak acceleration in units of **g**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Block Diagram** (Refer Section 4.2 of these notes).

**Working Principle:**

A vibration analyzer operates in the following sequence:

1. **Transduction**: A piezoelectric accelerometer mounted on the vibrating surface produces an electrical charge proportional to instantaneous acceleration.
2. **Charge Conversion**: A built-in **charge amplifier** (or IEPE conditioner) converts the high-impedance charge into a low-impedance voltage signal, scaled typically to **100 mV/g** or **1 V/g** sensitivity.
3. **Anti-Alias Filtering**: A low-pass filter (cut-off at 0.4 × sampling rate) prevents aliasing.
4. **Digitization**: A high-resolution **ADC** (16-bit or higher) samples the signal at rates from 1 kS/s to 100 kS/s depending on the frequency range of interest.
5. **Windowing**: A **Hanning window** is applied to the time-block to minimize spectral leakage.
6. **FFT Computation**: The digitized time-block is transformed to the frequency domain using the **Cooley-Tukey FFT algorithm**.
7. **Display**: The resulting spectrum is plotted as amplitude (linear or log) versus frequency. Time-domain waveform and overall RMS values are also computed.
8. **Parameter Readout**: Velocity and displacement are obtained by **single and double integration** of the acceleration spectrum (with corrections for low-frequency noise).

**Key Specifications of a Typical FFT Analyzer:**

| Specification | Typical Value |
|---------------|---------------|
| Frequency range | DC to 20 kHz (some up to 100 kHz) |
| Frequency resolution | 100, 200, 400, 800, 1600, 3200 lines |
| Dynamic range | 70 – 90 dB |
| ADC resolution | 16 – 24 bits |
| Input channels | 1 to 4 (synchronous sampling) |
| Window functions | Hanning, Hamming, Flat-top, Rectangular, Exponential |
| Averaging modes | RMS, Peak hold, Linear, Exponential |
| Real-time bandwidth | 1 kHz to 40 kHz |

> *Allocation: 4 marks for block diagram + working, 3 marks for specifications.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $X_{peak} = 80\ \mu m = 80 \times 10^{-6}\ m$
- $f = 60\ Hz$

**(i) Peak Velocity:**

$$V_{peak} = 2\pi f \cdot X_{peak}$$

$$V_{peak} = 2\pi \times 60 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$V_{peak} = 120\pi \times 80 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$V_{peak} = 9600\pi \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{V_{peak} \approx 0.0302\ m/s = 30.2\ mm/s}$$

**(ii) Peak Acceleration:**

$$A_{peak} = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot X_{peak}$$

$$A_{peak} = (2\pi \times 60)^2 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$A_{peak} = (120\pi)^2 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$A_{peak} = 14400\pi^2 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$A_{peak} \approx 142,223 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{A_{peak} \approx 11.38\ m/s^2}$$

**(iii) RMS Velocity:**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.0302}{1.414}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 0.0214\ m/s = 21.4\ mm/s}$$

**Peak Acceleration in g:**

$$A_{peak}(g) = \frac{11.38}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{A_{peak} \approx 1.16\ g}$$

> *Allocation: 1 mark each for (i), (ii), (iii), and the unit conversion. 3 marks reserved for correct formula selection and final answers with units.*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** Derive the relationships between **displacement, velocity, and acceleration** for a body executing Simple Harmonic Motion. Show that acceleration leads displacement by 180°. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **construction and working of a piezoelectric accelerometer** with a neat sketch. List any four advantages and two limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Given:** A body executes SHM with displacement

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 1: Velocity is the first derivative of displacement:**

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = X \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

This can be rewritten as $v(t) = X\omega \sin(\omega t + \pi/2)$, showing velocity **leads** displacement by **90°**.

**Step 2: Acceleration is the second derivative of displacement:**

$$a(t) = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$

This can be rewritten as $a(t) = X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t + \pi)$, showing acceleration **leads** displacement by **180° (π radians)**, i.e., acceleration is **anti-phase** with displacement.

**Step 3: Derive amplitude relationships:**

From the peak values:
- $X_{peak} = X$
- $V_{peak} = \omega X$
- $A_{peak} = \omega^2 X$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{peak} = \omega X_{peak} = 2\pi f \cdot X_{peak}}$$

$$\boxed{A_{peak} = \omega^2 X_{peak} = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot X_{peak}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{A_{peak}}{V_{peak}} = \omega = 2\pi f}$$

**Proof of 180° phase lead:**

$$a(t) = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t) = X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t + \pi)$$

Since $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$, the phase difference is **π radians = 180°**, with acceleration leading.

> *Allocation: 2 marks for differentiation steps, 2 marks for amplitude relations, 2 marks for the 180° proof, 1 mark for clear conclusion.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Construction (Sketch Description):**

A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of:
- A **piezoelectric crystal disc** (PZT or quartz) sandwiched between two electrodes.
- A **seismic mass** (tungsten or similar high-density material) placed on top of the crystal, preloaded by a bolt/spring.
- A **base/stud** for mounting on the vibrating surface.
- A **hermetically sealed housing** protecting the assembly from environment.
- An **electrical connector** (e.g., MIL-standard, BNC, or M5) for output.

**Working Principle:**

When the base is subjected to vibration, the inertia of the seismic mass applies a **varying force** $F = ma$ on the piezo crystal (Newton's second law). This force produces a **stress** $\sigma = F/A$ on the crystal, which by the **piezoelectric effect** generates a **proportional electrical charge**:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

where $d$ = piezoelectric charge sensitivity (C/N).

The charge is converted to a voltage by the charge amplifier:

$$V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C_f} = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_f} \cdot a$$

The output voltage is therefore **directly proportional to acceleration** — hence the name "accelerometer."

**Advantages (any four):**
1. Wide frequency range (0.5 Hz to 20 kHz).
2. Wide dynamic range — from µg to thousands of g.
3. Compact and lightweight — minimal mass loading.
4. Self-generating — no external power required (basic version).
5. Rugged and reliable in harsh industrial environments.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. Cannot measure **static (DC) acceleration** — piezo crystals respond only to changing force.
2. Sensitive to **temperature variations** and **transverse vibrations** (cross-axis sensitivity typically 1–5%).
3. Output is **charge-mode** requiring a low-noise charge amplifier, increasing cost.

> *Allocation: 2 marks for sketch, 2 marks for working, 2 marks for advantages, 1 mark for limitations.*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Vibration Analyzer Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units**: Numerical answers without units (mm/s, g, µm) lose **at least 0.5 mark** per sub-part. Always box the answer with units.
>
> 2. **Wrong Frequency Conversion**: Students often use $f$ instead of $\omega = 2\pi f$ in $V = \omega X$ and $A = \omega^2 X$. This produces answers off by a factor of $2\pi$ (≈ 6.28) — full-mark deductions follow.
>
> 3. **Confusing Peak, RMS, and Peak-to-Peak**: In KTU exams, you must clearly state which value is given and which is asked. Using $X_{pp}$ in a formula meant for $X_{peak}$ gives wrong answers.
>
> 4. **Incorrect FFT Concept**: Some students define FFT as "Fast Frequency Time" or describe it as a filter. The correct definition — "**Fast Fourier Transform — an efficient algorithm to compute the Discrete Fourier Transform**" — is mandatory for full marks.
>
> 5. **Block Diagram with Missing Blocks**: Always include **transducer → amplifier → filter → ADC → FFT → display**. Missing the anti-aliasing filter or charge amplifier costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Measurement of Vibration Using Vibration Analyzer**

- ✅ Vibration is **oscillatory motion** of a body about an equilibrium position.
- ✅ Three fundamental parameters: **displacement (µm)**, **velocity (mm/s)**, **acceleration (g)**.
- ✅ The canonical SHM equations: $x = X\sin\omega t$, $v = X\omega\cos\omega t$, $a = -X\omega^2\sin\omega t$.
- ✅ Velocity **leads** displacement by **90°**; acceleration **leads** velocity by 90° (180° ahead of displacement).
- ✅ Key relationships: $V = \omega X$, $A = \omega^2 X$, $A = \omega V$, where $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- ✅ RMS for pure sinusoid = Peak / $\sqrt{2}$. Peak-to-Peak = 2 × Peak.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric accelerometer** is the most common vibration transducer — uses the piezoelectric effect.
- ✅ Charge generated: $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$; output voltage proportional to acceleration.
- ✅ **FFT Analyzer** converts time-domain signal to frequency-domain spectrum using the Cooley-Tukey algorithm.
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** must be applied before ADC to satisfy Nyquist criterion.
- ✅ **Hanning window** is preferred to minimize spectral leakage.
- ✅ Frequency selection rule: < 10 Hz → displacement; 10–1000 Hz → velocity; > 1000 Hz → acceleration.
- ✅ Velocity and displacement are obtained from acceleration by **single and double integration** in the frequency domain.
- ✅ **ISO 10816 / ISO 20816** standards define vibration severity zones for industrial machinery.
- ✅ Experimental setup includes: **function generator → power amplifier → shaker → specimen → accelerometer → FFT analyzer → display**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Different Types of Dial Indicators, Stands, and Holders for Dial Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Different Types of Dial Indicators, Stands, and Holders for Dial Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Dial Indicator?

A **Dial Indicator** (also called a **Dial Gauge** or **DTI** — Dial Test Indicator) is a precision linear or angular displacement measuring instrument that converts the minute linear movement of a vertically reciprocating measuring plunger into a magnified, easily readable rotary motion of a pointer on a graduated circular dial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (PCNSL408 — Measurements Lab):**
> A dial indicator is a *comparative measuring device* consisting of a hardened contact point mounted on a rack-and-pinion or lever mechanism, geared to rotate a pointer across a graduated circular scale. It is used for measuring small linear displacements with high magnification, typically within the range of **0.01 mm to 0.001 mm** least count, and total spindle travel of **5 mm, 10 mm, or 25 mm** depending on grade.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the speedometer needle of a car. The car wheel rotates many full turns, but the needle only sweeps once around the dial. A dial indicator does exactly the opposite — a tiny linear push of the plunger (say, by just **1/100th of a millimetre**) causes the pointer to move a large, easily visible angular sweep across the dial. The internal gear train acts like a *mechanical amplifier*, making the invisible visible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in metrology:** Human eyes cannot resolve displacements of 0.01 mm directly. The dial indicator uses a **rack-and-pinion gear train** (or lever-and-spiral gear system) to magnify this movement up to **1000×** so that an operator can read it comfortably.

### 1.3 Core Functional Objectives of the Experiment

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifies the following learning intent for this experiment:

1. Identify and classify the **different grades** of dial indicators (Standard, Long Range, One-Revolution, Balanced Reading).
2. Recognize the **three primary dial indicator types**: *Plain (Plunger)*, *Lever (Test)*, and *Back-Plunger (Long Range)*.
3. Select the appropriate **stand and holder** combination based on the workpiece geometry and inspection requirement.
4. Understand the principle of magnification through **gear train ratio**.
5. Develop hands-on competency in **zero-setting**, **pre-loading**, and **plunger orientation** as per IS 3642 / ISO 463 standards.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Reference:** Indian Standard **IS 3642 (Part 1 & Part 2)** and **ISO 463:2022** govern the geometric specifications, accuracy classes (Grade A, B, C), and dial graduation requirements for dial indicators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Mechanism of Magnification

A dial indicator operates on one of two core mechanical principles:

### A. Rack-and-Pinion Principle (Plain & Back-Plunger Types)
- The plunger carries a precision **rack** (linear gear teeth) at its base.
- This rack engages with a small **pinion gear** attached to the pointer spindle.
- As the plunger moves linearly by *s*, the pinion rotates by angle *θ*.
- A second gear (driving gear) on the same shaft rotates the main pointer.

### B. Lever-and-Spiral Gear Principle (Test Indicators)
- The plunger acts as a **lever arm** pivoting about a fulcrum.
- Linear displacement *s* at the contact tip causes angular tilt.
- A **tangentially driven pinion** (spiral gear) rotates the pointer.
- This gives much higher sensitivity (down to **0.001 mm** or **0.00005 inch**).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

1. **Input displacement** *s* applied at contact point by workpiece surface.
2. **Conversion** of linear motion to rotary motion via gear train.
3. **Magnification** of small angular movement into large dial sweep.
4. **Visual readout** by pointer crossing pre-printed graduations on the dial face.
5. **Counting full revolutions** of the small subdial (revolution counter) for total travel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **total indication reading** = pointer reading on main dial + (number of full revolutions × range per revolution).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Description | Unit | Standard Value |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------------|------|----------------|
| 1 | Magnification Ratio | $M = \dfrac{\theta_{out}}{s_{in}}$ | Output angle per input displacement | rad/mm | 1000 – 5000 |
| 2 | Least Count | $LC = \dfrac{S}{N}$ | Graduation spacing S divided by divisions N | mm | 0.01 mm typical |
| 3 | Total Travel | $T = n \times LC \times 360$ | n revolutions × LC per revolution | mm | 5, 10, 25 mm |
| 4 | Indication Error | $E = \dfrac{\vert M_{actual} - M_{nominal} \vert}{M_{nominal}} \times 100$ | Percentage magnification error | % | ±2% to ±5% |
| 5 | Hysteresis Error | $H = R_{forward} - R_{reverse}$ | Difference at same point | µm | < 1 µm (Grade A) |
| 6 | Repeatability | $R = 2.77 \times \sigma$ | 3-sigma spread × factor | µm | < 0.5 µm |
| 7 | Lever Test Indicator Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{L}{l}$ | Long arm L, short arm l | ratio | 10:1 typical |
| 8 | Plunger Pre-load | $F_{pl} = F_{spring} - F_{gravity}$ | Spring minus weight component | N | 0.5 – 1.5 N |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Dial indicators form the backbone of **precision shop-floor metrology** and are used to:

- Measure **run-out** of rotating shafts on a lathe between centres.
- Check **flatness** and **parallelism** of machined surfaces.
- Align machine tool spindles (**tram-ing**).
- Inspect **bearing inner/outer races** for concentricity.
- Set cutting tools at precise depths on milling machines.
- Verify **squareness** and **perpendicularity** of fixtures.

> [!NOTE]
> In CNC machining cells, dial indicators are still preferred over electronic probes for **first-piece inspection** because they offer zero warm-up drift, instant readability, and complete immunity to electromagnetic noise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Magnification Ratio for Rack-and-Pinion Dial Indicator

Let us derive the relationship between plunger displacement and pointer rotation.

### Setup
Let the pinion gear have:
- Pitch circle radius = *r* (mm)
- Number of teeth = *z*

When the plunger (with rack) moves by distance *s*, the arc length traversed on the pinion equals *s*.

The angle of pinion rotation in radians is given by the arc-length relation.

$$
s = r \cdot \theta
$$

Rearranging for the rotation angle:

$$
\theta = \frac{s}{r}
$$

The pinion shares a common shaft with a driving gear (or the pointer spindle). If the pointer directly attaches to the pinion, then pointer angle = pinion angle. Now, the pointer sweeps over the dial circumference. If the dial has effective radius *R* and total *N* divisions across a full circle (i.e., 360° = $2\pi$ rad), then each division corresponds to a specific arc length on the dial.

### Derivation of Least Count Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{One full pointer revolution (360°)} &= 2\pi \cdot R \quad \text{(arc length on dial)} \\
\text{Plunger travel for one full revolution} &= n_{teeth} \cdot p \quad \text{(p = circular pitch)} \\
\text{Least Count (LC)} &= \frac{n_{teeth} \cdot p}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $n_{teeth}$ = number of rack teeth swept per revolution
- $p$ = circular pitch of rack (mm/tooth)
- $N$ = number of dial graduations on main dial

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A dial indicator has a rack with 20 teeth of circular pitch 0.5 mm, and the main dial is graduated into 100 divisions. Determine the least count and total travel for one full revolution.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Plunger travel per revolution:

$$
T_{rev} = n_{teeth} \times p = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 2 — Least Count:

$$
LC = \frac{T_{rev}}{N} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.1 \text{ mm}
$$

This means each division on the dial represents **0.1 mm**. This is a low-precision grade indicator.

Step 3 — For a higher grade, if the dial has 1000 graduations instead of 100:

$$
LC = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.01 \text{ mm}
$$

This is the **standard metrology grade** (0.01 mm least count).

Step 4 — Total instrument travel for a 10 mm range instrument:

$$
T_{total} = 1 \text{ revolution} = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

The small subdial (revolution counter) confirms how many full revolutions have occurred.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Dial Indicator Types

| Parameter | Plain Plunger DTI | Back-Plunger DTI | Lever Test Indicator | One-Revolution DTI |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Operating Principle** | Rack-and-pinion | Rack-and-pinion, reversed mounting | Lever and spiral gear | Special gear train, low friction |
| **Typical Range** | 5 mm, 10 mm, 25 mm | 25 mm, 50 mm, 100 mm | 0.2 mm, 0.4 mm, 0.8 mm | 1 mm, 2 mm |
| **Least Count** | 0.01 mm / 0.001 inch | 0.01 mm | 0.01 mm, 0.002 mm, 0.001 mm | 0.001 mm |
| **Direction of Plunger** | Axial, in-line with dial axis | Axial but from rear | Perpendicular, with lever arm | Axial |
| **Best For** | General purpose, surface plates, lathes | Long travel, large workpieces | Small bores, restricted access | Repeatability, no cumulative error |
| **Reading Direction** | Clockwise / CCW depending on model | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | Clockwise (or CCW) only |
| **Cumulative Error** | Yes (multiple revolutions) | Yes | No (limited range) | No (single revolution) |
| **Standard Reference** | IS 3642 / DIN 878 | IS 3642 / DIN 878 | ISO 463 / DIN 2270 | ISO 463 |

## 3.3 Derivation of Indication Error (KTU Frequently Tested)

For a dial indicator with nominal magnification *M*, the indication error is defined as:

$$
E_{\%} = \frac{\vert M_{actual} - M_{nominal} \vert}{M_{nominal}} \times 100
$$

**Worked Example:** A dial indicator has a nominal magnification of 1000. During calibration against a slip gauge, the actual plunger movement measured is 0.498 mm when the pointer has moved through 50 divisions (nominal movement = 0.5 mm). Find the indication error.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{nominal} &= \frac{50 \text{ divisions}}{0.5 \text{ mm}} = 100 \text{ div/mm} \\
M_{actual} &= \frac{50 \text{ divisions}}{0.498 \text{ mm}} = 100.4 \text{ div/mm} \\
E_{\%} &= \frac{\vert 100.4 - 100 \mid}{100} \times 100 = 0.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within **Grade A** tolerance (±1%) but slightly above **Grade AA** (±0.5%).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Plain Dial Indicator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Plunger with Rack Teeth"] --> nodeB["Pinion Gear 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Spindle Shaft"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Pinion Gear 2"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Pointer Hub"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Main Pointer on Dial"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Revolution Counter Pinion"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Small Sub Dial Pointer"]

    nodeH --> nodeJ["Sub Dial 0 to 10 Revolutions"]
    nodeK["Hairspring Minimizes Backlash"] -.-> nodeE
    nodeL["Bezel Locking Screw"] --> nodeF
    nodeM["Plunger Return Spring"] --> nodeA
    nodeN["Contact Tip Interchangeable Ball"] --> nodeA
```

```mermaid

    start["Measurement Requirement"] --> q1{"Is plunger travel less than 1 mm?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> lever["Use LEVER TEST INDICATOR"]

    q2 -- No --> q3{"Need zero cumulative error?"}
    q3 -- Yes --> onerev["Use ONE REVOLUTION DTI"]
    q3 -- No --> plain["Use PLAIN PLUNGER DTI 10 mm"]

```

```mermaid

```

## 4.4 Description of Each Functional Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Component Roles in the Inspection Setup:**

| S.No | Component | Functional Role | Key Specification |
|------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Plunger | Transfers workpiece displacement | Hardened steel, lapped finish, 4 mm dia |
| 2 | Rack and Pinion | Converts linear to rotary motion | Module 0.2 mm typical |
| 3 | Pointer and Dial | Visual indication | 100 or 1000 graduations |
| 4 | Bezel | Rotates for zero setting | 360° with lock screw |
| 5 | Magnetic Base | Mounts indicator on ferrous surface | ON/OFF switch, V-groove |
| 6 | Universal Stand | Adjustable height arm | Fine adjust knob, swivel joint |
| 7 | Comparator Stand | Vertical precision column | Granite base, 250 mm column |
| 8 | Holder or Clamp | Holds indicator to stand arm | 8 mm or 3/8 inch bore |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Tip:** Always rotate the bezel to align the zero mark with the actual contact point of plunger with workpiece **after applying the standard pre-load** of approximately **1 N**. Failing to pre-load introduces zero error of typically **0.02 – 0.05 mm**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between a Plain Plunger Dial Indicator and a Lever Type Test Indicator with respect to principle, range, least count and application."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Plain Plunger DTI | Lever Test Indicator |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Principle** | Rack-and-pinion, axial | Lever and spiral gear, perpendicular |
| **Range** | 5 to 25 mm | 0.2 to 0.8 mm |
| **Least Count** | 0.01 mm | 0.002 mm or 0.001 mm |
| **Application** | Run-out, flatness, parallelism | Internal bore concentricity, restricted gap measurement |

The plain plunger has the contact point moving along the axis of the dial, while in the lever type the contact point moves perpendicular to the dial face. (1 mark for principle, 1 mark for range and least count, 1 mark for application distinction.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"What is the function of a magnetic base stand in dial indicator mounting? State any two precautions while using it."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Function (1 mark):** A magnetic base stand provides a rigid, vibration-free, and repositionable mounting for the dial indicator on any ferrous surface (machine bed, surface plate, workpiece). It uses a permanent magnet with ON/OFF actuation to allow quick setting up and resetting without drilling or clamping.

**Precautions (2 marks — any two):**
1. Always ensure the **base surface is clean and flat**; dirt reduces magnetic holding force and introduces setup error.
2. The magnetic switch must be **fully OFF before repositioning** to avoid finger pinch and base slip.
3. The holding force is **insufficient on non-ferrous metals** like aluminium or brass; use a clamp stand instead.
4. Avoid using on thin plates less than **8 mm thick**, as flexure can release the magnetic grip.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a **plain plunger dial indicator**. Explain the role of the rack, pinion, hairspring and bezel in its operation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A dial indicator has a rack of 25 teeth with circular pitch 0.4 mm. The main dial is divided into 100 equal parts and the small dial counts up to 10 revolutions. Determine: (i) Least count, (ii) Total range of instrument, (iii) Indication reading when the pointer shows 47 divisions on main dial and 6 divisions on sub dial in clockwise direction. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

A plain plunger dial indicator consists of the following parts (4 marks for sketch description):

1. **Outer casing** — robust metal body, often with lugs for clamping.
2. **Plunger with rack teeth** — hardened steel, reciprocates along the axis of the instrument.
3. **Pinion gear** — meshes with the rack, converts linear to rotary motion.
4. **Driving gear** — on same spindle as pointer, multiplies rotation.
5. **Pointer** — sweeps across the graduated main dial.
6. **Small subdial pointer and revolution counter** — indicates number of full revolutions.
7. **Bezel and locking screw** — rotates to set zero; locked before measurement.
8. **Hairspring** — applies small friction torque to eliminate gear-train backlash.
9. **Contact tip** — interchangeable carbide or ruby ball at plunger end.
10. **Return spring** — pushes plunger back when released.

**Working (3 marks):** When the contact tip touches the workpiece and is displaced by *s*, the rack drives the pinion, rotating the main pointer. Each tooth movement corresponds to a fixed dial graduation. The bezel is rotated to align zero before locking. The hairspring keeps gears in mesh to eliminate backlash, ensuring consistent readings in both directions.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** Number of rack teeth $n = 25$, circular pitch $p = 0.4$ mm, main dial divisions $N_{main} = 100$, sub dial revolutions $n_{rev} = 10$.

**(i) Least Count:**

$$
LC = \frac{n \times p}{N_{main}} = \frac{25 \times 0.4}{100} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.1 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Total Range:**

$$
T_{total} = n_{rev} \times n \times p = 10 \times 25 \times 0.4 = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Indication Reading:**

Reading on main dial = 47 divisions × 0.1 mm = **4.7 mm**
Reading on sub dial = 6 revolutions × 10 mm/revolution = **60 mm**

Total indication = $60 + 4.7 = 64.7$ mm

If direction is clockwise, the reading is positive (+64.7 mm); if the convention is CCW positive, it is recorded as −64.7 mm.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Cycle 1]`

**(a)** Compare the **three major categories** of stands used for mounting dial indicators. For each, state one typical application and one limitation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and use of a **lever-type test indicator**. Justify why its least count can be higher than that of a plain plunger dial indicator. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Three Major Stand Categories (3 marks for comparison table):**

| Stand Type | Construction | Typical Application | Limitation |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|------------|
| **Magnetic Base Stand** | Permanent magnet inside a cast iron housing with ON/OFF knob; vertical post with swivel joint and clamping arm | Mounting on machine beds, surface plates, large castings | Works only on ferrous surfaces; holding force drops on painted or oily surfaces |
| **Universal Fine Adjust Stand** | Heavy cast base with vertical column; indicator held in fine-adjust collet; adjustment via micrometer knob | Precision alignment of spindles, surface grinder setup, comparator work | Bulky and expensive; not portable |
| **Surface Gauge Stand** | Cast iron base with V-groove; vertical scriber post with adjustable clamp | Scribing lines parallel to a reference surface; shop floor marking | No fine adjust; lower precision than comparator stand |
| **Comparator Stand** | Granite or cast iron base, hardened vertical column, micrometer adjustable table | Inspection of slip gauges, plug gauges, precision parts | Costly; requires vibration-free environment |

(Additional 2 marks for correctly identifying specific use cases and 2 marks for stating valid limitations.)

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Construction of Lever Test Indicator (4 marks):**

A lever test indicator has:
- A **contact lever** pivoting on a fulcrum inside the body.
- A **tangentially cut pinion** (spiral gear) fixed to the lever's longer arm.
- This pinion engages with a **central gear** mounted on the pointer spindle.
- The pointer reads on a small circular dial with typically **±40 graduations** on either side of zero.
- A **return spring** keeps the lever against a reference stop.
- The contact tip is at the end of the lever arm and moves **perpendicular** to the dial face.

**Use (2 marks):** It is used for **measuring run-out on small bores**, checking **concentricity**, and detecting **minute deviations** in restricted spaces (like gear teeth flanks, keyway walls, narrow grooves) where a plunger indicator cannot physically reach.

**Justification of Higher Least Count (1 mark):** Because the lever amplifies displacement through a mechanical advantage ratio $K = L/l$ where the long arm $L$ is typically **10 times** the short arm $l$, the rotational movement fed to the pointer gear is **10× larger** per unit plunger displacement. This allows finer graduations (0.001 mm or 0.00005 inch) compared to the standard 0.01 mm of plain plunger indicators.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Least Count with Resolution:** Least count is the smallest reading shown on the dial; resolution is the smallest detectable change by the instrument. Many students interchange them — examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Multiply by Sub-Dial Revolutions:** In total indication calculations, students often only report the main dial reading. Always compute: *Total = (sub-dial revs × range/rev) + main dial reading*.
>
> 3. **Confusing "One-Revolution" with "Plain Plunger":** A one-revolution DTI has *no cumulative error* because the range is exactly one full pointer rotation. Plain plunger DTIs can accumulate backlash over multiple revolutions.
>
> 4. **Stating "Magnetic Stand is for Aluminium":** Magnetic bases only work on **ferrous materials**. Aluminium, copper and brass require mechanical clamp stands.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Pre-load Concept:** Always mention **plunger pre-load (~0.5 to 1.0 N)** when describing measurement procedure. Examiners look for this as evidence of practical lab exposure.
>
> 6. **Wrong Sketch Orientation:** The contact tip must be shown **touching the workpiece surface**, not floating. The bezel lock and ON/OFF magnetic switch must be clearly labelled.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- A dial indicator is a **comparative measuring instrument** with magnification up to **1000×** or more.
- **Three main types**: Plain Plunger, Back-Plunger (Long Range), Lever Test Indicator.
- **Special type**: One-Revolution DTI — eliminates cumulative error.
- **Least Count formula**: $LC = \dfrac{\text{Plunger travel per revolution}}{\text{Number of graduations}}$.
- **Total range**: Plunger travel per revolution × Maximum revolutions on sub-dial.
- **Plain plunger range**: typically **5 mm, 10 mm, or 25 mm** with **0.01 mm LC**.
- **Lever test indicator LC**: typically **0.002 mm or 0.001 mm** with **±0.2 to ±0.8 mm range**.
- **Magnetic base**: ON/OFF switch, V-groove for cylindrical surfaces, only for **ferrous** materials.
- **Universal stand**: heavy base + fine adjust + swivel joints — used in **tool room**.
- **Comparator stand**: granite base + hardened column — used in **inspection room**.
- **Surface gauge**: scribing + measuring — used for **marking out** on a surface plate.
- **Bezel rotation**: allows zero alignment after pre-load is applied.
- **Hairspring function**: removes gear train backlash; critical for bidirectional accuracy.
- **Standard**: IS 3642 / ISO 463 — accuracy grades A, B, C.
- **Pre-load**: ~0.5 to 1.0 N of plunger force during measurement to eliminate lost motion.
- **Reading convention**: count sub-dial revolutions first, then add main dial reading.
- **Common errors**: indication error, hysteresis error, repeatability — all increase with wear.
- **Calibration**: using slip gauges or a sine bar on a surface plate.
- **Lab viva favourite**: "*Why do we rotate the bezel before locking?*" — to align the **actual contact zero** after pre-load.
- **Lab viva favourite**: "*Which indicator for a 50 mm bore measurement?*" — Lever Test Indicator, used with bore gauge holder.
- **Industrial use**: run-out, alignment, parallelism, flatness, squareness, depth, concentricity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Determination of Area Using Planometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Determination of Area Using Planimeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **PLAMIMETER (Planimeter):** A *plamimeter* is a precision mechanical-optical instrument used to measure the **area of an arbitrary, irregular two-dimensional figure** by mechanically tracing its boundary on a scaled drawing, map, or photograph. The result is obtained directly from a graduated counting wheel without recourse to mathematical integration of the boundary function.

In the KTU 2024 Measurements Lab (PCNSL408) syllabus, this experiment (Module Experiment 2) requires the student to:

1. **Study** the construction, working principle, and components of the *polar (Amsler) plamimeter*.
2. **Determine the multiplying constant (M)** of the plamimeter using a calibration figure of known area.
3. **Measure the area** of an irregular figure (e.g., a map, an irregular lamina, or a cross-section) and report the result in standard SI units (cm² or m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Spelling Clarification:** The instrument is correctly spelt **"Planimeter"** (from Latin *planum* = flat surface + Greek *metron* = measure). The word *"Planometer"* occasionally seen in question papers is a typographical variant referring to the same instrument. KTU examiners use *Planimeter* in the official valuation key.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mouse-Tracing" Intuition

Imagine you are drawing the outline of an irregular pond on a piece of paper using your computer mouse. If your mouse had a tiny **odometer (a wheel that counts every millimeter it rolls)**, the area of the pond you have traced could be calculated automatically. Why? Because as the mouse moves **along the boundary**, the wheel rolls *only* when motion is **perpendicular to the tracing arm** (analogous to the radial direction from the pole), and slides without rolling when motion is **tangential**. The integrated perpendicular displacement is mathematically proportional to the enclosed area.

**Real-world parallel:**

| Everyday Device | Planimeter Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Mouse scroll wheel | Measuring roller (counts vertical displacement) |
| Anchor of the mouse on the desk | Pole/anchor block (fixed reference point) |
| Mouse pointer | Tracer point (the moving probe) |
| On-screen area fill | Mechanical integration into area |

## 1.3 Classification of Planimeters

Planimeters are broadly classified into two mechanical families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Hierarchy:**
> - **Planimeters**
>   - **Polar (Amsler) Planimeter** ← *Most commonly used in KTU labs*
>     - Fixed pole
>     - Pole placed *outside* the figure → **Area = M × R + C**
>     - Pole placed *inside* the figure → **Area = M × R + C + M × C₁** (where C₁ is the "circle of zero" or anchor circle constant)
>   - **Linear (Roller) Planimeter**
>     - Pole moves along a straight track
>     - Used for elongated strip maps
>   - **Prytz (Hatchet) Planimeter**
>     - Simple single-bar instrument
>     - Lower accuracy; rare in academic labs
>   - **Digital / Electronic Planimeter**
>     - Modern version with LCD display and encoder
>     - Used in GIS and CAD environments

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Specifications

For a typical **Amsler Polar Planimeter** encountered in KTU labs:

- **Length of tracer arm (a):** 100 mm to 350 mm (adjustable)
- **Wheel circumference:** 100 graduation units (typical) → 1 revolution = 100 divisions
- **Vernier resolution:** $\frac{1}{10}$ of a wheel division
- **Multiplying constant (M):** Usually 100 mm² per division (depends on arm setting)
- **Additive constant (C):** Ideally zero when calibrated; otherwise determined empirically

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind the Rolling-Sliding Wheel

A plamimeter performs a **mechanical integration** of the form:

$$A = \oint x \, dy$$

where the integral is taken around the closed boundary of the figure. This is the classical **Green's Theorem** formulation for area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Green's Theorem Connection:** The area enclosed by any closed curve can be expressed as the line integral of position with respect to perpendicular displacement. The planimeter's wheel physically executes this integration as the tracer is moved along the boundary.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. The **tracer point** is placed exactly on the boundary of the figure to be measured.
2. The **pole (anchor block)** is fixed at a point **outside** the figure using its needle weight.
3. The **tracer arm** pivots freely about the pole joint and the tracer end.
4. As the operator **traces the boundary clockwise**, the tracer arm undergoes a complex motion involving both rotation about the pole and translation along the boundary.
5. This motion decomposes into two components at the **measuring wheel**:
   - **Perpendicular component** (radial) → Wheel **rolls** → counted on the dial
   - **Tangential component** → Wheel **slides** → not counted
6. After completing the closed traverse, the **net rolling** of the wheel is directly proportional to the enclosed area.

## 2.2 Components of a Polar (Amsler) Planimeter

| Component | Function | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Pole Block (Anchor)** | Fixed reference point with a needle that pierces the drawing board | Provides the immovable origin for radial distance *r* |
| **Pole Arm (Carrying Arm)** | Rigid bar connecting pole to tracer arm pivot | Maintains the geometry during tracing |
| **Tracer Arm (Measuring Arm)** | The long bar ending in the tracer point; length adjustable | Its length *a* sets the **multiplying constant M** |
| **Tracer Point (Probe / Stylus)** | Magnified lens with a fine cross-hair at the center | The exact point that follows the boundary |
| **Measuring Wheel (Integrator Wheel)** | Graduated drum that rolls on the paper surface | Records perpendicular displacement in divisions |
| **Vernier Scale** | Auxiliary scale alongside the wheel | Reads fractional divisions to $\frac{1}{10}$ |
| **Counting Dial** | Tall slender needle that records complete revolutions of the wheel | Each full turn = **+100** divisions |
| **Index Marker** | Fixed reference point on the carriage | Reading is taken by aligning the vernier zero |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these four formulas — they cover 95% of KTU 2024 questions on this experiment.**

### Primary Working Formulas

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = M \times (F.R. - I.R. \pm 10n + C)$ | $A$ = Area; $M$ = Multiplying constant; $F.R.$ = Final Reading; $I.R.$ = Initial Reading; $n$ = Number of complete revolutions; $C$ = Additive constant | $A$ in cm² if M is in cm²/div |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{A_{known}}{(F.R. - I.R.) \pm 10n + C}$ | Used to **calibrate** the plamimeter using a known area | $A_{known}$ measured with ruler |
| 3 | $M = \pi R^2$ (for circular test figure) | If a circle of radius *R* is used for calibration | Simplifies calibration |
| 4 | Pole-zero condition: $n = +ve$ | Traced **clockwise** with pole **outside** the figure | KTU standard convention |
| 4a | Pole-zero condition: $n = -ve$ | Traced **counter-clockwise** with pole **outside** | Reverse convention |
| 5 | Relative Error: $\%E = \dfrac{\vert A_{measured} - A_{true} \vert}{A_{true}} \times 100$ | Used to assess experimental accuracy | Reported at end of record |

### Reading Convention at the Counting Mechanism

A typical plamimeter has **three simultaneous scales**:

1. **Disc Dial (large cylinder):** Records 0–9 full revolutions → one full turn = 10 wheel rotations
2. **Wheel Graduations:** 0–99 on the rim of the measuring wheel
3. **Vernier:** 0–9 (giving 0.1 precision)

**Total Reading** = (Disc × 1000) + (Wheel × 10) + (Vernier × 1)

Example: Disc = 2, Wheel = 47, Vernier = 6 → Reading = **2476 divisions**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **disc value is multiplied by 1000**, not 100. A reading of "2 revolutions of the disc" is actually **2000 divisions**, not 200. Always verify the gear ratio printed on the instrument.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Planimeter |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Computing catchment area from topographic maps for dam design |
| **Hydrology & Water Resources** | Determining watershed boundaries and runoff estimation |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Computing cross-sectional area of irregular machine components from blueprints |
| **Mining & Geology** | Ore reserve estimation from geological cross-sections |
| **Cartography & GIS** | Historical method (now largely replaced by digital tools) for area of countries, lakes, forests |
| **Medical Imaging** | Rare: estimating tumor area from histological cross-sections |
| **Agriculture** | Field area measurement from cadastral maps |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of the Working Formula

The classical derivation of the polar planimeter formula uses **Green's Theorem** applied to a two-link mechanism.

### Setup (Refer to the diagram in Section 4)

Let:
- $O$ = position of the **pole** (fixed)
- $P$ = position of the **tracer point** (moving on the boundary)
- $Q$ = pivot point of the **measuring wheel** (between pole and tracer)
- $r$ = length of the pole arm (from $O$ to $Q$)
- $a$ = length of the tracer arm (from $Q$ to $P$)
- $\theta$ = angle of the pole arm with respect to the *x*-axis
- $\phi$ = angle of the tracer arm with respect to the pole arm
- $\psi$ = angle that the wheel plane makes with the tracer arm

When the tracer $P$ moves an infinitesimal distance $dP$ along the boundary, the wheel rolls by an amount:

$$ds = (a \sin\psi) \, d\phi + (a \cos\psi + r \sin\theta) \, d\theta$$

For the standard Amsler configuration where the wheel plane is aligned with the pole arm (i.e., $\psi = \phi$), this simplifies to:

$$ds = a \sin\phi \, d\phi + (a \cos\phi + r \sin\theta) \, d\theta$$

### Applying Green's Theorem

The total area swept by the tracer arm is:

$$A = \oint x \, dy = \oint y \, dx$$

Through algebraic manipulation (full steps in standard textbooks), this reduces to:

$$A = a \cdot s + a \cdot r \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \oint d\theta + a^2 \oint \sin\phi \cos\phi \, d\phi$$

For a **complete closed trace**, the second and third integrals vanish (since the wheel rotates by exactly $2\pi$ over the closed path, giving $\oint d\theta = 2\pi$, but the geometric cancellation leaves):

$$A = M \cdot N + C$$

where:
- $M$ = multiplying constant (depends on $a$)
- $N$ = net reading = (F.R. − I.R.) $\pm 10n$
- $C$ = additive constant (zero for a perfectly adjusted plamimeter)

**Hence the celebrated working formula:**

$$\boxed{A = M \times (F.R. - I.R. \pm 10n + C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign of $10n$ is **positive** when tracing **clockwise** with the pole **outside** the figure, and the counting wheel **rolls in the positive direction**. Always follow the convention prescribed in your lab manual.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure

### Part A: Determination of Multiplying Constant *M* (Calibration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always calibrate the plamimeter FIRST** before measuring unknown areas. The multiplying constant depends on the tracer arm length, which can shift slightly.

**Step 1 — Setup:**
1. Fix the drawing sheet on a smooth, horizontal board.
2. Place the plamimeter such that the **tracer point** lies exactly on the boundary of a **circle of known radius** (e.g., $R = 5$ cm drawn with a compass).
3. Fix the **pole block** outside the circle.
4. Set the tracer arm to a fixed, predetermined length (e.g., $a = 100$ mm) and lock it with the clamp screw.

**Step 2 — Take Initial Reading:**
1. With the tracer precisely on the boundary, record:
   - Disc reading: $D_1$
   - Wheel reading: $W_1$
   - Vernier reading: $V_1$
2. Compute: $I.R. = 1000 D_1 + 10 W_1 + V_1$

**Step 3 — Trace the Boundary:**
1. Slowly and carefully trace the circle **clockwise** with the tracer.
2. Maintain a steady pace to avoid jerking the wheel.
3. After exactly one full revolution, stop precisely on the starting mark.

**Step 4 — Take Final Reading:**
1. Record $D_2$, $W_2$, $V_2$ and compute $F.R.$

**Step 5 — Compute *M*:**
1. Known area: $A_{known} = \pi R^2$
2. Net reading: $N = (F.R. - I.R.) + 10n$ (here $n = 0$ for the small circle)
3. $M = \dfrac{A_{known}}{N + C}$, with $C = 0$ if previously determined to be zero
4. Repeat 3 times and take the **mean** of $M$ values.

### Part B: Determination of the Additive Constant *C* (Optional but Examinable)

**Step 1:** Place the pole block **inside** a known circle of radius $R$.
**Step 2:** Trace the boundary once clockwise.
**Step 3:** Read the net change $N$.
**Step 4:** Apply the modified formula:

$$A = M \times N + M \times C_1 + C$$

where $C_1$ is the area of the "anchor circle" (the locus of the pole when the tracer arm is perpendicular to the pole arm). For a small adjustment, $C \approx 0$.

### Part C: Measurement of the Unknown Area

**Step 1:** Place the unknown figure (e.g., a map of Kerala, a leaf tracing, an irregular lamina) on the board.
**Step 2:** Set up the plamimeter identically to Part A.
**Step 3:** Take Initial Reading (I.R.) with tracer on a recognizable point (e.g., a corner).
**Step 4:** Trace the boundary carefully **clockwise** (or as instructed).
**Step 5:** Take Final Reading (F.R.) on the same starting point.
**Step 6:** Compute:

$$A_{unknown} = M \times (F.R. - I.R. + 10n)$$

**Step 7:** Repeat the measurement 3 times and report the **mean area** with **standard deviation**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** A polar plamimeter with tracer arm set to $a = 120$ mm is used to trace a circle of radius $R = 5$ cm. The initial reading is 3426 and the final reading is 3583. Find the multiplying constant *M*. Then use this plamimeter to measure an irregular figure whose initial and final readings are 1204 and 1890, with the wheel having completed exactly 1 full revolution in the positive direction.

### Solution — Part (i): Calibration

Known area of the circle:

$$A_{known} = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (5)^2 = 25\pi \approx 78.54 \text{ cm}^2$$

Net reading for the calibration circle (no full revolution, $n = 0$):

$$N_{cal} = F.R. - I.R. = 3583 - 3426 = 157 \text{ divisions}$$

Multiplying constant:

$$M = \frac{A_{known}}{N_{cal}} = \frac{78.54}{157} \approx 0.5003 \text{ cm}^2 / \text{division}$$

### Solution — Part (ii): Unknown Area

Net reading (with $n = 1$ full revolution):

$$N_{unk} = (F.R. - I.R.) + 10n = (1890 - 1204) + 10(1) = 686 + 10 = 696 \text{ divisions}$$

Measured area:

$$A_{unk} = M \times N_{unk} = 0.5003 \times 696 \approx 348.21 \text{ cm}^2$$

### Verification of Result

Converting to m² for cross-check:

$$A_{unk} = 348.21 \text{ cm}^2 = 348.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \approx 0.0348 \text{ m}^2$$

This is consistent with a typical KTU sample figure.

## 3.4 Source-of-Error Matrix

| Source of Error | Type | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Jerky tracing motion | Random | Use steady, slow tracing speed |
| Wheel slipping on paper | Random | Use smooth, dust-free paper |
