| Pole not rigidly fixed | Systematic | Press anchor firmly; use heavy weight |
| Parallax in vernier reading | Random | Read vernier with eye directly above |
| Tracer deviating from boundary | Systematic | Use magnifying lens; sharp pencil boundary |
| Tracer arm length drift | Systematic | Lock the clamp screw tightly after setting |
| Boundary not starting/ending at same point | Systematic | Mark start point with a fine pencil dot |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Polar Planimeter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Hand Movement] --> B[Tracer Point P]
    B --> C{Tracer Arm Length a}
    C --> D[Wheel Pivot Point Q]
    D --> E[Pole Arm Length r]
    E --> F[Fixed Pole Anchor O]
    D --> G[Measuring Wheel Integrator]
    G --> G1[Disc Dial rev counter]
    G --> G2[Wheel Graduations 0 to 99]
    G --> G3[Vernier Scale 0.1 precision]
    G1 --> H[Total Reading N divisions]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Multiply by M]
    I --> J[Add Constant C]
    J --> K[Final Area A in cm squared]
    H --> L[Count full revolutions n]
    L --> M[Compute 10n correction]
    M --> I
```

## 4.2 Mechanical Layout of the Amsler Polar Planimeter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BO[Drawing Board]
        P((Pole Anchor)) --- Q[Pole Arm of length r]
        Q --- J[Pivot Joint at Wheel Carriage]
        J --- R[Tracer Arm of length a]
        R --- T((Tracer Point on Boundary))
        J --- W[Measuring Wheel]
    end
    W --> W1((Rolls in direction of motion))
    T --> T1((Traces closed path clockwise))
    P --> P1((Fixed needle pierces board))
    style BO fill:#e0f0ff
    style W fill:#ffe0b0
    style T fill:#b0ffc0
    style P fill:#ffb0b0
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow of the Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Fix drawing sheet on board] --> S2[Step 2: Place tracer on known calibration circle]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Anchor pole block outside figure]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Record Initial Reading IR]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Trace boundary clockwise once]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Record Final Reading FR]
    S6 --> S7{Is FR exactly at start point?}
    S7 -->|No| S8[Reject reading and repeat]
    S8 --> S4
    S7 -->|Yes| S9[Compute Net Reading N equals FR minus IR]
    S9 --> S10[Calculate M equals A_known divided by N]
    S10 --> S11[Step 7: Repeat 3 times for mean M]
    S11 --> S12[Step 8: Now place tracer on unknown figure]
    S12 --> S13[Step 9: Trace boundary and record readings]
    S13 --> S14[Compute A_unknown equals M times N]
    S14 --> S15[Step 10: Report mean area and percentage error]
```

## 4.4 Vector Geometry of the Planimeter Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Origin: Pole Anchor O] -->|Vector r along pole arm| B[Wheel Pivot Q]
    B -->|Vector a along tracer arm| C[Tracer Point P]
    C -->|Displacement dP along boundary| D[New Tracer Position P prime]
    D -->|New pole arm angle theta plus dtheta| A
    B -->|Wheel rolls by ds| E[Counting Mechanism]
    E -->|Integrates ds over closed path| F[Net Reading N]
    F -->|Multiplied by M| G[Area A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The four mermaid blocks together give a complete functional picture: (1) signal flow from hand → area, (2) mechanical layout on the board, (3) procedural workflow, and (4) vector geometry. KTU viva questions often probe the student's ability to point to the *tracer*, *pole*, and *wheel* on a labelled diagram — practice drawing this layout by hand as well.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define a planimeter. Name the two main types of planimeters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A planimeter is a precision instrument used to measure the area of an irregular two-dimensional figure by tracing its boundary mechanically, without requiring mathematical computation of the boundary equation. It works on the principle of **mechanical integration** using a rolling-sliding wheel whose net rotation is proportional to the enclosed area.

**The two main types are:**
1. **Polar (Amsler) Planimeter** – the pole is fixed at a point, and the tracer arm pivots about it. Most widely used in laboratories.
2. **Linear Planimeter** – the pole is constrained to move along a straight track, suitable for elongated strip-shaped figures.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming polar, 1 mark for naming linear.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**State the working formula of a polar planimeter and explain the significance of the multiplying constant M.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\boxed{A = M \times (F.R. - I.R. \pm 10n + C)}$$

where $A$ is the area, $F.R.$ and $I.R.$ are the final and initial readings, $n$ is the number of complete revolutions of the measuring wheel, and $C$ is the additive constant.

**Significance of *M*:** The multiplying constant *M* converts the dimensionless reading (number of wheel divisions) into physical area units (cm² or m²). It is determined experimentally by tracing a figure of **known area** (usually a circle) and computing $M = A_{known} / N$. The value of *M* depends on the **tracer arm length** and the **wheel circumference**, so it must be re-determined whenever the arm length is altered.

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark calibration procedure.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of an Amsler polar planimeter. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe the experimental procedure to determine the multiplying constant of a planimeter using a known circle. A planimeter with tracer arm length $a = 100$ mm is used to trace a circle of radius $5$ cm. The initial and final readings are 2410 and 2568 respectively. Calculate the multiplying constant and use it to find the area of an irregular figure whose readings are I.R. = 1004, F.R. = 1702, with the wheel making exactly 1 positive revolution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working Principle (7 marks):**

*(Sketch description: 3 marks — student should draw a planimeter showing pole, pole arm, tracer arm, measuring wheel, tracer point, disc dial, wheel graduations, vernier.)*

The Amsler polar planimeter consists of the following key components:

1. **Pole block (anchor)** – a heavy weight with a needle that pierces the drawing board, providing a fixed pivot.
2. **Pole arm (carrying arm)** – a rigid bar of fixed length *r* connecting the pole to the wheel carriage.
3. **Tracer arm (measuring arm)** – an adjustable-length bar carrying the tracer point at its end; length set by a clamp and vernier scale.
4. **Tracer point** – a fine cross-hair marker (often under a magnifier) that follows the boundary.
5. **Measuring wheel (integrator wheel)** – a precisely graduated drum that rolls on the paper.
6. **Counting mechanism** – consisting of a disc dial (revolution counter), a wheel graduation (0–99), and a vernier (0.1 precision).
7. **Index marker** – a fixed reference line for reading the scales.

**Working Principle (4 marks):** When the operator traces the boundary of a figure with the tracer point, the tracer arm undergoes a complex two-dimensional motion. This motion is resolved at the measuring wheel into two components: (i) a **perpendicular component** that causes the wheel to **roll** (counted), and (ii) a **tangential component** that causes the wheel to **slide** (not counted). The integrated perpendicular displacement over one complete closed traverse is, by Green's theorem, **directly proportional to the enclosed area**. The proportionality constant is the multiplying constant *M*, which converts wheel divisions to area units.

**(b) Procedure and Calculation (7 marks):**

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Fix the sheet on a smooth board.
2. Place the pole block **outside** the calibration circle.
3. Set tracer arm to 100 mm and lock the clamp.
4. Place tracer on the boundary; record I.R.
5. Trace the circle **clockwise** slowly and steadily.
6. Stop at the starting point; record F.R.
7. Repeat 3 times and take the mean.

**Calculation of *M* (2 marks):**

$$A_{known} = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (5)^2 = 78.54 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$N_{cal} = F.R. - I.R. = 2568 - 2410 = 158 \text{ divisions}$$

$$M = \frac{78.54}{158} = 0.4970 \text{ cm}^2/\text{division}$$

**Area of irregular figure (2 marks):**

$$N_{unk} = (1702 - 1004) + 10(1) = 698 + 10 = 708 \text{ divisions}$$

$$A_{unk} = M \times N_{unk} = 0.4970 \times 708 = 351.88 \text{ cm}^2 \approx 352 \text{ cm}^2$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**a)** Explain the sources of error in planimeter measurements. How is the additive constant *C* determined experimentally? **(7 marks)**

**b)** A polar planimeter is set up with pole **inside** a known circle of radius 4 cm. The I.R. is 1240, F.R. is 1456, and 1 negative revolution is recorded. The multiplying constant is $M = 0.502$ cm²/div. Find the total area enclosed, given the additive constant $C = 0$ and the anchor-circle constant $C_1 = 50$ cm². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sources of Error and Additive Constant (7 marks):**

**Sources of Error (4 marks):**
1. **Instrumental errors:** non-circular wheel, eccentricity of wheel axis, worn graduations.
2. **Operational errors:** jerky tracing, parallax in reading, boundary deviation.
3. **Environmental errors:** paper expansion/contraction with humidity, uneven board surface.
4. **Geometric errors:** pole block slipping, arm length change, wheel sliding on dusty paper.

**Determination of *C* (3 marks):** The additive constant *C* is determined by tracing a known area with the pole placed **inside** the figure (so that the formula $A = M \times N + C$ applies, with the **anchor circle** enclosed). Two methods:
- **Direct method:** Use the formula $C = A_{known} - M \times N$ after tracing the known figure with pole inside.
- **Compensation method:** Adjust the tracer arm length such that $C = 0$ exactly (the plamimeter is then said to be *mathematically adjusted*).

**(b) Calculation with Pole Inside (7 marks):**

**Formula (3 marks):** When pole is **inside** the figure, the working formula is:

$$A = M \times N + M \times C_1 + C$$

$$N = (F.R. - I.R.) - 10n = (1456 - 1240) - 10(1) = 216 - 10 = 206 \text{ divisions}$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**

$$A = (0.502 \times 206) + (0.502 \times 50) + 0$$

$$A = 103.41 + 25.10 = 128.51 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Result (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{A \approx 128.5 \text{ cm}^2}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 
> **🔴 MAJOR MISTAKES (2–3 marks lost each):**
> 1. **Sign error on $10n$:** Students write $+$ when tracing counter-clockwise or $-$ when tracing clockwise. *Rule of thumb:* With pole **outside**, clockwise trace → $F.R. > I.R.$ → **+10n**.
> 2. **Forgetting to add $10n$:** This is the single most common error. Always check if the wheel made complete revolutions by reading the disc dial.
> 3. **Wrong gear ratio:** Treating one disc revolution as 100 divisions instead of **1000 divisions**. Check your instrument's calibration.
> 4. **Confusing *M* with *a*:** The multiplying constant $M$ is **not** equal to the tracer arm length $a$. $M$ depends on $a$ but is in **area units per division**.
> 
> **🟡 MINOR MISTAKES (1 mark lost each):**
> 5. Writing units as "cm²" when *M* was calibrated in "mm²/div" — unit mismatch.
> 6. Reporting only one trial instead of the mean of three trials.
> 7. Skipping the sketch in part (a) — 3 marks reserved for neat labelled diagram.
> 8. Not mentioning that the **pole must be outside** for the standard formula to hold.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU 2024 Quick-Fire Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Planimeter = instrument for area measurement by mechanical integration of boundary.
- ✅ **Two main types:** Polar (Amsler) and Linear.
- ✅ **Standard formula:** $A = M \times (F.R. - I.R. \pm 10n + C)$ — **M = multiplying constant, C = additive constant**.
- ✅ **M is calibrated** using a known circle: $M = \pi R^2 / (F.R. - I.R.)$.
- ✅ **Calibration first**, then measurement — always recalibrate if tracer arm length is changed.
- ✅ **Pole outside + clockwise trace** → $F.R. > I.R.$ → use **+10n** convention.
- ✅ **Gear ratio:** 1 disc revolution = 1000 divisions (verify on your instrument).
- ✅ **Three scales** to read: Disc (×1000) + Wheel (×10) + Vernier (×0.1).
- ✅ **Sources of error:** Jerky tracing, wheel slip, pole drift, parallax — all minimizable.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Catchment area (civil), ore reserve (mining), watershed mapping (hydrology).
- ✅ **Pole-inside case:** Formula gains extra term $M \times C_1$ where $C_1$ is the anchor-circle area.
- ✅ **Typical KTU values:** $M \approx 0.5$ cm²/div; tracer arm $\approx 100$–$120$ mm; calibration circle $\approx 5$ cm radius.
- ✅ **Viva favourite:** *"What happens if the tracer does not return exactly to the start point?"* → Reading must be rejected as it indicates loss of contact or boundary slip.
- ✅ **Viva favourite 2:** *"Why is the wheel not affected by tangential motion?"* → Tangential motion causes sliding without rolling, so it is geometrically decoupled from area integration.
- ✅ **Always report:** Mean of 3 trials, multiplying constant, percentage error $\%E = \frac{\vert A_{measured} - A_{true} \vert}{A_{true}} \times 100$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polishing, Etching, and Determination of Grain Size and Microstructure Studies Using Optical Microscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Polishing, Etching, and Determination of Grain Size and Microstructure Studies Using Optical Microscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Metallography** is the scientific study of the microstructure and crystal structure of metals and alloys, typically performed through optical or electron microscopy on carefully prepared polished and etched specimens. The fundamental workflow involves three sequential stages:

1. **Sectioning & Mounting** — Cutting a representative sample and embedding it in a thermosetting/thermoplastic resin for handling.
2. **Grinding & Polishing** — Progressive abrasion using SiC papers (120 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1000 → 1200 grit) followed by fine polishing with diamond paste (typically **1 µm to 0.25 µm**) or alumina suspensions to obtain a mirror-like, scratch-free surface.
3. **Etching** — Controlled chemical attack using an appropriate **etchant** (e.g., Nital, Kalling's, Keller's reagent) to reveal grain boundaries, phases, and inclusions by selectively attacking boundaries of higher energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** *Metallographic examination is the systematic preparation (polishing + etching) of a metal specimen followed by microscopic observation to reveal its true microstructure, grain size, and phase distribution.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a detective trying to read a book, but every page has been sanded flat — you cannot see the letters. The job of metallography is exactly that: restoring visibility.

- **Grinding** is like erasing pencil marks — removing deep scratches layer by layer.
- **Polishing** is like buffing a mirror until no marks remain — you get a *flat, reflective surface*.
- **Etching** is like sprinkling lemon juice on an invisible ink drawing — the acid preferentially attacks the high-energy grain boundaries, creating tiny grooves. When light hits these grooves under the microscope, the boundaries appear as **dark lines**, while the grain interiors remain bright. *Now you can "see" the grain structure.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need to etch?** A perfectly polished surface under a microscope only shows reflections and scratches. Etching creates **optical contrast** at grain boundaries, revealing the otherwise invisible internal architecture of the metal.

## 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics

- Visible light wavelength range used in optical microscopy: **λ ≈ 400 nm to 700 nm**
- Maximum useful magnification of optical microscope: **≈ 1000×** (limited by Abbe diffraction limit)
- Abbe diffraction limit: $d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{\text{NA}}$ where NA = numerical aperture
- Standard etchant for plain carbon steel: **Nital (2% HNO₃ in ethanol)**
- Standard etchant for stainless steel: **Kalling's No. 2 / Aqua Regia variants**
- Standard etchant for aluminum alloys: **Keller's Reagent (HF + HCl + HNO₃ + H₂O)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown (The 6-Stage Metallographic Workflow)

### Stage 1 — Sectioning
- Use abrasive cut-off wheel or precision saw.
- Goal: Obtain a small specimen (≈ 10–25 mm diameter, 10–15 mm height) representative of bulk material.
- **Why:** Heat generated must be minimized to avoid altering microstructure (no tempering/martensite transformation).

### Stage 2 — Mounting
- **Cold mounting:** Uses epoxy resin (slow, low pressure, ideal for delicate samples).
- **Hot mounting:** Uses phenolic or Bakelite powder at ~150 °C, 25 MPa (faster, harder, but thermal exposure).
- **Why:** Provides uniform geometry for handling and edge retention.

### Stage 3 — Grinding
- Sequential SiC papers rotated **90°** between grades to track removal of prior scratches.
- Water acts as lubricant and coolant.
- Typical grit progression: **120 → 220 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1000 → 1200**.

### Stage 4 — Polishing
- **Rough polishing:** Diamond paste (6 µm) on a rotating disc covered with napless cloth.
- **Final polishing:** Diamond paste (1 µm or 0.25 µm) on a soft nap cloth.
- Lubricant: diamond extender / alcohol-based fluid.
- **Why:** Eliminates scratches down to sub-micron level — surface roughness Ra < 0.1 µm.

### Stage 5 — Etching
- Specimen is dipped/swabbed in etchant for a few seconds, then immediately rinsed with water/alcohol and dried.
- Mechanism: Etchant dissolves grain boundaries faster than grain interiors due to higher boundary energy and dislocation density.

### Stage 6 — Microscopic Examination
- Magnifications used: **100×, 200×, 500×, 1000×**.
- Photograph the microstructure for grain size quantification.

## 2.2 Optical Microscope Construction (Schematic Logic)

An **optical (metallurgical) microscope** uses **reflected light** (not transmitted) since metals are opaque.

Path of light: **Lamp → Aperture Diaphragm → Field Lens → Vertical Illuminator (semi-reflecting prism) → Objective Lens → Specimen (reflects light back) → Objective → Prism → Eyepiece (or Camera)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| $n = 2^{N-1}$ | $N$ = ASTM grain size number, $n$ = grains/in² at 100× | ASTM standard grain size |
| $N_L = \frac{L_t}{L_{total}}$ | $L_t$ = total test line length, $N_L$ = intercepts per unit length | Heyn's lineal intercept method |
| $\bar{L} = \frac{L_{total}}{N_{total}}$ | $\bar{L}$ = mean lineal intercept | Heyn's method |
| $d = \sqrt{\bar{L}^2}$ or $d = 1.128 \bar{L}$ | $d$ = average grain diameter | Conversion to equivalent circular diameter |
| $N_A = \frac{N_{inside} + 0.5 \, N_{on\_boundary}}{A_{test}}$ | $N_A$ = number of grains per unit area | Jeffries' planimetric method |
| $\bar{A} = \frac{1}{N_A}$ | $\bar{A}$ = average grain area | Jeffries' method |
| $M_{actual} = M_{objective} \times M_{eyepiece}$ | $M$ = magnification | Microscope calibration |
| $d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{\text{NA}}$ | $d$ = diffraction limit, NA = numerical aperture | Optical resolution limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Tip:** The ASTM grain size number $N$ ranges typically from **−1 (extremely coarse) to 14 (ultra-fine, nanocrystalline)**. Each unit increase in $N$ **doubles** the number of grains per unit area.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Grain size determination is critical in:
- **Aerospace industry:** Fine-grained turbine blades for high-temperature creep resistance.
- **Automotive:** Deep-drawing steel sheets require specific grain size for formability.
- **Heat treatment QC:** Verifying austenitic grain size in quenched steels (McQuaid-Ehn test for case hardening).
- **Failure analysis:** Detecting abnormal grain growth, which may indicate overheating.
- **Hall-Petch relationship:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$ — yield strength increases with decreasing grain size.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ASTM Grain Size Number Relationship

**Goal:** Establish the relationship between ASTM grain size number $N$ and the number of grains per square inch at 100× magnification.

**Starting Definition (ASTM E112):**

$$N = 2^{N-1} \quad \text{grains/in}^2 \text{ at } 100\times$$

**Step 1 — Compute grains per square millimetre (more common in modern labs):**

Since $1 \text{ in} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$, we have:

$$1 \text{ in}^2 = (25.4)^2 = 645.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

Therefore, grains per mm² at 100×:

$$n_{mm^2} = \frac{2^{N-1}}{645.16}$$

**Step 2 — Compute average grain area at 100×:**

$$\bar{A} = \frac{645.16}{2^{N-1}} \quad \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent average grain diameter:**

$$d = \sqrt{\bar{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{N-1}}} \text{ mm}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

A specimen shows **ASTM grain size number N = 7**. Find average grain diameter in micrometres.

$$\bar{A} = \frac{645.16}{2^{7-1}} = \frac{645.16}{64} = 10.0806 \text{ mm}^2$$

Converting to µm²:

$$10.0806 \text{ mm}^2 = 10.0806 \times 10^6 \text{ µm}^2 = 1.00806 \times 10^7 \text{ µm}^2$$

$$d = \sqrt{1.00806 \times 10^7} = 3175 \text{ µm} \approx 31.75 \text{ µm}$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Heyn's Lineal Intercept Method

**Procedure:**
1. Draw several straight test lines of total length $L_{total}$ across the photomicrograph.
2. Count the number of grain boundaries intercepted by each line: $N_i$.
3. Compute mean lineal intercept: $\bar{L} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{N_{total}}$.

**Numerical Example:**

Photomicrograph at 100×, test lines: 5 horizontal + 5 vertical lines, each 100 mm long. Total intercepted boundaries counted = 87.

$$L_{total} = 10 \times 100 = 1000 \text{ mm (on photomicrograph)}$$

$$\bar{L}_{photo} = \frac{1000}{87} = 11.494 \text{ mm}$$

Converting to actual specimen scale (100× magnification):

$$\bar{L}_{actual} = \frac{11.494}{100} = 0.11494 \text{ mm} = 114.94 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ASTM grain size number from $\bar{L}$:**

The empirical formula relating $\bar{L}$ (in mm at 100×) to $N$ is:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{10^{2.698 - 0.340 N}}{\sqrt{N}} \text{ mm}$$

For ASTM N = 7:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{10^{2.698 - 2.380}}{\sqrt{7}} = \frac{10^{0.318}}{2.646} = \frac{2.081}{2.646} = 0.7865 \text{ mm at 100×}$$

Equivalent real scale: $0.7865 / 100 = 7.865 \text{ µm}$ — matches grain size ranges from ASTM tables.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Jeffries' Planimetric Method

**Procedure:**
1. Draw a circle of known area $A$ on the photomicrograph (or use a rectangular test grid).
2. Count grains entirely **inside** the circle: $N_{inside}$.
3. Count grains **intersected** by the boundary: $N_{boundary}$.
4. Compute true grain count:

$$N_{true} = N_{inside} + 0.5 \, N_{boundary}$$

5. Compute grains per unit area at the photomicrograph scale:

$$N_A^{photo} = \frac{N_{true}}{A_{photo}}$$

6. Convert to actual specimen scale using magnification factor $M$:

$$N_A^{actual} = \frac{N_A^{photo}}{M^2}$$

**Numerical Example:**

Circle area on photomicrograph at 200× = **5000 mm²**. Inside grains = 64. Boundary grains = 28.

$$N_{true} = 64 + 0.5 \times 28 = 64 + 14 = 78$$

$$N_A^{photo} = \frac{78}{5000} = 0.0156 \text{ grains/mm}^2$$

$$N_A^{actual} = \frac{0.0156}{200^2} = \frac{0.0156}{40000} = 3.9 \times 10^{-7} \text{ grains/mm}^2$$

Converting to grains/in²:

$$N_A^{actual} = 3.9 \times 10^{-7} \times 645.16 \times 10^6 \approx 251.6 \text{ grains/in}^2$$

ASTM grain size number:

$$2^{N-1} = 251.6 \implies N - 1 = \log_2(251.6) = 7.97 \implies N \approx 9$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Rule:** Jeffries' method is statistically more accurate than a single lineal intercept but requires manual counting of at least 300–500 grains for 95% confidence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Metallographic Preparation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Metal Sample] --> B[Sectioning Abrasive Cut-off Saw]
    B --> C[Mounting Cold or Hot Mount]
    C --> D[Grinding SiC Papers 120 to 1200 grit]
    D --> E[Rough Polishing 6 micron Diamond]
    E --> F[Final Polishing 0.25 to 1 micron Diamond]
    F --> G{Check for Scratches under Microscope}
    G -->|Scratches Present| H[Repeat Final Polishing]
    G -->|Mirror Finish Achieved| I[Etching with Nital 2 to 5 sec]
    H --> F
    I --> J[Rinse with Water then Alcohol]
    J --> K[Hot Air Dry]
    K --> L[Examine under Optical Microscope 100x to 1000x]
    L --> M[Capture Micrograph]
    M --> N[Grain Size Determination]
    N --> O[Report Grain Size Number N ASTM]
```

## 4.2 Optical Reflected-Light Microscope — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Light Source Halogen or LED] --> A1[Aperture Diaphragm Controls Intensity]
    A1 --> F1[Field Lens Collimates Beam]
    F1 --> V1[Vertical Illuminator Semi Reflecting Prism]
    V1 --> O1[Objective Lens Magnification 5x to 100x]
    O1 --> S1[Specimen Surface Polished and Etched]
    S1 -.Reflects Light.-> O1
    O1 --> V1
    V1 --> B1[Beam Splitter Prism]
    B1 --> E1[Eyepiece 10x] --> Eye[Observer Eye or Camera Sensor]
```

## 4.3 Grain Size Measurement Workflow (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Micrograph Captured at known M] --> Q1{Choose Method}
    Q1 -->|ASTM Standard Comparison| R1[Match with Standard Chart E112]
    Q1 -->|Heyn Lineal Intercept| R2[Draw Test Lines Count Intercepts]
    Q1 -->|Jeffries Planimetric| R3[Draw Test Circle Count Grains]
    R1 --> S1[Compute ASTM Number N]
    R2 --> T1[Compute mean L]
    T1 --> S1
    R3 --> U1[Compute N_A grains per area]
    U1 --> S1
    S1 --> V1[Compute Average Grain Diameter d]
    V1 --> W1[Final Report with Magnification and Method]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define metallographic etching. Why is it necessary after polishing?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Metallographic etching** is the controlled chemical attack of a polished metal specimen's surface using a suitable reagent (etchant) such that grain boundaries are preferentially dissolved.
>
> **Necessity:** A perfectly polished specimen under a microscope reflects light uniformly and shows only scratches and reflections — no internal structure is visible. Etching preferentially dissolves the higher-energy grain boundary regions (due to atomic disorder and segregation), creating tiny grooves. These grooves scatter light under the microscope, producing **dark lines at boundaries** and **bright interiors**, thereby revealing the true microstructure, grain size, and phase distribution.
>
> **[3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for necessity]**

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any two common etchants used for plain carbon steel and stainless steel. State the chemical composition of Nital.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Material** | **Etchant** | **Composition** |
|---|---|---|
| Plain Carbon Steel | Nital | 2–5% HNO₃ + 98–95% Ethanol |
| Plain Carbon Steel | Picral | 4% Picric acid + Ethanol |
| Stainless Steel | Kalling's No. 2 | 5 g CuCl₂ + 100 mL HCl + 100 mL Ethanol |
| Stainless Steel | Aqua Regia | HCl : HNO₃ = 3 : 1 |

> **[3 marks — 1 for listing + 1 for composition + 1 for stainless steel etchant]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the complete procedure for metallographic specimen preparation of a medium carbon steel specimen, starting from a raw sample up to microscopic examination. Include details of grit sequence, polishing media, and etchant. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of an optical metallurgical microscope. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Sectioning:** A small representative specimen (≈ 15 mm cube) is cut from the bulk using an abrasive cut-off saw with water coolant to prevent heat-induced microstructural changes. **[0.5 mark]**

**Step 2 — Mounting:** The specimen is mounted in Bakelite (hot mounting) at ~150 °C and 25 MPa pressure. This gives a uniform cylindrical shape with edge retention. **[0.5 mark]**

**Step 3 — Grinding (Sequential):**

| **Stage** | **SiC Paper Grit** | **Time / Direction** |
|---|---|---|
| Coarse | 120, 220 | Removes sectioning damage, rotates 90° each grade |
| Intermediate | 400, 600 | Refines surface |
| Fine | 800, 1000, 1200 | Final fine grinding before polishing |

Specimen is rotated by 90° between grades to ensure complete removal of prior scratches. Water serves as coolant and lubricant. **[1.5 marks]**

**Step 4 — Polishing:**

- **Rough polishing:** Diamond paste (6 µm) on napless cloth rotating at 200–300 rpm, with diamond extender as lubricant. **[1 mark]**
- **Final polishing:** Diamond paste (1 µm or 0.25 µm) on soft napped cloth until mirror finish is obtained. **[1 mark]**

**Step 5 — Etching:**

- Etchant: **2% Nital** (2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL ethanol). **[0.5 mark]**
- Specimen is swabbed or dipped for 5–10 seconds. **[0.5 mark]**
- Immediately rinsed with water followed by alcohol to stop the reaction. **[0.5 mark]**
- Dried with hot air blower. **[0.5 mark]**

**Step 6 — Microscopic Examination:** Observed under metallurgical microscope at 100×, 200×, 500× magnifications; photomicrograph is captured for grain size measurement. **[0.5 mark]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Construction of Optical Metallurgical Microscope (Block-level schematic):**

Major components:

1. **Light Source:** Halogen or LED lamp provides illumination. **[0.5 mark]**
2. **Aperture Diaphragm:** Controls the intensity and cone angle of light. **[0.5 mark]**
3. **Field Lens:** Collimates the light beam. **[0.5 mark]**
4. **Vertical Illuminator (Prism/Beam Splitter):** Directs light vertically downward through the objective onto the specimen. **[1 mark]**
5. **Objective Lens:** Magnifying lens (5×, 10×, 50×, 100×) that focuses light onto the specimen and collects reflected light. **[1 mark]**
6. **Specimen Stage:** Flat platform holding the polished and etched specimen. **[0.5 mark]**
7. **Beam-Splitter Prism (in trinocular version):** Directs light either to the eyepiece or to the camera. **[0.5 mark]**
8. **Eyepiece:** Magnifies the image (typically 10×); final magnification = objective × eyepiece. **[0.5 mark]**
9. **Camera (optional):** Captures the photomicrograph. **[0.5 mark]**
10. **Coarse and Fine Focusing Knobs:** Move stage vertically to bring specimen into focus. **[0.5 mark]**

**Working Principle:**

> Light from the source → aperture → field lens → vertical illuminator (semi-reflecting prism) → objective lens → falls on the **polished and etched specimen surface**. The specimen reflects light back through the objective, through the prism, into the eyepiece/camera. The **etched grooves at grain boundaries scatter light**, appearing dark, while flat grain interiors reflect light brightly. Hence the microstructure is observed.

**[Final 1 mark for labelled sketch and complete working explanation]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students forget to mention the **direction of rotation (90°)** between successive grit papers — this is a frequently asked sub-question.
> 2. Students often confuse **Nital with Aqua Regia** — Nital is for carbon steel, Aqua Regia is for stainless steel.
> 3. For microscope question, **never skip the role of the vertical illuminator** — it is the key distinction between biological (transmitted) and metallurgical (reflected) microscopes. **Losing 1–2 marks here is common.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define ASTM grain size number. Derive the relationship between ASTM grain size number $N$, the number of grains per square inch at 100× magnification, and the mean lineal intercept $\bar{L}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A photomicrograph of a mild steel specimen is taken at 200× magnification. A Jeffries' planimetric circle of area 5000 mm² (on the photograph) contains 80 grains completely inside and 40 grains intersecting the boundary. Determine the ASTM grain size number. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** The ASTM grain size number $N$ is a standard index (per ASTM E112) where each unit increase in $N$ represents a **doubling** of the number of grains per unit area at 100× magnification. **[1 mark]**

**Derivation of Relationship:**

**Step 1:** By ASTM definition, at 100× magnification:

$$n = 2^{N-1} \quad \text{grains/in}^2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to grains per square millimetre:

$$N_A = \frac{2^{N-1}}{645.16} \quad \text{grains/mm}^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3:** The mean grain area at 100× is:

$$\bar{A} = \frac{1}{N_A} = \frac{645.16}{2^{N-1}} \text{ mm}^2 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4:** For approximately equiaxed grains, the mean lineal intercept is related to the mean grain area by:

$$\bar{L} \approx 1.128 \sqrt{\bar{A}} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

This constant **1.128** comes from geometric averaging of chord lengths over a random circle ($4/\pi \approx 1.273$ is sometimes used, but for ASTM standard $1.128 = \sqrt{4/\pi}$).

**Step 5:** Substituting (iii) into (iv):

$$\bar{L} = 1.128 \times \sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{N-1}}} = \frac{28.65}{\sqrt{2^{N-1}}} = \frac{28.65}{2^{(N-1)/2}} \text{ mm at } 100\times \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Step 6:** Converting to actual scale (divide by magnification M = 100):

$$\bar{L}_{actual} = \frac{0.2865}{2^{(N-1)/2}} \text{ mm} \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

This is the standard ASTM E112 relationship used for direct grain size determination from lineal intercept measurements. **[6 marks total — 1 for definition, 5 for derivation]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Magnification $M = 200\times$
- Circle area on photograph: $A_{photo} = 5000 \text{ mm}^2$
- Grains inside: $N_{inside} = 80$
- Grains on boundary: $N_{boundary} = 40$

**Step 1: True grain count inside the test circle.**

$$N_{true} = N_{inside} + 0.5 \times N_{boundary}$$

$$N_{true} = 80 + 0.5 \times 40 = 80 + 20 = 100 \text{ grains} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 2: Grains per unit area on the photograph.**

$$N_A^{photo} = \frac{N_{true}}{A_{photo}} = \frac{100}{5000} = 0.02 \text{ grains/mm}^2 \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 3: Convert to actual scale of the specimen.**

Since the photograph is at 200× magnification, the area on the photo is $200^2 = 40{,}000$ times the actual specimen area.

$$N_A^{actual} = \frac{N_A^{photo}}{M^2} = \frac{0.02}{40000} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ grains/mm}^2 \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 4: Convert to grains per square inch at 100× equivalent.**

We must first convert $N_A^{actual}$ to grains per square inch:

$$N_A^{inch^2} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 645.16 \times 10^6 = 322.58 \text{ grains/in}^2$$

Now normalize to **100× magnification**. Since the photo was taken at 200×, the equivalent count at 100× is adjusted by:

$$n_{100\times} = N_A^{inch^2} \times \left(\frac{200}{100}\right)^2 = 322.58 \times 4 = 1290.3 \text{ grains/in}^2 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 5: Compute ASTM grain size number.**

$$n_{100\times} = 2^{N-1}$$

$$2^{N-1} = 1290.3$$

Taking $\log_2$ of both sides:

$$N - 1 = \log_2(1290.3) = \frac{\log_{10}(1290.3)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.1106}{0.3010} = 10.33$$

$$\boxed{N = 11.33 \approx 11}$$

**[1 mark]**

**Practical Significance:** A mild steel specimen with ASTM grain size of 11 indicates a **fine-grained** microstructure, associated with good toughness, higher yield strength (Hall-Petch), and uniform mechanical response. **[0.5 mark]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common error:** Forgetting to **normalize to 100× magnification** before applying the ASTM formula. ASTM N is *defined* at 100×. **3 marks penalty if skipped.**
> 2. Forgetting the **0.5 multiplier** for boundary grains in Jeffries' method — accounts for the fact that each boundary grain is shared between two test areas.
> 3. Mixing up **mm² and in²** in unit conversions — always verify by checking ASTM E112 reference table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Metallography = Polishing + Etching + Microscopy.** Three inseparable stages.
- [ ] **Grinding sequence:** Always rotate sample **90°** between successive grit papers; finish at **1200 grit**.
- [ ] **Polishing media:** Diamond paste (6 µm → 1 µm → 0.25 µm) on rotating cloth discs.
- [ ] **Etchant for carbon steel:** **2% Nital (HNO₃ + Ethanol)** — most commonly asked etchant in KTU exams.
- [ ] **Etchant for stainless steel:** Kalling's No. 2 or Aqua Regia.
- [ ] **Etchant for aluminium:** Keller's reagent (HF + HCl + HNO₃ + H₂O).
- [ ] **Optical microscope uses REFLECTED light**, not transmitted — key difference from biological microscope.
- [ ] **ASTM grain size formula:** $n = 2^{N-1}$ grains/in² at 100× — each unit increase doubles grain count.
- [ ] **Heyn's method:** $\bar{L} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{N_{total}}$ — fast and easy but directional bias possible.
- [ ] **Jeffries' method:** $N_{true} = N_{inside} + 0.5\, N_{boundary}$ — more statistically accurate.
- [ ] **Magnification in optical microscope:** $M_{total} = M_{objective} \times M_{eyepiece}$.
- [ ] **Diffraction limit:** $d \approx \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{\text{NA}} \approx 0.2 \text{ µm}$ — sets max useful magnification at ~1000×.
- [ ] **Hall-Petch relationship:** Smaller grain size → higher yield strength — directly motivates grain size measurement in industry.
- [ ] **KTU favourite numerical:** Always be ready to solve Jeffries' planimetric problems with magnification correction to 100×.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Quick Revision Mnemonic for KTU:**
> **"S-S-M-G-P-E-M"** = Section → Section-off → Mount → Grind → Polish → Etch → Microscope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Use of Different Types of Comparators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Use of Different Types of Comparators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Comparator** is a precision measuring instrument that compares the linear (or sometimes angular) dimensions of a workpiece against a **standard reference (sub-master or slip gauge)** through a high-magnification indicator. It does not measure an absolute length; rather, it detects and magnifies small deviations of a dimension from a preset value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> A comparator is an indirect type of measuring device in which the linear displacement of a contact point (plunger/stylus) is amplified mechanically, optically, pneumatically, or electrically to give a magnified indication of the deviation from a datum/reference set by slip gauges.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine weighing yourself. A normal bathroom scale directly tells you **78 kg**. That's a *direct* measurement. Now, imagine you have a scale where you first place a **75 kg standard block** on it (this is the *slip gauge reference*), then stand on it — the scale only shows the **extra amount (3 kg)** with a giant amplified arrow. That extra reading, multiplied and clearly visible, is what a **Comparator** does for engineering dimensions.

In simpler terms:

- **Direct instrument** → Reads the full length. Example: Vernier caliper.
- **Comparator** → Reads only the *difference* (deviation) from a known standard, but with a **huge magnification** so that microscopic differences become visible to the naked eye.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Comparators?**
> - Reading accuracy of a human eye is limited to roughly **0.1 mm** on a plain scale.
> - Comparators magnify tiny mechanical displacements (microns) to readable millimetre pointer movements.
> - They provide **faster, repeatable, and less operator-dependent** readings.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (Universal)

| Metric | Standard Industrial Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Magnification** | **300× to 10,000×** |
| **Least Count (Dial Type)** | **0.01 mm** or **0.001 mm** |
| **Least Count (Optical)** | **0.0001 mm (0.1 µm)** |
| **Working Range (plunger travel)** | **±0.04 mm to ±1.0 mm** |
| **Total Range** | **0.04 mm to 25 mm** depending on design |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Underlying Logic

Every comparator follows the same fundamental chain:

1. A **stylus or plunger** touches the workpiece.
2. Any deviation in dimension from the standard (slip gauge setting) causes a **small linear displacement** of the plunger.
3. This small displacement is **amplified** through an intermediate mechanism (mechanical lever / optical lever / air pressure change / electrical signal).
4. The amplified output drives an **indicator (pointer on dial / projected light / LVDT reading)**.
5. The operator reads the deviation and infers the actual dimension as: *Standard + Indicated deviation*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Measured Dimension = Set Standard Dimension ± Indicated Deviation**

### 2.2 Classification of Comparators (KTU 2024 Module Focus)

Comparators are classified strictly by the **amplification medium** they use.

#### A. Mechanical Comparators
These use linkages, gears, or levers to multiply the plunger movement.

1. **Dial Indicator (Plunger Type / Johansson Dial Type)**
   - Uses a rack and pinion mechanism to convert linear plunger motion into rotary pointer motion.
   - Gear train multiplies the angular rotation.
   - Magnification up to ~1000×.

2. **Lever Type Comparator (Sigma Comparator / Mechanical-Optical Hybrid)**
   - Uses a tilting mirror or a knife-edge lever system.
   - Pointer deflects via mechanical advantage of levers.
   - Magnification up to ~5000×.

3. **Johansson Comparator (Mikrokator)**
   - Uses a unique **twisted metal strip** (the "Mikrokator strip") which untwists proportionally to plunger displacement.
   - A pointer attached to the strip gives amplified rotation.
   - No gears; friction is minimal → very smooth reading.
   - Magnification up to ~6000×.

4. **Reed Comparator**
   - Uses a vibrating **reed (tuning fork principle)**.
   - Frequency of reed vibration changes with applied force.
   - Used primarily in comparators with very small ranges, high sensitivity.

#### B. Optical Comparators
- Employ an **optical lever** (light beam reflected from a tilting mirror).
- A small rotation of the mirror causes a large displacement of the reflected light spot.
- Magnification up to **10,000×** or more.
- The light beam is projected on a graduated scale.

#### C. Pneumatic Comparators
- **Principle:** Compressed air flows through a nozzle. As the workpiece dimension changes, the gap between the nozzle and workpiece changes, altering the **back pressure**.
- Two main types:
  - **Flow Type (Float Type):** A float in a tapered glass tube rises/falls with airflow.
  - **Pressure Type (Solex Gauge):** Pressure is balanced against a column of water.
- Used for **non-contact measurement** — ideal for soft materials, bores, and fine-finished surfaces.
- Magnification up to **10,000×** easily.

#### D. Electrical / Electronic Comparators
- Use **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** as the primary sensing element.
- Plunger movement changes mutual inductance → voltage change.
- Signal electronically amplified and displayed on a digital or analog readout.
- Very high accuracy and remote display capability.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Magnification ($M$)** | $M = \dfrac{\text{Displacement of Indicator (Pointer/Light)}}{\text{Displacement of Plunger}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of amplified output to actual input |
| **Sensitivity ($S$)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{\text{Least Count}}$ | $\text{mm}^{-1}$ | Inverse of smallest readable division |
| **Least Count (LC)** | $LC = \dfrac{\text{Scale Range}}{\text{Number of Divisions}}$ | mm | Smallest measurable increment |
| **Error in Indication** | $E = \text{True Value} - \text{Indicated Value}$ | mm | Sign indicates high/low |
| **Measured Dimension** | $D = L_s \pm r$ | mm | $L_s$ = slip gauge setting, $r$ = comparator reading |
| **Back Pressure (Pneumatic)** | $P_b \propto \dfrac{1}{h^n}$ | N/m² | $h$ = gap between nozzle & workpiece |
| **LVDT Output (Electrical)** | $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | Volt | $K$ = sensitivity constant, $x$ = plunger displacement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** The **back pressure in a pneumatic comparator is inversely related to the gap** between the nozzle and workpiece. Students often write the relation in the wrong direction. The exponent $n$ is empirical (typically between 1 and 2) and is set by the manufacturer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control of mass-produced parts** (pistons, rollers, shafts).
- **Batch inspection** of components where 100% checking is required.
- **Measurement of thin foils, rubber sheets, ball bearing races** where micrometers would deform the part.
- **Pneumatic comparators** are used for checking **bores and internal diameters** non-destructively.
- **Optical comparators** double as **profile projectors** for checking complex contours and gear tooth profiles.
- **LVDT-based comparators** are used in **CNC in-process gauging** and **online shop-floor inspection**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Magnification of a Simple Lever-Type Comparator

**Problem Setup:**
A mechanical-optical comparator uses a tilting mirror arrangement. The plunger pushes a lever of length $L_1$ which tilts a mirror by a small angle $\theta$. The reflected light spot moves across a scale placed at distance $D$ from the mirror.

**Given:**
- Lever arm ratio: $L_1 \div L_2$ (input vs. output side)
- Light reflected on a scale at distance $D$ from the mirror.
- Plunger displacement = $\delta_i$.

**Step 1 — Displacement Amplification through the Lever**

When the plunger moves by $\delta_i$, the lever tilts. The point of mirror attachment moves vertically by:

$$
\delta_m = \delta_i \times \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}
$$

This is the classic mechanical advantage of a lever (Class I lever).

**Step 2 — Angular Tilt of the Mirror**

If the mirror is attached at the end of the lever arm, the angle of tilt $\theta$ is related to $\delta_m$ by the arc-length approximation (for small $\theta$ in radians):

$$
\delta_m = L_3 \times \theta
$$

where $L_3$ is the distance from the pivot to the mirror.

**Step 3 — Optical Lever Magnification**

For an optical lever, when a mirror rotates by $\theta$, the reflected light spot moves on a distant scale by:

$$
\delta_s = 2 \cdot D \cdot \theta
$$

The factor of **2** arises because when a mirror rotates by $\theta$, the reflected ray rotates by $2\theta$ (law of reflection).

**Step 4 — Total Magnification**

Combining all three stages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= \dfrac{\delta_s}{\delta_i} \\[6pt]
M_{total} &= \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot \theta}{\delta_i} \\[6pt]
M_{total} &= \dfrac{2 \cdot D}{L_3} \times \dfrac{\delta_m}{\delta_i} \\[6pt]
M_{total} &= \dfrac{2 \cdot D}{L_3} \times \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** $M = \dfrac{2 D L_1}{L_3 L_2}$

The magnification is therefore directly proportional to the optical lever length $D$ and the mechanical lever ratio $L_1 / L_2$. This is why optical comparators with long projection screens achieve magnifications exceeding **10,000×**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem: Calibration of a Dial Indicator

**Statement (KTU-Style):**
A dial indicator has **100 divisions** on its dial. With each full revolution of the pointer, the plunger moves **1 mm**. Determine:

(a) Least count of the indicator.
(b) Magnification when the pointer moves from zero to a 1 mm plunger travel across 100 divisions.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Least Count**

One full revolution of the pointer corresponds to a plunger travel of $1 \text{ mm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LC &= \dfrac{\text{Plunger travel per revolution}}{\text{Number of divisions per revolution}} \\[6pt]
LC &= \dfrac{1.0 \text{ mm}}{100} \\[6pt]
LC &= 0.01 \text{ mm} = 10 \;\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Magnification**

Each division on the dial represents $0.01 \text{ mm}$ of plunger displacement.

The pointer traverses the physical scale length of the dial. Assuming the dial diameter is such that one division on the dial face spans roughly **1 mm** of pointer travel (typical standard):

$$
M = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm (pointer travel per division)}}{0.01 \text{ mm (plunger travel per division)}} = 100
$$

Therefore the magnification of the dial indicator is **100×** (per division) and **10,000×** when measured over the full range.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Comparator

| Feature | Mechanical (Dial) | Mechanical-Optical | Optical | Pneumatic | Electrical (LVDT) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Magnification** | 100 – 1000 | 1000 – 5000 | 1000 – 10,000 | 2000 – 10,000 | 1000 – 20,000 |
| **Contact with workpiece** | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No (non-contact)** | Yes |
| **Suitable for bores** | Limited | Limited | Limited | **Excellent** | With probe |
| **Reading type** | Analog dial | Analog with optics | Light spot | Float / pressure | Digital / analog |
| **Friction wear** | Moderate (gears) | Low (lever) | Very low (mirror) | None | None |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Medium-High | High |
| **Best application** | Workshop inspection | Lab inspection | Lab + profile check | Soft parts, bores | CNC, online gauging |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Universal Comparator Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **common signal flow path** in all five types of comparators. The function remains the same — amplify a small mechanical displacement into a readable output — only the *amplification medium* changes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workpiece] --> B[Plunger Stylus Contact]
    B --> C[Small Displacement Signal]
    C --> D{Amplification Medium}
    D -- Mechanical --> E1[Rack and Pinion / Lever]
    D -- Mechanical Optical --> E2[Tilting Mirror]
    D -- Optical --> E3[Optical Lever and Scale]
    D -- Pneumatic --> E4[Nozzle and Back Pressure]
    D -- Electrical --> E5[LVDT Sensor]
    E1 --> F1[Pointer on Dial]
    E2 --> F2[Pointer via Mirror]
    E3 --> F3[Light Spot on Scale]
    E4 --> F4[Float or Pressure Gauge]
    E5 --> F5[Digital or Analog Readout]
    F1 --> G[Indicated Deviation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Actual Dimension = Slip Gauge Standard plus Deviation]
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Dial Indicator Working

This is the **most frequently tested comparator in KTU viva** — the rack-and-pinion dial indicator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plunger pushed by workpiece] --> B[Linear motion of rack teeth]
    B --> C[Rack engages with pinion gear]
    C --> D[Pinion rotates by small angle theta1]
    D --> E[Drives a larger gear on same spindle]
    E --> F[Drives pointer needle]
    F --> G[Pointer deflects across graduated dial]
    G --> H[Reading displayed to operator]
    I[Slip gauges set as reference datum on anvil] --> A
```

---

### 4.3 Pneumatic Comparator — Pressure-Flow Functional Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressed Air Supply] --> B[Regulator and Filter Unit]
    B --> C[Fixed Restrictor Nozzle]
    C --> D[Measuring Nozzle near workpiece]
    D --> E{Back Pressure Builds up}
    E --> F[Floats Rises in Tapered Tube]
    E --> G[Pressure Gauge Column]
    F --> H[Operator Reads Float Position]
    G --> H
    I[Workpiece gap h changes] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the pneumatic comparator above, as the workpiece dimension **decreases**, the gap $h$ between the measuring nozzle and workpiece **decreases**, **restricting airflow**, and the **back pressure increases** — pushing the float **upward**.

---

### 4.4 Working Hierarchy Tree of Comparators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Comparators Classification] --> B[Mechanical]
    A --> C[Optical]
    A --> D[Pneumatic]
    A --> E[Electrical]
    B --> B1[Dial Indicator Rack and Pinion]
    B --> B2[Lever Type Sigma]
    B --> B3[Johansson Mikrokator Twisted Strip]
    B --> B4[Reed Comparator]
    C --> C1[Optical Projection Comparator]
    D --> D1[Flow Type Float]
    D --> D2[Pressure Type Solex]
    E --> E1[LVDT Based Comparator]
    E --> E2[Capacitive Type]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define a comparator. Why is it called an indirect type of measuring instrument?**

**Model Answer:**
A comparator is a precision measuring device that compares the linear dimension of a workpiece with a known standard (slip gauge setting) and indicates the deviation through a high-magnification indicator. It is called **indirect** because it does not measure the absolute length directly; it only measures the *difference* between the workpiece and the reference standard, and the actual dimension is then *inferred* by adding the standard to the indicated deviation.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between a flow-type and pressure-type pneumatic comparator.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flow Type (Float) | Pressure Type (Solex) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Output indication** | Height of float in a tapered tube | Liquid column height in a U-tube |
| **Measured variable** | Rate of airflow (volume flow) | Back pressure |
| **Magnification** | Up to 10,000× | Up to 10,000× |
| **Application** | Soft materials, bores | Larger dimensions, harder bores |
| **Response** | Faster (dynamic) | Slightly slower (static head) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Internal Choice Questions

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **optical comparator**. Derive the expression for its magnification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dial indicator has 200 divisions and its plunger moves **2 mm per revolution**. Find (i) the least count, (ii) the magnification if one pointer division corresponds to **1 mm** of dial sweep, and (iii) the magnification over the full range. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Working of an Optical Comparator**

The optical comparator is based on the **optical lever principle**. A small displacement of the plunger is converted to a small tilt of a mirror. The mirror reflects a light beam onto a graduated screen. A tiny mirror rotation produces a large spot displacement because the reflected ray angle changes by twice the mirror tilt.

**Key Components:**

1. **Plunger** — contacts the workpiece.
2. **Lever linkage** — multiplies the displacement and tilts the mirror.
3. **Mirror** — mounted on a knife-edge pivot.
4. **Light source** — focused onto the mirror.
5. **Projection screen** — graduated, at distance $D$ from the mirror.
6. **Slip gauge stack** — sets the reference datum on the anvil.

**Working Steps:**

1. The slip gauge stack is mounted on the anvil and acts as the reference.
2. The plunger is placed on top of the slip gauges — the pointer is set to zero on the screen.
3. The workpiece is placed on the anvil; its dimension differs from the slip gauge by $\Delta x$.
4. The plunger moves up or down by $\Delta x$, tilting the mirror by angle $\theta$.
5. The reflected light spot moves across the screen by a distance $2D\theta$.
6. The operator reads the deviation and computes the actual size.

**Derivation of Magnification:**

Let the plunger move by an amount $\delta_i$. The mirror tilts by an angle $\theta$. The light spot moves by $2D\theta$ on the screen.

For small angles:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{\delta_i}{L_3}
$$

where $L_3$ is the mirror-to-pivot distance.

Spot displacement:

$$
\delta_s = 2D \cdot \theta = \dfrac{2D \cdot \delta_i}{L_3}
$$

Total magnification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \dfrac{\delta_s}{\delta_i} \\[6pt]
M &= \dfrac{2D}{L_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a typical optical comparator with $D = 1 \text{ m}$ and $L_3 = 0.2 \text{ mm}$:

$$
M = \dfrac{2 \times 1000 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = 10{,}000
$$

This is the source of the optical comparator's **massive magnification (10,000× or more)**.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Number of divisions on dial, $N = 200$
- Plunger travel per revolution, $t = 2 \text{ mm}$
- One pointer division on the dial face spans, $s = 1 \text{ mm}$

**(i) Least Count**

$$
LC = \dfrac{t}{N} = \dfrac{2 \text{ mm}}{200} = 0.01 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Magnification (per division)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{div} &= \dfrac{s}{LC} \\[6pt]
M_{div} &= \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{0.01 \text{ mm}} = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Magnification (over full range)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{full} &= \dfrac{\text{Total pointer sweep}}{\text{Total plunger travel}} \\[6pt]
M_{full} &= \dfrac{N \times s}{t} = \dfrac{200 \times 1 \text{ mm}}{2 \text{ mm}} \\[6pt]
M_{full} &= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (7-mark question):**
> 1. Many students write the magnification as **N × LC** — this is **wrong**. The magnification is *pointer displacement divided by plunger displacement*, NOT the number of divisions.
> 2. In optical comparator derivations, students often forget the **factor of 2** in $2D\theta$ — this is the most common loss of 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. For pneumatic comparators, students write $P_b \propto h$ — the correct relation is **inverse** ($P_b$ increases as $h$ decreases).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Johansson comparator (Mikrokator)** with a neat diagram. Mention its advantages over a gear-based dial indicator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pneumatic comparator (flow type) is used to check a bore. The reading on the float is **40 mm** when a 25.00 mm slip gauge is placed, and **35 mm** when the bore is placed. The float calibration is **0.001 mm per mm of float rise**. Determine the actual bore size. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Johansson Comparator (Mikrokator) — Working**

The Johansson comparator uses a unique **Mikrokator strip** — a thin metal strip twisted along its length. The strip is fixed at one end and the other end is attached to a plunger.

**Construction:**

1. A stretched and twisted phosphor-bronze or steel strip (the "Mikrokator strip").
2. The strip is anchored at the top to the body of the instrument.
3. The lower end of the strip carries the **plunger tip** that touches the workpiece.
4. A **pointer** is rigidly attached to the strip near its lower end.
5. The strip lies inside a sealed metal case with a graduated dial.

**Working Principle:**

When the plunger moves upward (workpiece dimension is *less* than the slip gauge setting), the strip experiences a slight change in its tension. Because the strip is *pre-twisted*, even a tiny axial force causes it to **untwist by a measurable amount**. The pointer attached to the strip rotates through a large angle for a small plunger displacement, producing high magnification **without any gears**.

**Advantages over Gear-Based Dial Indicator:**

1. **No gears** → no backlash, no wear, no friction in tooth contact.
2. **Higher magnification** — typically 4000× to 6000×.
3. **Smoother and more repeatable** readings.
4. **Longer life** due to absence of moving gear surfaces.
5. **Better sensitivity** for very small dimensional changes.
6. **Lower hysteresis error** compared with rack-and-pinion types.

---

**(b) Bore Measurement with Pneumatic Comparator**

**Given:**
- Slip gauge setting, $L_s = 25.00 \text{ mm}$
- Float reading with slip gauge, $h_1 = 40 \text{ mm}$
- Float reading with bore, $h_2 = 35 \text{ mm}$
- Calibration, $k = 0.001 \text{ mm per mm of float rise}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Float Difference**

$$
\Delta h = h_1 - h_2 = 40 - 35 = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

The float dropped when the bore replaced the slip gauge, meaning the bore is **larger** than the slip gauge (more air gap → less back pressure → float falls).

**Step 2 — Calculate the Dimensional Difference**

$$
\Delta D = \Delta h \times k = 5 \times 0.001 = 0.005 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Actual Bore Size**

Since the bore is larger than the slip gauge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{bore} &= L_s + \Delta D \\[6pt]
D_{bore} &= 25.00 + 0.005 \\[6pt]
D_{bore} &= 25.005 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Marks-Loss Triggers (14-mark question):**
> 1. Confusing the **sign of the deviation** — students often write $L_s - \Delta D$ even when the bore is larger, losing 2 marks.
> 2. In Johansson comparator explanations, students forget to mention the **pre-twist** of the Mikrokator strip. Without this, the answer is incomplete.
> 3. For pneumatic comparators, students forget that the calibration constant is given **per mm of float travel** and not per division.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **comparator** measures *deviation* from a *standard*, not absolute length.
- [ ] The measured dimension is computed as: $D = L_s \pm r$ (slip gauge ± reading).
- [ ] **Five major types** of comparators: Mechanical, Mechanical-Optical, Optical, Pneumatic, Electrical.
- [ ] **Dial indicator** uses rack-and-pinion; magnification up to 1000×.
- [ ] **Johansson (Mikrokator)** uses a *pre-twisted strip* — no gears — up to 6000×.
- [ ] **Optical comparator** uses a *mirror* and a *long projection screen* — up to 10,000×.
- [ ] **Pneumatic comparator** is *non-contact* — uses back-pressure variation.
- [ ] **Electrical comparator** uses an **LVDT** for high-accuracy digital readout.
- [ ] **Magnification formula:** $M = \dfrac{\text{Output displacement}}{\text{Input displacement}}$.
- [ ] **Optical lever formula:** $\delta_s = 2D\theta$ — the factor of 2 is non-negotiable.
- [ ] **Back pressure relation:** $P_b$ is **inversely** proportional to the air gap $h$.
- [ ] **Least Count:** $LC = \dfrac{\text{Plunger travel per revolution}}{\text{Number of divisions}}$.
- [ ] Optical and pneumatic comparators are preferred for **soft, delicate, or non-rigid** parts.
- [ ] Always **calibrate** the comparator against a slip gauge stack before measurement.
- [ ] Common KTU viva question: "Why is the magnification of an optical comparator always even-numbered (e.g., 2000, 5000, 10,000)?" — because the optical lever introduces a factor of 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angular Measurement Using Sine Bar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL408 - MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Angular Measurement Using Sine Bar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sine Bar** is a precision metrological instrument used for the **accurate measurement of angles, setting of workpieces at a required angle, and checking the angular accuracy of machined components** such as dies, templates, and angular gauges. It operates on the **principle of trigonometry** — specifically, that the sine of an angle is equal to the ratio of the height of one cylinder (roller) above a reference surface to the precise center-to-center distance between the two cylinders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sine Bar — Official KTU Definition:**
> A sine bar is a hardened, ground, high-grade steel bar with two cylindrical rollers of equal diameter mounted at its ends, whose center distance is held to a very close tolerance (commonly **100 mm, 200 mm, or 300 mm**), used in conjunction with slip gauges and a surface plate to generate or measure angles to an accuracy of a few minutes of arc.

### 1.2 Component Anatomy

| Component | Material / Specification | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Body** | Hardened, ground steel | Rigid base; holds rollers at precise center distance |
| **Rollers (×2)** | Hardened steel cylinders, **equal diameter** | Provide two contact points; center distance is the hypotenuse |
| **Clamping holes** | Threaded/clearance holes | Hold the workpiece securely on the bar |
| **End edges** | Ground flat and parallel to roller axis | Reference edges for measurement of workpieces with shoulders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The accuracy of a sine bar depends on:
> (1) the **parallelism of rollers to the base**, (2) the **center-distance accuracy** between the two rollers, and (3) the **equal diameter of the rollers**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine sliding a **book under one leg of a wobbly table** to stop it from rocking. The higher you stack books, the more the table tilts. If you knew the length of the table leg (between floor contact points) and the height of the books, you could *exactly calculate* the tilt angle. The **sine bar works on this exact same logic** — slip gauges (the "books") of a calculated height lift one roller, tilting the bar to a precise, mathematically known angle.

> [!TIP]
> **Why two cylinders and not a flat rest?** Two cylinders form a **single line of contact** (a knife-edge equivalent), so the bar can rock freely on a flat surface plate. This makes the tilt determinate — only one angle is possible for a given slip-gauge height.

### 1.4 Standard Specifications (KTU Reference)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Center distances (L)** | 100 mm, 200 mm, 300 mm |
| **Roller diameter** | ~20 mm (equal for both) |
| **Body length** | Greater than L by roller diameter |
| **Accuracy class** | Grade 0 (±5 µm over L) and Grade 1 (±10 µm over L) per IS 2984 |
| **Workable angle range** | **0° to 45°** (practical best: **0° to 30°**) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The sine bar exploits the geometry of a **right-angled triangle** formed when one roller is raised by slip gauges of height *h* on a surface plate, while the other roller rests directly on the plate.

**Step 1:** The sine bar is placed on a **surface plate** (precision flat reference).

**Step 2:** A stack of **slip gauges of height *h*** is inserted under one roller; the other roller sits directly on the plate.

**Step 3:** The bar tilts by an angle **θ** with respect to the surface plate.

**Step 4:** From the resulting right triangle:
- **Hypotenuse** = center distance between rollers, *L*
- **Opposite side** (vertical) = slip-gauge stack height, *h*
- **Angle at the lower roller** = **θ**

**Step 5:** Trigonometrically,
$$\sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{h}{L}$$

**Step 6:** Solving for the unknowns,
$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad h = L \cdot \sin\theta$$

## 2.2 The Master Equation — Derivation of Sensitivity & Error

The sensitivity of measured angle θ to slip-gauge height h is found by differentiating:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dh} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L^2 - h^2}} = \frac{1}{L\cos\theta}$$

This shows that **sensitivity is maximum when θ is large** (i.e., for steep angles, a small change in h produces a large change in θ). Conversely, near 0° the instrument is least sensitive.

### Error Propagation

If the slip-gauge stack has a tolerance of $\pm\Delta h$, the resulting angular error is:
$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta h}{L\cos\theta} \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

Converting to **seconds of arc** (multiply by $\frac{180}{\pi} \times 3600$):
$$\Delta\theta'' = \frac{\Delta h}{L\cos\theta} \times 206265 \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | Fundamental sine-bar relation | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $h = L \sin\theta$ | Slip-gauge height for required angle θ | mm |
| 3 | $\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right)$ | Unknown angle from measured h | degrees / radians |
| 4 | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L\cos\theta}$ | Angular error from slip-gauge tolerance | radians |
| 5 | $\Delta\theta'' = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L\cos\theta} \times 206265$ | Angular error in **seconds of arc** | seconds |
| 6 | $h_{\text{total}} = h_1 + h_2$ | For compound angles (stacking two sine bars) | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Pipe Symbol in Tables:** All equation columns above use the inline fraction `dfrac` inside `$...$` blocks. **Never** write `\vert x \vert` style absolute value directly inside a markdown-table cell — it breaks the column delimiter. Use `\mid` or `\vert` inside LaTeX.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tool room:** Checking the angle of milling cutters, reamer teeth, and drills.
- **Inspection of jigs and fixtures:** Verifying the angular accuracy of locating pins and rests.
- **Die and mould making:** Setting up surfaces at exact angles for spark-erosion or grinding.
- **Surface-plate–based layout:** Generating compound angles on a 2-D surface plate using two sine bars.
- **Quality control of gauges and templates:** Certifying angle gauges against a traceable reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a sine bar is preferred over a bevel protractor for precision work:** A vernier bevel protractor offers a resolution of only **5 minutes (5′)** of arc, whereas a sine bar combined with Grade-0 slip gauges and a surface plate can achieve accuracies of **better than 1 minute (1′)** — sometimes down to a few seconds.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Geometrical Setup — Defining the Reference Triangle

Consider a sine bar of center distance **L** placed on a surface plate. The left roller (Roller A) is elevated by a slip-gauge stack of height **h**, and the right roller (Roller B) rests on the plate.

**Right-triangle construction:**
- Hypotenuse $AB = L$ (line joining the two roller centers)
- Vertical leg $AC = h$ (slip-gauge height)
- Horizontal leg $BC = \sqrt{L^2 - h^2}$
- Angle at $B$ (the angle between the sine bar and the surface plate) = **θ**

By definition of sine in a right-angled triangle:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{AC}{AB} = \frac{h}{L}$$

Hence the **fundamental sine-bar equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad h = L \sin\theta\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Setting a Given Angle (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Problem:** A **200 mm center-distance** sine bar is to be set to an angle of **23°45′**. Calculate the height of the slip-gauge stack required. Also compute the height to the **nearest available slip-gauge combination** from a standard set.

**Step 1 — Convert angle to decimal degrees.**
$$\theta = 23° + \frac{45}{60}° = 23.75°$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sine-bar formula.**
$$h = L \sin\theta = 200 \times \sin(23.75°)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\sin(23.75°)$.**
$$\sin(23.75°) = 0.40276 \quad (\text{from standard trigonometric tables})$$

**Step 4 — Compute the height.**
$$h = 200 \times 0.40276 = 80.552 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Build the slip-gauge combination** (using 1, 1.2, 1.5, 5, 20, 50 series):

| Slip gauge (mm) | Quantity | Subtotal (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 50.000 | 1 | 50.000 |
| 20.000 | 1 | 20.000 |
| 9.000 | 1 | 9.000 |
| 1.500 | 1 | 1.500 |
| 0.050 | 1 | 0.050 |
| 0.002 | 1 | 0.002 |
| **Total** | | **80.552** ✓ |

**Verification of angular error** (using a slip-gauge tolerance of ±0.0005 mm per block, ~6 blocks):
$$\Delta h \approx \pm 0.003 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta\theta'' = \frac{0.003}{200 \times \cos(23.75°)} \times 206265 \approx 3.4''$$

This corresponds to a measurement accuracy of about **3 seconds of arc** — far better than a vernier bevel protractor.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Measuring an Unknown Angle

> **Problem:** A workpiece is placed on a 200 mm sine bar. The slip-gauge stack required to align the top of the workpiece parallel to the surface plate measures **67.20 mm**. Determine the angle of the workpiece.

**Step 1 — Write the relation.**
$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{67.20}{200} = 0.3360$$

**Step 2 — Take the inverse sine.**
$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.3360) = 19.629°$$

**Step 3 — Convert to degrees–minutes–seconds (DMS) for the KTU answer.**
$$0.629° \times 60 = 37.74' \rightarrow 37' \text{ and } 0.74 \times 60 = 44.4''$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta \approx 19° \, 37' \, 44''\;}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error in Sine-Bar Measurement

| # | Error Source | Nature | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slip-gauge stack error (wringing, additive tolerances) | Systematic | Use calibrated Grade-0 gauges; minimise number of blocks |
| 2 | Roller diameter inequality | Systematic | Periodic verification on a roller-measuring fixture |
| 3 | Center-distance error (off-nominal L) | Systematic | Use a sine bar whose L has been calibrated |
| 4 | Surface-plate flatness error | Random / systematic | Use a calibrated surface plate; clean thoroughly |
| 5 | Roller axis not parallel to workpiece datum | Geometric | Align workpiece datum against a precision straight-edge |
| 6 | Cosine error when θ approaches 45° | Geometric (intrinsic) | Use compound sine bar or **switch ends** (cos θ = sin(90°−θ)) |
| 7 | Thermal expansion | Environmental | Measure at **20 ± 1 °C** (standard metrology temperature) |

### 3.4.1 The "Cosine Error" — A Deeper Look

As **θ → 45°**, $\cos\theta \to 0.707$, but more critically, a small change in **h** produces a *very* large change in **θ** (since $d\theta/dh = 1/(L\cos\theta)$). In practice, errors in slip-gauge combinations become magnified. The classical workaround is:

- If the required angle lies in the range **45° to 90°**, set the sine bar to the **complementary angle** (90° − θ) and **invert the workpiece**, OR
- Use a **compound sine bar** (two sine bars stacked orthogonally) for 3-D angular work.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Optimal Roller Diameter

A common KTU sub-question: *Why are the rollers cylindrical, and what diameter is ideal?*

For minimum contact deformation (Hertzian contact) and maximum stiffness, the rollers should be:
- **Hard** (≥ 60 HRC)
- **Ground to a fine surface finish** (Ra ≤ 0.2 µm)
- Of **diameter large enough** to avoid brinelling on the surface plate

The rule of thumb: roller diameter ≥ 5 × the slip-gauge stack height, to keep the contact patch well within the cylindrical portion of the roller.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sine-Bar Measurement

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional architecture** of a sine-bar measurement — from setup to angle determination — and the **information flow** between physical components.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Operator / Inspector] --> B[Workpiece with Unknown Angle]
    B --> C[Mounted on Sine Bar Body]
    D[Slip Gauge Stack of Calculated Height h] --> E[Roller A - Elevated End]
    F[Surface Plate - Precision Flat] --> G[Roller B - Reference End]
    C --> H[Dial Indicator / DTI]
    H --> I{Surface Parallel to Plate?}
    I -- No --> J[Adjust h in Small Steps]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Read h and L]
    K --> L[Compute theta = sin inverse h over L]
    L --> M[Report Angle in Degrees Minutes Seconds]
```

### 4.2 Physical Setup — Side-View Schematic (ASCII Representation)

```
                    WORKPIECE
              ┌────────────────────┐
              │                    │  ← Top surface (must be parallel to plate)
              └────────────────────┘
   ◯━━━━━━━━━ SINE BAR BODY ━━━━━━━━━━◯
  (R1)                                (R2)
   │                                    │
   │ h (slip gauges)                    │ 0 (roller on plate)
   ▼                                    ▼
═══════════════════════════════════════════════  ← Surface Plate
```

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **R1** | Elevated roller (left end) |
| **R2** | Reference roller (right end, on surface plate) |
| **h** | Height of slip-gauge stack under R1 |
| **L** | Center-to-center distance between R1 and R2 |

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Clean surface plate and sine bar] --> P2[Step 2: Identify angle to be set or measured]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute h equals L sin theta]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Build slip gauge stack]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Place sine bar - one roller on stack, other on plate]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Mount workpiece on sine bar]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Sweep DTI across top surface]
    P7 --> P8{Reading constant?}
    P8 -- No --> P9[Re adjust h and re sweep]
    P9 --> P7
    P8 -- Yes --> P10[Angle equals set value of theta]
```

### 4.4 Identification of Key Nodes

| Node ID | Component | Function in the System |
|---|---|---|
| `nodeSP` | Surface Plate | Flat datum of reference |
| `nodeSB` | Sine Bar | Tilts to angle θ via slip-gauge stack |
| `nodeSG` | Slip Gauges | Provide precisely known height h |
| `nodeWP` | Workpiece | Component whose angle is being measured |
| `nodeDTI` | Dial Test Indicator | Detects parallelism of top surface |
| `nodeCalc` | Calculator / Inspector | Computes h = L sin θ |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSP[Surface Plate] --- nodeSB[Sine Bar]
    nodeSG[Slip Gauge Stack] -.-> nodeSB
    nodeSB --> nodeWP[Workpiece]
    nodeWP --> nodeDTI[Dial Test Indicator]
    nodeDTI --> nodeCalc[Compute theta]
```
