# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**Define a sine bar. State the principle on which it works.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 m):** A sine bar is a precision instrument consisting of a hardened steel body with two equal-diameter cylindrical rollers mounted at its ends, whose **center distance (L)** is held to a close tolerance (100, 200 or 300 mm).
- **Principle (1.5 m):** It works on the **sine principle of trigonometry**, where the sine of the angle generated equals the ratio of slip-gauge height (h) to the roller center distance (L): $\sin\theta = h/L$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]
**Why is a sine bar preferred over a vernier bevel protractor for precision angle measurement? Mention its working range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Accuracy (1.5 m):** A sine bar with Grade-0 slip gauges achieves an accuracy of **a few seconds to 1 minute of arc**, compared to the vernier bevel protractor's resolution of only **5 minutes of arc**.
- **Range (1 m):** It works effectively in the range **0° to 45°** (best results between 0° and 30°).
- **Additional advantage (0.5 m):** It uses slip gauges — a direct traceable length standard — making it suitable for calibration laboratories.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two complete alternative questions are given below for internal choice.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)  →  **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, End Semester]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle of a sine bar**. Derive the relation $\sin\theta = h/L$ for angle generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **200 mm** sine bar is to be used to set an angle of **18°30′**. Calculate:
   (i) The slip-gauge stack height required.
   (ii) The slip-gauge combination to build this height.
   (iii) The angular error (in seconds) if the slip-gauge stack has a cumulative error of **±0.002 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Construction, Working & Derivation (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):** A sine bar consists of:
1. A **rigid steel body**, hardened and precision-ground.
2. **Two cylindrical rollers** of equal diameter (typically ~20 mm) fitted at the two ends, with their **axes parallel** to each other and perpendicular to the bar's length.
3. The **center-to-center distance L** between the rollers is held to a very high tolerance (e.g., ±0.005 mm for Grade 0).
4. **Clamping slots/holes** on the top of the body to hold the workpiece.
5. The body has **flat parallel reference edges** for aligning the workpiece.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- The sine bar rests on a **surface plate**.
- A stack of **slip gauges of height h** is placed under one roller.
- The other roller sits directly on the plate, causing the bar to tilt at angle θ.
- A workpiece mounted on the bar can then be set to, or measured at, the same angle θ by checking the parallelism of its top surface to the surface plate using a **dial test indicator (DTI)**.

**Derivation (2 marks):** From the right triangle formed by the roller centers and the slip-gauge height:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad h = L\sin\theta$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Slip-gauge height (2 marks):**
$$\theta = 18°30' = 18.5°$$
$$h = L\sin\theta = 200 \times \sin(18.5°) = 200 \times 0.31730 = \mathbf{63.46 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Slip-gauge combination (2 marks):**

| Slip gauge (mm) | Quantity | Subtotal (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 50.000 | 1 | 50.000 |
| 10.000 | 1 | 10.000 |
| 3.000 | 1 | 3.000 |
| 0.400 | 1 | 0.400 |
| 0.060 | 1 | 0.060 |
| **Total** | | **63.460** ✓ |

**(iii) Angular error (3 marks):**
$$\Delta\theta'' = \frac{\Delta h}{L\cos\theta} \times 206265$$
$$\Delta\theta'' = \frac{0.002}{200 \times \cos(18.5°)} \times 206265 = \frac{0.002}{200 \times 0.94832} \times 206265$$
$$\Delta\theta'' = \frac{0.002}{189.664} \times 206265 = 2.17''$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\theta \approx \pm 2.2 \text{ seconds of arc}\;}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)  →  **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **procedure to measure an unknown angle** of a workpiece using a sine bar. State the formula used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a sine-bar measurement, the slip-gauge stack required to make a workpiece's top surface parallel to the surface plate was measured to be **94.32 mm**, using a **300 mm center-distance** sine bar.
   (i) Determine the angle of the workpiece in **DMS (degrees–minutes–seconds)**.
   (ii) State **two precautions** to be observed for accurate measurement.
   (iii) What is the maximum recommended angle for a sine bar, and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks)**

**Setup (2 marks):**
1. Clean the surface plate, sine bar rollers, and the workpiece thoroughly.
2. Place the sine bar on the surface plate.

**Initial trial (2 marks):**
3. Mount the workpiece on the sine bar.
4. Use a **dial test indicator (DTI)** to sweep across the top surface of the workpiece.
5. If the DTI reading varies, the workpiece is not parallel — slip gauges of trial height h are placed under one roller.

**Final adjustment (2 marks):**
6. Calculate the height: $h = L \sin\theta$ for the **assumed** angle θ.
7. Keep adjusting slip-gauge height in **fine increments** (wrung-on thin gauges) until the DTI reads a **constant value** across the entire top surface.

**Final measurement (1 mark):**
8. Once the DTI reading is constant (parallelism achieved), the height h of the slip-gauge stack is recorded. The unknown angle is:
$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Angle in DMS (3 marks):**
$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{94.32}{300} = 0.3144$$
$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.3144) = 18.328°$$
Converting the decimal:
$$0.328° \times 60 = 19.68' = 19' \text{ and } 0.68 \times 60 = 40.8''$$
$$\boxed{\;\theta \approx 18° \, 19' \, 41''\;}$$

**(ii) Two precautions (2 marks):**
1. The **surface plate must be clean and flat** (calibrated); any dirt or burr will introduce error.
2. The **slip gauges must be properly wrung** to avoid stack errors; use the **minimum number of blocks**.
3. (Bonus) The measurement should be done at **20 ± 1 °C** to avoid thermal errors.
4. (Bonus) The **roller diameters must be verified** for equality.

**(iii) Maximum recommended angle (2 marks):**
- The maximum practical angle is **45°**, but the recommended safe upper limit is **30°**.
- **Reason:** As θ increases, the **cosine error** amplifies. The angular sensitivity $d\theta/dh = 1/(L\cos\theta)$ becomes very large for high θ, so a tiny slip-gauge error produces a significant angular error. The sine bar is most accurate at **low angles** (near 0°) and progressively less so above 30°.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert DMS to decimal degrees** before computing the sine. Always do this: e.g., 23°45′ → 23 + 45/60 = 23.75°.
> 2. **Mixing up which leg is the hypotenuse.** The hypotenuse is the line joining the **roller centers (L)**, NOT the surface of the bar.
> 3. **Skipping slip-gauge combination.** Examiners expect a complete table showing the standard gauge blocks used; just writing "80.55 mm" loses **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Units in the final angle.** Always give the final answer in **DMS** (degrees, minutes, seconds) for KTU. Decimal answers alone are considered incomplete.
> 5. **Neglecting the cosine error** in error-propagation parts. Always show the $\cos\theta$ term in the denominator.
> 6. **Drawing the sketch incorrectly** — the roller must be drawn as a *circle in side view* and the slip-gauge stack as a *rectangular block* under it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-yield for KTU ESE):**

- ☐ **Master formula:** $\sin\theta = h/L$ — write it on the first page of your answer sheet.
- ☐ **Practical range:** **0° to 45°**, best results in **0° to 30°**.
- ☐ **Standard L values:** **100 mm, 200 mm, 300 mm** — memorize these.
- ☐ **Always convert DMS to decimal** before computing the sine.
- ☐ **Use Grade-0 slip gauges** for the highest accuracy (~1 minute or better).
- ☐ **Equation for error in seconds of arc:** $\Delta\theta'' = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L\cos\theta} \times 206265$.
- ☐ **Two cylindrical rollers** of **equal diameter** — essential; otherwise the geometry breaks.
- ☐ **Surface plate** is the reference datum; it must be **clean, flat, and calibrated**.
- ☐ **Dial Test Indicator (DTI)** is used to confirm parallelism — never the naked eye.
- ☐ **Compound sine bar** is used for angles > 45° or for 3-D angle measurement.
- ☐ **Always give the slip-gauge combination** as a table, not just the numerical height.
- ☐ **Final answer in DMS** (degrees-minutes-seconds) is the KTU convention.
- ☐ **Temperature:** Standard metrology temperature is **20 °C ± 1 °C**.
- ☐ **Comparison with bevel protractor:** Sine bar is **~5× more accurate** (1′ vs 5′).
- ☐ **Sine bar cannot measure internal angles directly** — it measures the angle of a surface relative to a reference plane.
- ☐ **Workpiece must be clamped** securely — the bar's clamping slots are not optional.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SHaL" (Sine, Height, Length):**
> **S**in θ = **H**eight ÷ **L**ength — three letters, one equation, full marks!
